rows.headline: France's CNIL and Groupe VYV Find Nine in Ten Young French People Use Conversational AI
rows.title: France's CNIL and Groupe VYV Find Nine in Ten Young French People Use Conversational AI
rows.summary: On 5 May 2026, France's CNIL and Groupe VYV published findings from a four-country EU survey on young people's use of conversational AI. In France, approximately nine in ten young people reported using conversational AI tools. Around 48 percent said they discuss personal topics through these tools, including mental health. The survey covered four EU countries. CNIL conducted the research in partnership with Groupe VYV. The findings identified conversational AI as an informal pathway young people use to address mental health topics.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns Organisations to Prepare for Incoming Vulnerability Patch Wave
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns Organisations to Prepare for Incoming Vulnerability Patch Wave
rows.summary: On 1 May 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published guidance urging organisations to ready themselves for a significant wave of security patches. The NCSC said the patches will address vulnerabilities rooted in decades of accumulated technical debt. The centre framed the update as a warning about the scale and speed of remediation work organisations should expect. The NCSC published the guidance as a blog post on its official website.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation Framework for AI Misuse in Fraud and Cybercrime
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation Framework for AI Misuse in Fraud and Cybercrime
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published an evaluation framework addressing AI misuse in fraud and cybercrime. The framework sets out a structured approach for assessing how AI systems can be exploited to facilitate fraudulent and cybercriminal activity. AISI released the framework as a blog post on its official website. The publication reflects the institute's ongoing work to identify and measure risks posed by AI capabilities in the context of criminal misuse. No financial penalties or regulatory actions were announced alongside the release.

rows.headline: UK AISI Funds 60 Projects to Advance AI Alignment Research
rows.title: UK AISI Funds 60 Projects to Advance AI Alignment Research
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute announced funding for 60 projects aimed at advancing AI alignment research. The initiative targets research into ensuring AI systems behave in accordance with intended goals and human values. AISI published details of the funded projects on its official blog, outlining the scope of work supported under the programme. The announcement reflects the institute's continued focus on AI safety research as a core part of its mandate.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of OpenAI's GPT-5.5
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published its evaluation of the cyber capabilities of OpenAI's GPT-5.5 model. The assessment formed part of AISI's ongoing programme of frontier AI safety testing. AISI conducts pre-deployment evaluations in coordination with AI developers to assess potential risks across defined capability domains. The publication covers findings specific to GPT-5.5's performance in cyber-relevant tasks. No further details from the source were available at time of processing.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Benchmark for Measuring AI Agent Container Breakout Capabilities
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Benchmark for Measuring AI Agent Container Breakout Capabilities
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post introducing a benchmark designed to measure whether AI agents can escape their sandboxes. The benchmark focuses on container breakout capabilities and aims to provide a safe method for evaluating this risk. AISI developed the tool to assess how effectively AI agents can breach the isolated environments used to contain them. The publication addresses growing concerns about AI systems operating beyond their intended boundaries.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Blog on the First AI Safety Summit
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Blog on the First AI Safety Summit
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post reviewing the first AI Safety Summit. The post appears on the AISI website and addresses the inaugural summit held in 2023. The blog is attributed to AISI and is categorised as an official publication on the institute's domain. No further details about the blog's specific findings, conclusions, or named authors are available in the source record provided.

rows.headline: UK AISI Launches Alignment Project with GBP 15 Million Fund for AI Alignment Research
rows.title: UK AISI Launches Alignment Project with GBP 15 Million Fund for AI Alignment Research
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute announced the Alignment Project, a global fund of over GBP 15 million dedicated to AI alignment research. The fund targets research into ensuring AI systems behave in accordance with human intentions and values. AISI described the initiative as a global effort, indicating participation or funding opportunities beyond the United Kingdom. The announcement was published as a blog post on the AISI website. No further details on grant structures, recipient organisations, or application processes were included in the source material.

rows.headline: Spain's AEPD Launches Interactive Tool for Public Breach Notification Consultation
rows.title: Spain's AEPD Launches Interactive Tool for Public Breach Notification Consultation
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, Spain's Agencia Española de Protección de Datos published a new interactive consultation tool giving the public filterable, visual access to personal data breach notifications received under Article 33 GDPR. The tool complements existing monthly PDF statistical reports already produced by the agency. AEPD stated the launch forms part of its 2025–2030 Strategic Plan, which covers open government and digital transformation objectives. Breach notifications submitted to AEPD under the GDPR mandatory reporting regime are now accessible through the new interface alongside the agency's established reporting outputs.
rows.authority: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Cyber-Capability Evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published its evaluation of Claude Mythos Preview's cyber capabilities. The assessment examined the model's performance across cyber-relevant tasks and was conducted by AISI, the UK government body responsible for evaluating frontier AI systems for safety risks. The evaluation focused specifically on the cyber domain, assessing what the model can and cannot do in that context. AISI published the findings on its official blog, continuing its programme of pre-deployment and post-deployment model evaluations.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation of AI Models' Willingness to Sabotage Safety Research
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Evaluation of AI Models' Willingness to Sabotage Safety Research
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post examining whether AI models would sabotage AI safety research. The evaluation assessed model behaviour in scenarios where undermining safety research was a possible action. AISI conducted the work as part of its broader programme to identify risks posed by advanced AI systems. The post presents findings on how current models responded when given opportunities to interfere with safety-relevant tasks. No fine or enforcement action was associated with the publication.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Five Key Findings from First Frontier AI Trends Report
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Five Key Findings from First Frontier AI Trends Report
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published its first Frontier AI Trends Report, setting out five key findings on the current state of frontier AI development. The report examines trends across leading AI systems, covering capability trajectories, safety-relevant behaviours, and emerging risks identified through AISI's evaluation programme. The institute positioned the report as a baseline for tracking how frontier models evolve over time. AISI stated the findings are intended to inform policymakers, researchers, and developers working on the safety and governance of advanced AI systems.

rows.headline: UK AISI Publishes Its Approach to Tackling AI-Generated Child Sexual Abuse Material
rows.title: UK AISI Publishes Its Approach to Tackling AI-Generated Child Sexual Abuse Material
rows.summary: On 30 April 2026, the UK AI Security Institute published a blog post setting out its approach to tackling AI-generated child sexual abuse material. The post describes how AISI is working to address the threat posed by generative AI tools capable of producing such material. AISI frames the issue as a safety priority requiring technical and policy action. The blog represents the institute's public position on one of the most serious harms associated with AI-generated content.

rows.headline: China's TC260 Sets Basic Security Requirements for Generative AI Services
rows.title: China's TC260 Sets Basic Security Requirements for Generative AI Services
rows.summary: On 11 February 2024, China's National Information Security Standardisation Technical Committee (TC260) published guideline TC260-003, establishing baseline security requirements for generative AI services offered to the public in China. The standard covers training data security, requiring screening for content that could undermine national security or involve unlawfully collected personal information. It also addresses model security, output controllability, and pre-launch security assessments. Generated content touching on politics, ethnicity, religion, or violence must be filtered. Providers must label AI-generated content, retain interaction logs, maintain user complaint mechanisms, and conduct periodic self-assessments. TC260-003 functions as a recommended national standard and serves as a technical reference for regulators enforcing the Interim Measures for Generative AI Services, which entered into force on 15 August 2023.

rows.headline: Vermont General Assembly Considers HB 821 AI Regulation Bill for Consequential Decisions
rows.title: Vermont General Assembly Considers HB 821 AI Regulation Bill for Consequential Decisions
rows.summary: Vermont's House Bill 821, introduced in the 2023-2024 legislative session, proposed requirements for developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems affecting Vermont residents. The bill required notification to individuals subject to automated decision-making and granted consumers the right to request human review of AI-driven decisions covering employment, credit, housing, and access to services. Developers would conduct algorithmic impact assessments before deployment. Deployers would maintain documentation and disclose records to the Vermont Attorney General on request. The Attorney General would enforce the bill through civil penalties, with no fixed penalty amount specified. No enacted version has been confirmed.

rows.headline: China's CAC Publishes Draft Interim Measures on Anthropomorphic AI Interaction Services
rows.title: China's CAC Publishes Draft Interim Measures on Anthropomorphic AI Interaction Services
rows.summary: On 27 December 2025, the Cyberspace Administration of China published draft interim measures governing anthropomorphic AI interaction services, opening the text for public consultation. The draft prohibits providers and users from generating content that endangers national security, undermines national honour, disrupts ethnic unity, facilitates illegal religious activities, or incites criminal conduct. It also bans rumours that disrupt economic or social order and content harming users' physical or mental health. Service providers must prominently disclose to users that they are interacting with an AI system rather than a human. The CAC did not specify a consultation deadline or finalisation timeline.

rows.headline: New Hampshire House Introduces HB 1725 to Regulate High-Risk AI Systems
rows.title: New Hampshire House Introduces HB 1725 to Regulate High-Risk AI Systems
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the New Hampshire General Court introduced HB 1725, a bill imposing obligations on developers and deployers of high-risk artificial intelligence systems. The bill defines covered entities and requires developers to provide deployers with documentation on intended uses, known limitations, and performance characteristics. Deployers must implement risk management programmes and disclose to individuals when AI assists in consequential decisions affecting employment, housing, credit, or healthcare. The bill includes civil penalties for violations enforced through existing state infrastructure. New Hampshire joins several US states advancing AI-specific legislation during the 2024-2025 session amid the absence of a comprehensive federal AI statute.

rows.headline: EU Cybersecurity Act Grants ENISA Permanent Mandate and Creates ICT Certification Framework
rows.title: EU Cybersecurity Act Grants ENISA Permanent Mandate and Creates ICT Certification Framework
rows.summary: On 27 June 2019, the European Parliament and the Council adopted Regulation (EU) 2019/881, the EU Cybersecurity Act, which entered into force the same day and became applicable on 28 June 2019. The Act replaced ENISA's previous renewable mandate under Regulation (EU) No 526/2013 with a permanent mandate. It charges ENISA with supporting Union cybersecurity policy, Member State capacity building, and operational cooperation. Title III establishes a Union-wide ICT certification framework under which the Commission may request ENISA to prepare certification schemes specifying basic, substantial, or high assurance levels. Article 57 provides that existing national schemes covering ICT products or services addressed by a new European scheme cease to produce effects on the date set in the relevant implementing act.

rows.headline: US Federal Trade Commission Act Establishes FTC and Bans Unfair or Deceptive Practices
rows.title: US Federal Trade Commission Act Establishes FTC and Bans Unfair or Deceptive Practices
rows.summary: Originally enacted on 26 September 1914, the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. §§ 41–58) created the Federal Trade Commission as an independent federal agency and prohibited unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. Section 5 is the primary operative provision, empowering the FTC to issue cease-and-desist orders. The Wheeler-Lea Act of 1938 extended coverage to consumer protection regardless of competitive harm. In AMG Capital Management v. FTC (2021), the Supreme Court held unanimously that Section 13(b) does not authorise equitable monetary relief. The Act confers no private right of action and exempts banks, common carriers, and federal credit unions from FTC jurisdiction.

rows.headline: Hawaii Legislature Introduces Senate Bill 2967 to Regulate Artificial Intelligence
rows.title: Hawaii Legislature Introduces Senate Bill 2967 to Regulate Artificial Intelligence
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Hawaii State Legislature introduced Senate Bill 2967, a measure directed at regulating artificial intelligence systems within the state. The bill establishes requirements governing the development, deployment, and use of AI technologies. As introduced, SB 2967 is understood to address obligations on AI developers and deployers, covering areas such as transparency, algorithmic accountability, impact assessments, and consumer protections related to automated decision-making. The measure was referred to committee for consideration. Its precise provisions, including scope, applicability thresholds, enforcement mechanisms, and penalties, are set out in the official bill text available through the Hawaii State Legislature's bill tracking system.

rows.headline: Oklahoma Legislature Introduces HB 1916 to Establish Consumer Data Privacy Rights
rows.title: Oklahoma Legislature Introduces HB 1916 to Establish Consumer Data Privacy Rights
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Oklahoma Legislature introduced House Bill 1916, a consumer data privacy measure covering the collection, processing, and sale of personal data of Oklahoma residents. The bill establishes rights for consumers regarding their personal data and imposes obligations on controllers and processors. HB 1916 was referred to a House committee following introduction. Enforcement authority under comparable Oklahoma proposals has been assigned to the Office of the Attorney General, with civil penalties applicable per violation. The bill's current status remains unverified pending review of the official Oklahoma Legislature bill tracking system.

rows.headline: Germany Applies EU Data Governance Act Directly Under Article 288 TFEU
rows.title: Germany Applies EU Data Governance Act Directly Under Article 288 TFEU
rows.summary: On 24 September 2023, Regulation (EU) 2022/868, the Data Governance Act, became applicable across all EU Member States, including Germany, without requiring national transposition. The Act entered into force on 23 June 2022 and was published in the Official Journal at OJ L 152. It establishes a framework for reuse of publicly held data, operation of data intermediation services, and voluntary registration of data altruism organisations. Germany designated a competent authority for data intermediation services and established a single information point for public sector data reuse requests. German supervisory bodies hold primary enforcement responsibility under Article 26. The European Data Innovation Board, established under Article 29, coordinates implementation at EU level.

rows.headline: Netherlands Civil Code Article 6:162 Establishes General Tortious Liability Basis
rows.title: Netherlands Civil Code Article 6:162 Establishes General Tortious Liability Basis
rows.summary: On 1 January 1992, the Netherlands enacted Book 6 of the Burgerlijk Wetboek, codifying Article 6:162 as the general statutory basis for tortious liability. The provision requires any person who commits an unlawful act attributable to them to repair resulting damage suffered by another. It defines unlawful acts as violations of a right, breaches of statutory duty, or conduct contrary to unwritten standards of proper social conduct, subject to grounds of justification. Attribution arises from fault or causes for which the tortfeasor is accountable by law or prevailing opinion. Dutch courts have applied the provision across product liability, privacy, environmental harm, and climate litigation. No monetary threshold is specified within Article 6:162 itself.

rows.headline: Washington State Legislature Introduces House Bill 2503 on Privacy or Technology
rows.title: Washington State Legislature Introduces House Bill 2503 on Privacy or Technology
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Washington State Legislature introduced House Bill 2503. The bill was filed in the House of Representatives and logged in the official state legislative database at app.leg.wa.gov. The specific subject matter, sponsoring member, committee referral, and current procedural status of HB 2503 require verification against official state records. Washington's Legislature operates on a biennial basis, with the bill database providing full text, fiscal notes, and hearing schedules upon introduction. The precise scope of HB 2503 cannot be confirmed from available information at this time.

rows.headline: Tennessee's ELVIS Act Extends Right-of-Publicity Protections to AI Voice and Likeness Cloning
rows.title: Tennessee's ELVIS Act Extends Right-of-Publicity Protections to AI Voice and Likeness Cloning
rows.summary: On 21 March 2024, Governor Bill Lee signed HB 1898, the Ensuring Likeness Voice and Image Security (ELVIS) Act, into law, with effect from 1 July 2024. The Act amends Tennessee's Personal Rights Protection Act of 1984 to prohibit the use of AI systems to simulate an individual's voice or likeness for commercial purposes without prior consent. Liability extends to those who knowingly distribute such AI-generated content, not only its creator. Affected individuals may seek injunctive relief, actual or statutory damages, and attorneys' fees. The legislature cited AI voice cloning of recording artists as a primary concern driving the amendment.

rows.headline: Indonesia's BRIN Publishes National AI Strategy and Roadmap Covering 2020–2045
rows.title: Indonesia's BRIN Publishes National AI Strategy and Roadmap Covering 2020–2045
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Indonesia's National Research and Innovation Agency (BRIN) published the country's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Roadmap, spanning 2020 to 2045. Developed with the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology, the document sets out Indonesia's ambition to become a leading AI nation in South-East Asia by 2045. It identifies five priority deployment sectors: health, bureaucratic reform, education and research, food and agriculture, and smart cities. Implementation is structured across three phases: capability-building (2020–2024), scaling (2025–2039), and innovation leadership (2040–2045). The Roadmap addresses AI ethics, data infrastructure, talent development and regulatory frameworks, and references existing legislation including the Personal Data Protection Law. It is a non-binding strategic policy instrument.

rows.headline: UK's MHRA Appoints Jason Bonander as Chief Digital and Technology Officer
rows.title: UK's MHRA Appoints Jason Bonander as Chief Digital and Technology Officer
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency appointed Jason Bonander as its new Chief Digital and Technology Officer. Bonander joins MHRA to lead the transformation of the technology systems underpinning the regulation of medicines and medical devices in the United Kingdom. The agency described the hire as bringing top global technology talent to the role. As CDTO, Bonander will oversee digital and technology strategy across MHRA's regulatory functions.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: Egypt's MCIT Publishes National AI Strategy Setting Four-Pillar Development Framework
rows.title: Egypt's MCIT Publishes National AI Strategy Setting Four-Pillar Development Framework
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology published the Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, a non-binding policy roadmap for AI development across the Egyptian economy and public sector. The strategy identifies four pillars: building AI infrastructure and data availability, developing human capital through education and training, creating an enabling environment through regulatory frameworks and public-private partnerships, and promoting AI adoption in healthcare, agriculture, education, transport and financial services. The MCIT is designated as the lead coordinating authority for implementation. The strategy references alignment with the African Union's continental AI frameworks and UNESCO AI ethics principles. No binding obligations are imposed on private sector entities.

rows.headline: Saudi Arabia's SDAIA Publishes AI Adoption Framework for Public and Private Entities
rows.title: Saudi Arabia's SDAIA Publishes AI Adoption Framework for Public and Private Entities
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA) published its AI Adoption Framework, a national guidance instrument covering AI deployment across public and private sector entities in the Kingdom. The Framework sets out governance requirements including accountability structures, risk classification of AI use cases, data quality obligations, transparency standards, and human oversight mechanisms. Entities must conduct AI readiness assessments before deployment and assign defined organisational roles for AI governance. Higher-risk use cases, including automated decision-making with material impact on individuals, are subject to enhanced documentation, testing, and human review requirements. SDAIA stated the Framework aligns with internationally recognised AI governance standards while reflecting Saudi Arabia's national regulatory context.

rows.headline: Nigeria's NITDA Publishes Draft Code of Practice for Artificial Intelligence Systems
rows.title: Nigeria's NITDA Publishes Draft Code of Practice for Artificial Intelligence Systems
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Nigeria's National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) published a Draft Code of Practice for Artificial Intelligence Systems, open for public consultation. The Code sets out principles and obligations for AI developers, deployers and operators in Nigeria, covering transparency, accountability, fairness, safety and data protection. It requires AI systems to align with the Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023. High-risk sectors, including healthcare, financial services, law enforcement and critical infrastructure, face heightened expectations including conformity assessments and record-keeping. Public sector bodies deploying AI in administrative or adjudicative functions must provide explainability and individual redress mechanisms. NITDA retains authority to revise and finalise the Code after reviewing stakeholder submissions.

rows.headline: New Jersey Senate Bill 1802 Targets AI Transparency and Consumer Protection
rows.title: New Jersey Senate Bill 1802 Targets AI Transparency and Consumer Protection
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the New Jersey Legislature advanced S 1802, a proposed state-level AI governance bill requiring developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems to notify consumers when automated tools influence decisions affecting employment, housing, credit, and public services. Deployers of high-risk AI systems must provide meaningful explanations of automated outcomes and offer human review of adverse decisions. Developers must conduct impact assessments and maintain compliance documentation before deploying covered systems. Enforcement authority rests with the New Jersey Attorney General, with civil penalties of up to USD 10,000 per violation for first offences under the Consumer Fraud Act framework. The bill remains in committee.

rows.headline: US Congress Proposes Medical Debt Reporting Act to Bar Credit File Inclusion
rows.title: US Congress Proposes Medical Debt Reporting Act to Bar Credit File Inclusion
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the United States Congress advanced the Medical Debt Reporting Act, a federal legislative proposal that would prohibit consumer reporting agencies from including medical debt in consumer credit reports. The bill would amend the Fair Credit Reporting Act by barring both reporting agencies and furnishers, including hospitals and collection agencies, from sharing medical debt data for credit file purposes. Violations would be enforceable under existing FCRA civil liability provisions, with statutory damages ranging from USD 100 to USD 1,000 per violation. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau issued a parallel proposed rule in June 2024 that would similarly remove medical debt from credit reports under Regulation V.

rows.headline: Morocco Tables Digital X.0 Framework Bill Covering AI, Data and Digital Identity
rows.title: Morocco Tables Digital X.0 Framework Bill Covering AI, Data and Digital Identity
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Morocco's General Secretariat of the Government confirmed it is reviewing a draft framework law titled Digital X.0, presented by Minister Delegate for Digital Transition Amal El Fallah Seghrouchni. The bill is structured around three pillars: data governance aligned with existing personal data law 09-08, a citizen-controlled digital identity system, and an interoperability framework requiring explicit user consent for information exchanges between public and private actors. It also addresses cybersecurity as a component of digital trust. The draft forms the legal backbone of Morocco's Maroc Digital 2030 strategy and has not yet been adopted into law.

rows.headline: Utah Governor Signs HB 286 Amending AI Consumer Disclosure and Lab Programme Rules
rows.title: Utah Governor Signs HB 286 Amending AI Consumer Disclosure and Lab Programme Rules
rows.summary: On 20 March 2025, Governor Spencer Cox signed HB 286 into law, amending the Utah Artificial Intelligence Policy Act (Utah Code Title 13, Chapter 70). The amendments revised disclosure obligations for entities deploying generative AI in consumer interactions within regulated occupation contexts, requiring disclosure of the AI nature of an interaction upon consumer request. HB 286 also refined definitions of "generative artificial intelligence" and "regulated occupation" to clarify which businesses fall within the statute's scope. The bill updated eligibility criteria, procedural requirements, and reporting obligations for participants in the Utah AI Learning Laboratory programme. The Utah Division of Consumer Protection retains authority to investigate complaints and refer matters to the Attorney General for enforcement.

rows.headline: China's SAC Issues GB/T 45654-2025 National Standard for Generative AI Service Safety
rows.title: China's SAC Issues GB/T 45654-2025 National Standard for Generative AI Service Safety
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, China's Standardisation Administration (SAC), through National Technical Committee 260 on Cybersecurity, issued GB/T 45654-2025, a recommended national standard setting basic safety requirements for generative AI services across mainland China. The standard covers three principal areas: model training data, model-generated content, and associated safety controls. It names more than 30 specific safety risks, including algorithmic bias, disclosure of personally identifiable information, and copyright infringement. A supply chain security assessment clause for underlying hardware and software is a notable addition over the October 2023 preliminary draft. As a GB/T recommended standard, it carries normative weight under the Cyberspace Administration of China's mandatory Interim Measures for Generative AI Services issued in July 2023.

rows.headline: Nigeria's Federal Government Adopts National Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Governance Roadmap
rows.title: Nigeria's Federal Government Adopts National Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Governance Roadmap
rows.summary: On 2 September 2024, the Federal Government of Nigeria, through the Federal Ministry of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy, published the Nigeria National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (NAIS). The strategy establishes seven pillars covering AI infrastructure, research and innovation, talent development, ethical AI, public sector adoption, industry development and international cooperation. It provides for a National Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics as a central coordinating body and calls for a dedicated national AI regulatory framework addressing algorithmic accountability, bias mitigation and data protection. The strategy cross-references the Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023 and aligns with the African Union's Continental AI Policy Framework.

rows.headline: Hawaii HB 2500 Proposes Impact Assessments and Disclosure Rules for High-Risk AI
rows.title: Hawaii HB 2500 Proposes Impact Assessments and Disclosure Rules for High-Risk AI
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Hawaii State Legislature advanced HB 2500, a bill to regulate the development and deployment of artificial intelligence systems within the state. The measure would require developers and deployers of high-risk AI to conduct pre-deployment impact assessments and disclose when AI materially assists consequential decisions in employment, housing, credit, education, and public services. Affected individuals could request human review of such decisions. Developers supplying AI to public agencies would face additional documentation requirements covering training data and performance metrics. The Hawaii Office of Consumer Protection would administer civil penalties per violation. The bill does not establish a private right of action.

rows.headline: Ecuador's MINTEL Issues Ministerial Agreement 0030 on Personal Data Protection
rows.title: Ecuador's MINTEL Issues Ministerial Agreement 0030 on Personal Data Protection
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Ecuador's Ministry of Telecommunications and the Information Society (MINTEL) issued Ministerial Agreement 0030 under the Organic Law on Personal Data Protection (LOPDP), published in Official Register Supplement No. 459 of 26 May 2021. The Agreement forms part of the secondary regulatory framework operationalising the LOPDP across public and private sector entities processing personal data in Ecuador. MINTEL holds designated authority to issue binding technical and administrative norms under the LOPDP. The full text of Ministerial Agreement 0030 is available through Ecuador's Official Register and the MINTEL portal.

rows.headline: Vietnam's 15th National Assembly Passes Dedicated AI Law, Effective March 2026
rows.title: Vietnam's 15th National Assembly Passes Dedicated AI Law, Effective March 2026
rows.summary: On 11 December 2025, Vietnam's 15th National Assembly approved the Law on Artificial Intelligence, presented by Minister of Science and Technology Nguyen Manh Hung. The law comprises 35 articles and enters into force on 1 March 2026. It establishes a human-centred framework requiring human oversight in important decision-making and creates the legal foundation for domestic AI infrastructure, data governance and research. The State commits to funding a national AI computing centre and an open-data system. The law introduces a national AI development fund, an AI Voucher mechanism for business adoption, and a controlled sandbox regime waiving certain liabilities for sensitive AI applications. It also regulates AI-generated content, algorithmic ethics and cross-border AI service providers operating in Vietnam.

rows.headline: Oman's ITA Issues National AI Policy 2025 Under Vision 2040 Framework
rows.title: Oman's ITA Issues National AI Policy 2025 Under Vision 2040 Framework
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Oman's Information Technology Authority published the National AI Policy 2025, a foundational instrument guiding artificial intelligence development across public and private sectors. Issued under the Ministry of Transport, Communications and Information Technology, the policy designates the ITA as principal coordinating body for AI implementation. It sets strategic priorities covering domestic AI capability, investment in AI infrastructure, and sector integration across energy, logistics, healthcare and public administration. The policy addresses human capital development, calling for expanded AI education and training programmes. On governance, it articulates principles of transparency, accountability and data protection, directs public bodies to align AI procurement with existing data protection standards, and flags cross-border data flows and Gulf Cooperation Council regional cooperation as areas requiring ongoing regulatory attention.

rows.headline: Vermont HB 341 Would Require AI Transparency and Human Review for Consequential Decisions
rows.title: Vermont HB 341 Would Require AI Transparency and Human Review for Consequential Decisions
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Vermont legislators introduced HB 341, a bill that would impose transparency and accountability requirements on developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating in the state. The bill targets consequential decisions in employment, credit, housing, education and healthcare, requiring consumer notification when AI materially contributes to such decisions. A human review mechanism would also be mandatory. Developers of high-risk AI systems would need to complete impact assessments covering accuracy, bias and data governance before deployment. Enforcement authority would vest in the Vermont Attorney General, who could seek civil penalties. The bill creates no private right of action. No committee hearing date had been published at the time of introduction.

rows.headline: Argentina Adopts National AI Plan Setting Strategic Framework for Public and Private Sectors
rows.title: Argentina Adopts National AI Plan Setting Strategic Framework for Public and Private Sectors
rows.summary: On 28 August 2023, Argentina's Secretaría de Innovación Pública published the Plan Nacional de Inteligencia Artificial under the Chief of Cabinet's Office. The Plan establishes strategic priorities across four axes: human capital development, AI-driven productive-sector innovation, public digital infrastructure, and an ethical and regulatory framework aligned with UNESCO and OECD AI principles. The document emphasises algorithmic transparency, non-discrimination, and accountability in automated decision-making by public bodies. The Secretaría de Innovación Pública is designated as primary coordinating body. No specific budgetary envelope was identified in the published instrument.

rows.headline: California SB 942 Mandates Disclosure Labels and Detection Tools for Large AI Systems
rows.title: California SB 942 Mandates Disclosure Labels and Detection Tools for Large AI Systems
rows.summary: On 19 September 2024, Governor Gavin Newsom signed California Senate Bill 942, the AI Transparency Act, into law, with provisions taking effect on 1 January 2026. The Act covers any person or entity owning, operating, or licensing an AI system with more than one million monthly users that generates images, video, audio, or text. Covered providers must offer users a free detection tool, attach latent metadata to AI-generated content where technically feasible, and include a visible disclosure near that content. Civil penalties reach USD 5,000 per violation for failing to provide detection tools and USD 1,000 per piece of undisclosed content. The California Attorney General holds civil enforcement authority.

rows.headline: Illinois General Assembly Introduces HB 4711 to Regulate AI Systems Statewide
rows.title: Illinois General Assembly Introduces HB 4711 to Regulate AI Systems Statewide
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Illinois General Assembly introduced House Bill 4711, a bill to regulate the development, deployment and use of artificial intelligence systems in Illinois. The bill imposes transparency, risk assessment and disclosure obligations on developers and deployers of AI systems used in consequential decisions, including those affecting employment, housing, credit, education and public services. Covered entities must conduct impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI systems. The Illinois Attorney General would receive enforcement authority, including powers to investigate violations and seek civil penalties. The bill also creates a private right of action for harmed Illinois residents. HB 4711 was referred to a House committee following introduction.

rows.headline: Hawaii Legislature Takes Up Senate Bill 59 on Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.title: Hawaii Legislature Takes Up Senate Bill 59 on Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.summary: Hawaii Senate Bill 59 was introduced in the Hawaii State Legislature during the 2025 session, addressing the regulation of artificial intelligence systems. The bill concerns obligations placed on developers or deployers of AI systems within the state, with subject matter potentially including transparency requirements, impact assessments, and consumer notification where AI is used in consequential decisions. The bill was referred to relevant Senate committees upon introduction. No enactment date or gubernatorial action has been confirmed. Specific provisions, exemptions, enforcement mechanisms, and any civil penalties are set out in the bill text maintained by the Hawaii State Legislature.

rows.headline: EU Regulation 2018/1725 Sets Data Protection Rules for Union Institutions and Bodies
rows.title: EU Regulation 2018/1725 Sets Data Protection Rules for Union Institutions and Bodies
rows.summary: On 23 October 2018, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, published in the Official Journal on 21 November 2018. The Regulation entered into force and applied from 11 December 2018, repealing Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. It aligns data protection rules for EU institutions and bodies with the GDPR, establishes legal bases for processing, sets out data subject rights, and imposes controller and processor obligations. The European Data Protection Supervisor is designated as the independent supervisory authority and holds investigatory, corrective, and fine-imposing powers under Article 52.

rows.headline: Hawaii Legislature Introduces HB 2500 to Regulate AI Systems Statewide
rows.title: Hawaii Legislature Introduces HB 2500 to Regulate AI Systems Statewide
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Hawaii State Legislature introduced House Bill 2500, a proposal to regulate artificial intelligence development and deployment within the state. The bill covers automated decision-making systems used in employment, housing, credit, and public services. Provisions include mandatory impact assessments before high-risk AI deployment, disclosure obligations where AI drives consequential decisions, and human-review mechanisms for automated outcomes. Separate provisions set procurement and operational standards for state executive agencies acquiring AI tools. Fiscal notes address implementation costs for agencies assigned compliance and oversight duties. Final operative language remains subject to amendment through the committee process.

rows.headline: United States Patent Act Codifies Federal Rules on Patent Rights and Procedures
rows.title: United States Patent Act Codifies Federal Rules on Patent Rights and Procedures
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the United States Patent Act, codified at Title 35 of the United States Code, was recorded as the primary federal statute governing patent rights. First enacted in 1790 and restructured by the Patent Act of 1952, the statute was most recently amended by the Leahy-Smith America Invents Act of 2011, which shifted the United States to a first-inventor-to-file system. The Act grants patent holders the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling a patented invention for twenty years from the application filing date. The USPTO administers the statute within the Department of Commerce.

rows.headline: Japan's National Diet Adopts AI Promotion Act as First Dedicated AI Framework Law
rows.title: Japan's National Diet Adopts AI Promotion Act as First Dedicated AI Framework Law
rows.summary: In 2025, Japan's National Diet enacted the Act on Promotion of Research, Development and Utilisation of Artificial Intelligence-Related Technology, establishing the country's first statutory framework for AI governance. The Act sets foundational principles covering human-centricity, safety, fairness, transparency and accountability. It allocates responsibilities across the national government, local public entities, businesses and individual users. The national government bears primary responsibility for formulating and implementing AI promotion measures consistent with those principles. The Act also directs the government to support data sharing for AI development, foster AI literacy and pursue international cooperation on governance standards. The framework adopts a promotional rather than prescriptive design and enumerates no specific penalties or administrative fines.

rows.headline: Mauritius Government Publishes National Artificial Intelligence Strategy Under Vision 2030
rows.title: Mauritius Government Publishes National Artificial Intelligence Strategy Under Vision 2030
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Government of Mauritius published a National Artificial Intelligence Strategy through the Ministry of Information Technology, Communication and Innovation. The non-statutory policy document identifies priority sectors for AI deployment, including financial services, healthcare, agriculture, tourism and public administration. It sets out principles covering data governance, algorithmic accountability and ethical AI use, referencing the Data Protection Act 2017 as a foundational instrument. The Strategy calls for investment in digital skills, AI research centres and regulatory sandboxes for innovation in sectors such as fintech. Implementation is to be coordinated by MTIC with public bodies and private sector stakeholders. No budgetary allocation is confirmed in available documentation.

rows.headline: United States Congress Enacted the Copyright Act of 1976, Codified at Title 17
rows.title: United States Congress Enacted the Copyright Act of 1976, Codified at Title 17
rows.summary: On 19 October 1976, Congress enacted the Copyright Act of 1976 (Pub. L. 94-553), superseding the Copyright Act of 1909 and taking effect on 1 January 1978. The Act establishes copyright protection for original works fixed in a tangible medium, covering literary, musical, dramatic, pictorial, and audiovisual works under 17 U.S.C. § 102. Protection lasts for the author's life plus 70 years. Exclusive rights include reproduction, distribution, and public performance. The Act codifies fair use at 17 U.S.C. § 107 and sets statutory damages from USD 750 to USD 30,000 per infringed work, rising to USD 150,000 for wilful infringement.

rows.headline: Brazil's LGPD Establishes Federal Rules for Personal Data Processing Under Law 13,709
rows.title: Brazil's LGPD Establishes Federal Rules for Personal Data Processing Under Law 13,709
rows.summary: On 14 August 2018, Brazil's National Congress enacted Law No. 13,709, the Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais (LGPD), governing personal data processing by public and private entities. Law No. 13,853 of 8 July 2019 subsequently amended the statute and established the Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD) as supervisory authority. Core provisions entered into force on 18 September 2020, with administrative sanctions following on 1 August 2021. Article 52 sanctions include fines of up to 2% of Brazilian revenue, capped at BRL 50,000,000 (approximately USD 10,000,000) per infraction. The law enumerates ten legal bases for processing and grants data subjects rights of access, correction, portability and deletion.

rows.headline: Türkiye's Grand National Assembly Receives Draft Law Regulating AI-Generated Content
rows.title: Türkiye's Grand National Assembly Receives Draft Law Regulating AI-Generated Content
rows.summary: On 7 November 2025, a draft law amending the Turkish Penal Code and four other statutes was submitted to the Grand National Assembly of Türkiye. The 11-article Draft Law defines AI systems in statute and targets deepfake content specifically. Content providers and AI developers face joint liability for deepfake material, which must carry a visible 'Generated by Artificial Intelligence' label. Access to rights-violating or security-threatening content must be blocked within six hours. Administrative fines range from EUR 14,000 to EUR 270,000, imposed by the BTK. Users who direct an AI system to commit insult offences are treated as principal offenders under the amended Penal Code.

rows.headline: California Adds Neural Data to CCPA Sensitive Personal Information Categories Under SB 1223
rows.title: California Adds Neural Data to CCPA Sensitive Personal Information Categories Under SB 1223
rows.summary: On 15 October 2023, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 1223 into law, amending the California Consumer Privacy Act to classify neural data as sensitive personal information. The amendment introduced Civil Code section 1798.99.82, defining neural data as information generated by measuring activity of a consumer's central or peripheral nervous system. Consumers may direct businesses to limit use and disclosure of such data to purposes reasonably necessary for requested services. The California Privacy Protection Agency and the Attorney General share enforcement authority. Penalties reach USD 2,500 per unintentional violation and USD 7,500 per intentional violation or one involving a minor's data.

rows.headline: Spain's CNMV Issues Supervisory Circular CIR/DTSA/010/25 on Securities Markets
rows.title: Spain's CNMV Issues Supervisory Circular CIR/DTSA/010/25 on Securities Markets
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Spain's Comisión Nacional del Mercado de Valores published Circular CIR/DTSA/010/25, the tenth circular issued by its Dirección de Técnica y Supervisión de Actividades in 2025. The circular is addressed to regulated entities within the CNMV's supervisory perimeter, covering securities and financial market conduct matters. The reference follows the CNMV's standard internal classification, where 'CIR' denotes a circular, 'DTSA' identifies the issuing directorate, '010' marks its sequence position, and '25' indicates the year. The specific substantive content, affected entity categories, and associated deadlines were not available in the source material. The official text is accessible through the CNMV's circular register at cnmv.es.

rows.headline: New Hampshire HB 1725 Proposes Consumer Protection Rules for High-Risk AI Systems
rows.title: New Hampshire HB 1725 Proposes Consumer Protection Rules for High-Risk AI Systems
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the New Hampshire General Court introduced HB 1725, a bill establishing obligations for developers and deployers of high-risk artificial intelligence systems operating in the state. The bill targets AI systems that make or materially influence consequential decisions in areas including employment, housing, credit, education, healthcare, and insurance. Developers would be required to supply deployers with compliance documentation. Deployers would bear responsibility for consumer disclosures, opt-out mechanisms, and appeal processes. The New Hampshire Attorney General would enforce the provisions, with civil penalties applicable to violations. The bill defines high-risk AI by domain of application and the degree of automated influence over individual consumer outcomes.

rows.headline: Kenya's Ministry of ICT Publishes National Artificial Intelligence Strategy
rows.title: Kenya's Ministry of ICT Publishes National Artificial Intelligence Strategy
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Government of Kenya, through the Ministry of Information, Communications and the Digital Economy, published the Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy. The Strategy sets out a national framework for AI development, adoption and governance across public and private sectors. Priority sectors include agriculture, healthcare, education, financial services and public administration. Guiding principles include inclusivity, transparency, accountability and individual rights protection. The Strategy calls for a review of existing legal frameworks, references the Data Protection Act 2019 as the primary data governance instrument, and proposes an AI Centre of Excellence to coordinate research and capacity-building. The instrument does not create binding legal obligations.

rows.headline: New York City Local Law 144 Regulates Automated Employment Decision Tools Under Article 44-B
rows.title: New York City Local Law 144 Regulates Automated Employment Decision Tools Under Article 44-B
rows.summary: On 6 January 2023, New York City Local Law 144 of 2021 came into full effect after the New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection published final rules. The law, codified at Article 44-B of the New York General Business Law, applies to employers and employment agencies using automated employment decision tools (AEDTs) to screen candidates or employees for roles based in New York City. Covered employers must commission an independent bias audit within one year before deploying an AEDT, publish audit results publicly, and notify affected individuals at least ten business days in advance. Civil penalties reach USD 1,500 per violation, rising to USD 1,500 per day for subsequent violations.

rows.headline: Europe's EPC Establishes Unified Patent Grant System Across 39 Contracting States
rows.title: Europe's EPC Establishes Unified Patent Grant System Across 39 Contracting States
rows.summary: On 5 October 1973, the European Patent Convention was concluded in Munich, establishing the European Patent Organisation and a unified procedure for granting patents. The Convention entered into force on 7 October 1977, with a substantially revised text taking effect on 13 December 2007. The European Patent Office, headquartered in Munich, administers the system independently of the European Union. Membership spans 39 contracting states, including non-EU countries such as Turkey, Norway, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom. A granted European patent takes effect as a national patent in each designated state, subject to individual validation, translation, and maintenance requirements.

rows.headline: France's Légifrance Lists All Decrees Published on 21 December 2012
rows.title: France's Légifrance Lists All Decrees Published on 21 December 2012
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Légifrance indexed a search record covering French decrees dated 21 December 2012. The record links to the Journal Officiel de la République Française search portal, where decrees bearing that date are retrievable by instrument type and publication date. The source does not identify a specific decree by NOR code, JORF reference, or subject matter. Multiple decrees may share a single adoption date across policy domains including public administration, taxation, social security, and environmental regulation. The authoritative texts are held in the Légifrance database, operated by DILA under the Prime Minister's office.

rows.headline: Tennessee HB 1898 Proposes Consumer Health Data Privacy Rules for Non-HIPAA Entities
rows.title: Tennessee HB 1898 Proposes Consumer Health Data Privacy Rules for Non-HIPAA Entities
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Tennessee General Assembly introduced HB 1898, a bill establishing privacy requirements for consumer health data held by entities outside HIPAA's scope. The bill defines consumer health data broadly to include information linked to a consumer's physical or mental health, covering data from wearables and digital health platforms. Regulated entities must obtain affirmative consent before collecting or sharing health data beyond the purposes of a requested service and must publish a consumer health data privacy policy. The bill grants consumers rights to access, deletion, and consent withdrawal. Enforcement authority rests with the Tennessee Attorney General, and consumers retain a private right of action for violations.

rows.headline: Louisiana Legislature Tables HB 230 Targeting Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.title: Louisiana Legislature Tables HB 230 Targeting Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Louisiana Legislature introduced House Bill 230, a proposed measure to regulate artificial intelligence systems within the state. The bill was filed during the 2025 Regular Session of the Louisiana House of Representatives. Its precise scope, operative provisions, and current procedural status were not confirmed from available source material at the time of this entry. To pass, HB 230 must clear both the House and the Senate before being presented to the Governor for signature or veto. Full bill text, amendments, and status updates are available through the Louisiana Legislature's official portal at legis.la.gov.

rows.headline: Utah Governor Signs SB 226 Requiring App Stores to Verify Minor Users' Ages
rows.title: Utah Governor Signs SB 226 Requiring App Stores to Verify Minor Users' Ages
rows.summary: On 13 March 2025, Utah Governor Spencer Cox signed Senate Bill 226, the Minor Protection in App Stores Act, into law. The Act requires app store operators distributing mobile applications to Utah users to implement age verification for all account holders. Where a user is under 18, operators must obtain verifiable parental consent before permitting any download or purchase. The compliance burden falls on app store operators, not individual app developers. Operators must also provide parents with tools to review, approve, or block applications on minor accounts. The Utah Division of Consumer Protection will enforce violations and establish civil penalty schedules under existing consumer protection frameworks. Provisions take effect on 1 May 2025.

rows.headline: Hawaii Legislature Introduces SB 2967 to Regulate Artificial Intelligence Systems
rows.title: Hawaii Legislature Introduces SB 2967 to Regulate Artificial Intelligence Systems
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Hawaii State Legislature introduced Senate Bill 2967, a proposal to regulate the development and deployment of artificial intelligence systems within the state. The bill targets developers and deployers of high-risk AI applications, establishing requirements for algorithmic transparency, impact assessments, and disclosures to individuals subject to consequential automated decisions. It applies to entities meeting defined thresholds of activity or scale. The bill also addresses consumer rights in AI-assisted decisions affecting employment, housing, credit, and access to services. As of the published date, SB 2967 remained under committee review and had not been enacted into law.

rows.headline: California Legislature Advances AB 1018 Frontier AI Safety and Auditing Bill
rows.title: California Legislature Advances AB 1018 Frontier AI Safety and Auditing Bill
rows.summary: On 20 February 2025, Assembly Member Bauer-Kahan introduced AB 1018, the Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act, in the California State Assembly. The bill targets developers of frontier AI models trained above 10^26 floating-point operations. It would require pre-deployment safety evaluations, kill-switch capabilities, third-party audits, and safety documentation before covered models reach California users. Developers must draft written safety protocols before initial training and report material incidents to the California Government Operations Agency. The bill establishes a private right of action and civil penalties enforceable by the Attorney General. AB 1018 cleared the Assembly Committee on Privacy and Consumer Protection in April 2025 and remained under active consideration as of May 2025.

rows.headline: Ecuador's National Assembly Enacts LOTDA Digital and Audiovisual Transformation Law
rows.title: Ecuador's National Assembly Enacts LOTDA Digital and Audiovisual Transformation Law
rows.summary: On 23 November 2023, Ecuador's National Assembly approved the Organic Law for Digital and Audiovisual Transformation (LOTDA), published in the Official Gazette and entered into force under Ecuadorian constitutional promulgation procedures. The law requires digital platform operators serving Ecuadorian users to register with ARCOTEL. Streaming and video-on-demand platforms must meet content quotas for Ecuadorian and Ibero-American productions and contribute to national audiovisual content financing administered by CORDICOM. The LOTDA also mandates algorithmic transparency, terms disclosure for digital commerce, and cybersecurity obligations for critical infrastructure operators. Maximum administrative fines are graduated by infringement category and can reach tens of thousands of USD.

rows.headline: California Business and Professions Code Section 22757.2 Restricts Connected Toy Operators on Children's Data
rows.title: California Business and Professions Code Section 22757.2 Restricts Connected Toy Operators on Children's Data
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, California's Business and Professions Code Section 22757.2 established data privacy obligations for operators of internet-connected toys and devices directed at children. The provision prohibits operators from using, disclosing, or retaining children's personal information beyond what is reasonably necessary to provide the requested service. Covered data includes geolocation data and voice recordings. The statute bars use of children's data for targeted advertising or purposes inconsistent with the collection context. Operators must implement reasonable security procedures appropriate to the data collected. The California Attorney General enforces the provision, and violations carry civil penalties under the broader Code.

rows.headline: US Congress Codified Fair Use Doctrine in Copyright Act of 1976
rows.title: US Congress Codified Fair Use Doctrine in Copyright Act of 1976
rows.summary: On 19 October 1976, the United States Congress enacted the Copyright Act of 1976 (Public Law 94-553), which took effect on 1 January 1978. Section 107, codified at 17 U.S.C. § 107, established fair use as an express statutory defence to copyright infringement for the first time in federal law. The provision identifies four factors courts must weigh: purpose and character of use, nature of the work, amount used, and market effect. No single factor is determinative. In Campbell v. Acuff-Rose Music (1994), the Supreme Court confirmed all four factors must be weighed together. As of 2024 and 2025, multiple federal proceedings are examining whether training large language models on copyrighted works falls within Section 107.

rows.headline: Iowa General Assembly Considers House Bill 406 on Consumer Data Protection
rows.title: Iowa General Assembly Considers House Bill 406 on Consumer Data Protection
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Iowa's General Assembly introduced House Bill 406, a standalone consumer data protection measure filed in the Iowa House of Representatives. The bill proposes obligations on data controllers and processors operating in Iowa, covering consumer rights to access, correction, deletion, and portability of personal data. It also addresses opt-out rights for targeted advertising and data sales. Sensitive data categories and data protection assessments are included in scope. Enforcement authority would rest exclusively with the Iowa Attorney General, with no private right of action. Civil penalties for intentional violations would reach up to USD 7,500 per violation. A cure period would apply before enforcement action.

rows.headline: Israel's Innovation Authority Publishes National AI Ethics and Regulatory Policy Framework
rows.title: Israel's Innovation Authority Publishes National AI Ethics and Regulatory Policy Framework
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Israel's Innovation Authority and Ministry of Innovation, Science and Technology published a national policy document on artificial intelligence regulation and ethics. The document sets out foundational principles covering transparency, accountability, fairness, privacy and human oversight for AI developers, operators and public-sector bodies in Israel. It identifies higher-risk application areas, including healthcare, financial services, critical infrastructure and law enforcement, and provides sector-specific guidance for each. The framework does not impose statutory obligations but represents authoritative government policy. The authorities indicated that dedicated regulatory instruments may follow for high-risk domains.

rows.headline: Arizona Legislature Introduces House Bill 4098 With No Detail Publicly Confirmed
rows.title: Arizona Legislature Introduces House Bill 4098 With No Detail Publicly Confirmed
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Arizona House Bill 4098 was recorded in the Arizona Legislature. No secondary source content was available at the time of analysis to confirm the bill's subject matter, sponsor, committee referral, operative provisions, or procedural status. The bill is indexed under Arizona's legislative calendar and tracked via the official azleg.gov portal, where full text, amendment history, and current status are recorded. No further substantive detail regarding the bill's regulatory scope or affected parties can be reported on the basis of information currently available.

rows.headline: EU Adopts Directive 2001/29/EC Harmonising Copyright in the Digital Environment
rows.title: EU Adopts Directive 2001/29/EC Harmonising Copyright in the Digital Environment
rows.summary: On 22 May 2001, the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union adopted Directive 2001/29/EC on copyright and related rights in the information society. The Directive harmonises exclusive rights covering reproduction, communication to the public, making available, and distribution across EU Member States. It implements obligations arising from the 1996 WIPO Copyright Treaty and the WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty. Member States were required to transpose its provisions by 22 December 2002. Articles 6 and 7 require legal protection against circumvention of technological measures and removal of rights-management information. The Directive has since served as a central reference in Court of Justice of the European Union case law on hyperlinking, embedding, and the communication-to-the-public right.

rows.headline: Australia's ADJR Act 1977 Establishes Federal Framework for Commonwealth Administrative Review
rows.title: Australia's ADJR Act 1977 Establishes Federal Framework for Commonwealth Administrative Review
rows.summary: On 16 June 1977, the Australian Parliament enacted the Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977 (Cth), which received Royal Assent that day and came into operation on 1 October 1980. The Act created a codified pathway for persons aggrieved by Commonwealth administrative decisions to seek review in the Federal Court of Australia. Section 5 sets out grounds including lack of jurisdiction, procedural error, denial of natural justice, and fraud. Section 13 entitles applicants to written reasons. Schedule 1 excludes certain decisions, including those under the Migration Act 1958. The Act operates alongside the High Court's constitutional jurisdiction under section 75(v) of the Constitution.

rows.headline: Saudi Arabia's SDAIA National AI Strategy Targets USD 35 Billion GDP Contribution by 2030
rows.title: Saudi Arabia's SDAIA National AI Strategy Targets USD 35 Billion GDP Contribution by 2030
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, Saudi Arabia's Saudi Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA) published the Kingdom's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy, establishing an overarching policy framework for AI development across public and private sectors. Issued under SDAIA's mandate, derived from Royal Decree M/19 of 2019, the Strategy sets objectives across AI infrastructure, data ecosystems, talent development, research and innovation, and international partnerships. Priority sectors include healthcare, education, transport, energy and financial services. SDAIA projects AI will contribute approximately USD 35 billion to the Saudi economy by 2030, aligned with Vision 2030 diversification goals. SDAIA serves as the central implementation body, with sector regulators including the Communications, Space and Technology Commission retaining concurrent authority.

rows.headline: United Kingdom's Patents Act 1977 Establishes Core Patent Rights and Procedure
rows.title: United Kingdom's Patents Act 1977 Establishes Core Patent Rights and Procedure
rows.summary: On 29 July 1977, the Parliament of the United Kingdom enacted the Patents Act 1977, which received Royal Assent and came into force in stages, with main provisions taking effect on 1 June 1978. The Act replaced the Patents Act 1949 and gave effect to UK obligations under the European Patent Convention 1973 and the Patent Co-operation Treaty 1970. Section 1 sets patentability conditions, requiring novelty, inventive step, and industrial application. Section 60 confers exclusive exploitation rights on patent holders. Sections 39 to 43 govern employee invention ownership and employer compensation obligations. The Intellectual Property Office administers the Act, and the Intellectual Property Act 2014 inserted IPO opinion provisions under sections 74A and 74B.

rows.headline: European Commission Advances Digital Fairness Act to Address Consumer Harms in Digital Markets
rows.title: European Commission Advances Digital Fairness Act to Address Consumer Harms in Digital Markets
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the European Commission progressed its proposed Digital Fairness Act, a pre-legislative instrument intended to strengthen consumer protection across digital markets in EU member states. The proposal targets dark patterns, algorithmic personalisation exploiting behavioural data, subscription traps, and commercial use of minors' data. Preparatory work included a fitness check of three existing consumer directives and a targeted consultation published in 2024. The Commission indicated a formal legislative proposal was anticipated in 2025, subject to the ordinary legislative procedure. No binding obligations have been established, and scope, definitions and enforcement mechanisms remain subject to the legislative process.

rows.headline: Israel's Government Adopts National AI Strategy With USD 450 Million Budget
rows.title: Israel's Government Adopts National AI Strategy With USD 450 Million Budget
rows.summary: On 15 November 2021, the Israeli government approved Resolution No. 569, formally adopting the National Program for Artificial Intelligence. The programme allocates approximately ILS 1.6 billion (USD 450 million) over multiple years across four pillars: AI research and academic infrastructure, talent development, commercial adoption, and governance. A national steering committee chaired by the Prime Minister's Office will oversee implementation. Priority deployment sectors include health, agriculture, transportation, and defence. The programme also directs relevant authorities to monitor international AI regulatory developments, including the EU's legislative process, with a view to aligning Israeli standards where appropriate. Israel's target is to become a leading AI nation by 2030.

rows.headline: Ecuador Adopts National Strategy for Ethical and Responsible AI Development
rows.title: Ecuador Adopts National Strategy for Ethical and Responsible AI Development
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Government of Ecuador adopted a national strategy governing the ethical and responsible development and use of artificial intelligence. The strategy, understood to have been issued through the Ministerio de Telecomunicaciones y de la Sociedad de la Información, establishes a policy framework for AI governance across public and private sectors. The instrument sets out principles including transparency, accountability and human oversight of automated systems. It applies to public institutions deploying AI in governmental processes and to private sector actors operating within Ecuadorian territory. The ministry is noted as responsible for coordinating implementation across government entities.

rows.headline: Israel's Privacy Protection Authority Issues Draft AI Guidelines Ahead of Amendment 13
rows.title: Israel's Privacy Protection Authority Issues Draft AI Guidelines Ahead of Amendment 13
rows.summary: On 11 May 2025, Israel's Privacy Protection Authority published draft guidelines interpreting the Privacy Protection Law as it applies to AI systems. The guidelines address informed consent, requiring organisations to disclose data purposes, recipients, sources, processing methods, and associated risks. The Authority stated that organisations training AI models are very likely required to appoint a Data Protection Officer under Amendment 13, which enters into force in August 2025. On web scraping, the Authority stated that collecting personal data to train AI models without informed consent constitutes unlawful privacy infringement. Platform operators bear an obligation to prevent scraping of personal data from platforms they manage.

rows.headline: China's State Council Issues Trial Measures for AI Science and Technology Ethics Administration
rows.title: China's State Council Issues Trial Measures for AI Science and Technology Ethics Administration
rows.summary: On 9 October 2023, China's National Science and Technology Ethics Committee published the Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics (Trial), which entered into force on the same date. The instrument establishes tiered ethical review requirements for AI research, development and application. Organisations conducting higher-risk AI activities must obtain formal review from a recognised ethics body before commencing work. Designated high-risk categories include AI affecting human cognition, medical applications, biometric data processing, and use in education and employment decisions. Institutions must maintain a dedicated ethics committee and report incidents to competent authorities. Non-compliance may result in suspension of research activities or disqualification from public funding.

rows.headline: California SB 420 Proposes AI Transparency and Disclosure Rules for State Procurement
rows.title: California SB 420 Proposes AI Transparency and Disclosure Rules for State Procurement
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the California State Legislature advanced SB 420, a bill setting transparency and disclosure requirements for artificial intelligence systems used in state government operations and procurement. The bill requires state agencies that deploy AI tools to document the nature and scope of that use and make information available to affected individuals. Vendors supplying AI systems must disclose training data provenance, model performance metrics, and known limitations. Procurement officers must assess accuracy and fairness standards before contract award. SB 420 also proposes a registry of AI systems in use across state government, maintained by a designated state authority.

rows.headline: California's Age-Appropriate Design Code Imposes Child-Safety Obligations on Online Platforms
rows.title: California's Age-Appropriate Design Code Imposes Child-Safety Obligations on Online Platforms
rows.summary: On 15 September 2022, Governor Gavin Newsom signed Assembly Bill 2273 into law, adding sections 22757 through 22757.5 to the California Business and Professions Code. The provisions took effect on 1 July 2024. Section 22757.3 requires covered businesses to set default privacy settings to the highest level available, avoid using children's personal information in ways detrimental to their health, and present policies in accessible language. Covered businesses must also complete a Data Protection Impact Assessment before launching any service likely to reach minors. The California Attorney General enforces the Act, with civil penalties of up to USD 2,500 per affected child for negligent violations and USD 7,500 for intentional violations. Enforcement scope remains subject to ongoing federal litigation.

rows.headline: Brazil Enacts Law No. 15,211/2025 Extending Child Protections to Digital Platforms
rows.title: Brazil Enacts Law No. 15,211/2025 Extending Child Protections to Digital Platforms
rows.summary: On 6 March 2025, Brazil's President signed Law No. 15,211/2025, amending the Estatuto da Criança e do Adolescente to cover digital environments. The law imposes obligations on social networks, applications, and digital platforms operating in Brazil regarding users under eighteen. Requirements include age verification, restrictions on algorithmic amplification of content harmful to minors, and prohibitions on targeted advertising directed at children. The law also governs minors' personal data processing alongside the LGPD and ANPD guidance. Platforms breaching the provisions face fines of up to BRL 50,000,000 (approximately USD 9,100,000) per infraction, plus potential suspension of activities in Brazil. Platforms have 180 days from publication to adapt.

rows.headline: Illinois Governor Signs HB 4799 Requiring AI Disclosure in Employment Decisions
rows.title: Illinois Governor Signs HB 4799 Requiring AI Disclosure in Employment Decisions
rows.summary: On 9 August 2024, Illinois Governor JB Pritzker signed HB 4799 into law, amending the Illinois Human Rights Act to regulate employer use of artificial intelligence in employment decisions. The Illinois General Assembly had passed the bill on 28 May 2024. Provisions take effect on 1 January 2026. Employers are prohibited from using AI in ways that discriminate against protected classes and must notify employees and applicants when AI substantially influences recruitment, hiring, promotion, discharge, discipline, or performance evaluation. The Illinois Department of Human Rights will enforce the disclosure framework and study algorithmic discrimination, reporting findings and recommendations to the General Assembly.

rows.headline: California AB 412 Strengthens Genetic Data Privacy Rules for Direct-to-Consumer Testing Companies
rows.title: California AB 412 Strengthens Genetic Data Privacy Rules for Direct-to-Consumer Testing Companies
rows.summary: On 29 April 2025, the California State Legislature introduced Assembly Bill 412, extending privacy protections for genetic data held by direct-to-consumer testing companies. The bill requires companies to obtain express consumer consent before collecting, using, or disclosing genetic data. Consumers gain the right to request deletion of their data and destruction of biological samples. AB 412 also mandates consumer notice and an opportunity to withdraw consent before any acquisition, merger, or change of control takes effect. The California Attorney General and individual consumers may bring enforcement actions, with civil penalties and statutory damages available for violations.

rows.headline: Saudi Arabia's SDAIA Publishes AI Ethics Principles Across Seven Core Values
rows.title: Saudi Arabia's SDAIA Publishes AI Ethics Principles Across Seven Core Values
rows.summary: On 28 September 2023, Saudi Arabia's Data and Artificial Intelligence Authority (SDAIA) published its AI Ethics Principles, a national guidance framework for the development, deployment and use of AI systems. The document organises ethical obligations around seven values: fairness, privacy and data protection, reliability and safety, transparency and explainability, accountability, human centricity, and social and environmental wellbeing. SDAIA aligned the Principles with Saudi Vision 2030 and its existing Personal Data Protection Law. Although framed as guidance rather than binding regulation, SDAIA designated the framework the authoritative national reference for ethical AI practice, indicating future binding instruments may draw on its provisions.

rows.headline: Nebraska Legislature Introduces LB 1083 to Amend State Data Privacy Framework
