rows.title: Nebraska Legislature Introduces LB 1083 to Amend State Data Privacy Framework
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the Nebraska Legislature introduced Legislative Bill 1083, a measure addressing data privacy requirements for businesses operating in the state. The bill sets out obligations for data controllers and processors, including consumer rights to access, correct, delete, and opt out of personal data processing for targeted advertising and data sales. Entities meeting defined thresholds of data processing activity or revenue would fall within scope. Nebraska's Attorney General holds enforcement authority, with no private right of action available. Nebraska previously enacted the Nebraska Data Privacy Act under LB 1074 in 2024. LB 1083 was referred to a standing committee upon introduction.

rows.headline: President Trump Signs Executive Order Revoking Biden-Era AI Safety Framework
rows.title: President Trump Signs Executive Order Revoking Biden-Era AI Safety Framework
rows.summary: On 20 January 2025, President Trump signed Executive Order 14179, titled 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in Artificial Intelligence', published in the Federal Register. The Order revoked Biden administration Executive Order 14110 of October 2023, which had established AI safety evaluations, mandatory reporting for frontier-model developers, and civil-rights guidance for federal procurement. EO 14179 directs the Office of Science and Technology Policy and the White House AI and Crypto Czar to develop a national AI action plan within 180 days. The Order also rescinded OMB memoranda M-24-10 and M-24-18, removing federal requirements for Chief AI Officers, AI-use-case inventories, and minimum risk-management standards for rights-affecting applications.

rows.headline: New Mexico House Bill 141 Proposes Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.title: New Mexico House Bill 141 Proposes Artificial Intelligence Regulation
rows.summary: On 29 April 2026, the New Mexico Legislature published records relating to House Bill 141, a proposed measure addressing the regulation of artificial intelligence systems within the state. The bill was introduced in the New Mexico House of Representatives during the 2025 legislative session. HB 141 targets the development, deployment, and use of AI systems in New Mexico. The precise scope, including covered system categories, enforcement mechanisms, responsible regulatory body, and any penalty structures, is set out in the bill text and any amendments adopted during committee consideration. The bill remains subject to the full legislative process, including potential Senate consideration and the Governor's signature.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Approves Alliance du Commerce Article 40 Code of Conduct for Apparel and Footwear Retail
rows.title: France's CNIL Approves Alliance du Commerce Article 40 Code of Conduct for Apparel and Footwear Retail
rows.summary: On 28 April 2026, France's CNIL approved an Article 40 GDPR code of conduct submitted by Alliance du Commerce, covering the apparel and footwear retail sector. The code is binding on all signatories. Compliance oversight sits with an external monitoring body, not the CNIL itself, in line with the GDPR's code-of-conduct framework. Alliance du Commerce is a French trade federation representing apparel and footwear retailers.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK DSIT Secretary Calls for Greater British Control Over AI Infrastructure
rows.title: UK DSIT Secretary Calls for Greater British Control Over AI Infrastructure
rows.summary: On 28 April 2026, Technology Secretary Peter Kyle delivered a speech calling for a decisive shift toward backing British technology companies in AI development. Kyle argued that the United Kingdom must secure greater control and leverage over AI systems to protect national security amid a fractured global order. The speech framed AI as a force reshaping global power, security, and prosperity, and positioned domestic tech investment as a strategic priority. Kyle did not announce specific funding commitments or legislative measures in the address.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: Spain's INCIBE and AESIA Launch Joint AI and Digital Safety Campaign for Young People
rows.title: Spain's INCIBE and AESIA Launch Joint AI and Digital Safety Campaign for Young People
rows.summary: On 28 April 2026, Spain's Instituto Nacional de Ciberseguridad (INCIBE) and the Agencia Española de Supervisión de la Inteligencia Artificial (AESIA) launched a joint awareness campaign on AI use and digital safety for young people. The campaign targets three audiences: families, teachers, and young people themselves. Content is organised into three thematic blocks, released over several weeks. Materials include blog articles, infographics, podcasts, and online workshops. Topics covered include identifying AI-generated content, AI risks and opportunities in classrooms, and guidance for parents on supporting children's digital safety.

rows.headline: Belgium Adopts Revised National Cyber Crisis Response Plan Under NIS2 Framework
rows.title: Belgium Adopts Revised National Cyber Crisis Response Plan Under NIS2 Framework
rows.summary: On 27 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published a revised national cyber crisis response plan in the Belgian Official Gazette, adopted by Royal Decree. The plan is grounded in the Law of 26 April 2024 transposing the NIS2 Directive and the Law of 25 May 2007 on civil security, replacing the 2017 framework. The CCB reported 556 cybersecurity incidents in 2025, of which eight carried national impact. Public administration and healthcare were identified as the most frequently targeted sectors. No financial penalties are associated with the measure.

rows.headline: EU EDPB Adopts Opinion 10/2026 on Dutch DPA Approval of SLB Group Controller BCRs
rows.title: EU EDPB Adopts Opinion 10/2026 on Dutch DPA Approval of SLB Group Controller BCRs
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 10/2026 under Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, assessing the Dutch Data Protection Authority's draft decision to approve Controller Binding Corporate Rules submitted by the SLB Group. The Dutch DPA acts as BCR Lead in the matter. The EDPB confined its review to whether the BCRs meet Art. 47 GDPR requirements and the conditions in EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on Controller BCRs. The EDPB noted that data exporters subject to GDPR remain responsible for assessing third-country protection levels and applying any necessary supplementary measures, independently of BCR approval. The opinion was published on 27 April 2026.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes Recommended DPO Activity-Report Template for Organisations
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Recommended DPO Activity-Report Template for Organisations
rows.summary: On 27 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published a recommended template for data protection officer activity reports. The template is not legally required but is presented as a recommended tool. CNIL identifies three core functions for the report: piloting an organisation's compliance programme, providing accountability to senior management, and documenting the DPO's own actions. Organisations may choose to produce reports on a quarterly, semi-annual, or annual basis. The guidance is aimed at helping DPOs structure and communicate their work more consistently across sectors.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Belarus Data Protection Centre Issues Guidance on University Website Personal Data Publication
rows.title: Belarus Data Protection Centre Issues Guidance on University Website Personal Data Publication
rows.summary: On 25 April 2026, the National Centre for Personal Data Protection of the Republic of Belarus published guidance on lawful bases and data minimisation for higher education institutions publishing personal data online. The guidance, coordinated with the Ministry of Education, addresses eleven scenarios including enrolment lists, council membership, distinguished graduates and dormitory allocation. For enrolment lists, the Centre identifies Article 6(20) of the 2021 Personal Data Protection Law, read with Article 55(3) of the Education Code, as the legal basis, limiting permissible data to full name, scores and benefits. The Centre determines that benefit entitlement may be noted as 'benefit' without disclosing the underlying sensitive ground such as disability or orphan status.
rows.authority: National Centre for Personal Data Protection (Belarus)
rows.body_name: National Centre for Personal Data Protection (Belarus)

rows.headline: EU EDPB Approves Santander Group Controller BCRs Under Article 47 GDPR
rows.title: EU EDPB Approves Santander Group Controller BCRs Under Article 47 GDPR
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 12/2026, published on 27 April 2026, assessing the Spanish supervisory authority's draft decision to approve Santander Group's Controller Binding Corporate Rules. The EDPB found that the submitted BCRs meet all requirements of Article 47 GDPR and provide appropriate safeguards for international personal data transfers. The Board assessed the BCRs against EDPB Recommendations 1/2022, which supersede earlier Article 29 Working Party documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264. The opinion is confined to the consistency mechanism review under Article 47 and does not address other GDPR obligations or supplementary transfer measures.

rows.headline: UK DSIT Backs Ineffable Intelligence to Build Self-Learning Knowledge AI
rows.title: UK DSIT Backs Ineffable Intelligence to Build Self-Learning Knowledge AI
rows.summary: On 27 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology announced backing for Ineffable Intelligence, a company developing self-learning AI designed to generate new knowledge. The investment was made through the government's Sovereign AI initiative, with co-investment from the British Business Bank. The funding is intended to scale a UK-built AI system capable of producing knowledge-generating breakthroughs. DSIT described the initiative as part of broader efforts to develop sovereign AI capabilities within the United Kingdom.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: Basque Country's AVPD Marks Ten Years of GDPR with Ten Strategic Milestones
rows.title: Basque Country's AVPD Marks Ten Years of GDPR with Ten Strategic Milestones
rows.summary: On 27 April 2026, the Basque Data Protection Authority published a ten-year GDPR retrospective identifying ten strategic milestones for data protection in Euskadi. The authority described its role as a bridge between EU law and Basque territory. Key milestones included reinforcing explicit, informed, and unambiguous consent standards and operationalising data subject rights covering access, rectification, portability, and erasure. The AVPD also reported processing more than 200 rights-protection cases in 2025.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes Recommended DPO Activity-Report Template and Guidance
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Recommended DPO Activity-Report Template and Guidance
rows.summary: On 27 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published guidance and a model template to help data protection officers structure activity reports. The CNIL stated that producing such a report is not a legal obligation under the GDPR but falls within the DPO's role under Article 39. The guidance recommends reports draw on records of processing activities, DPIAs, audit results, breach records and complaints. The CNIL further recommends DPOs present the report formally to senior leadership and share a summary version, including data subject rights request volumes and breach counts, with staff.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Updates Electronic-Voting-by-Correspondence Security Recommendation After Public Consultation
rows.title: France's CNIL Updates Electronic-Voting-by-Correspondence Security Recommendation After Public Consultation
rows.summary: On 24 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published an updated security recommendation for electronic voting by correspondence. The revision followed a public consultation and aims to make requirements clearer and more operational. The recommendation introduces a three-level security typology based on ballot sensitivity. It sets high standards for the security, confidentiality, and integrity of electronic votes. The guidance is addressed to vote organisers and electronic-voting service providers.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Japan's Digital Agency Concludes Ten-Session Regulatory Reform Committee With 98% of Analogue Rules Revised
rows.title: Japan's Digital Agency Concludes Ten-Session Regulatory Reform Committee With 98% of Analogue Rules Revised
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, Japan's Digital Agency convened the final session of its Technology-Based Regulatory Reform Promotion Committee, marking the end of a ten-session programme. The agency reported that 98% of identified national analogue regulatory provisions requiring revision had been completed. Of approximately 10,000 analogue provisions identified at the outset, around 1,000 required technical verification. The agency's Technology Catalogue, spanning seven digital technology categories, reached 218 entries before new submissions closed in February 2026. Approximately 80% of technical verification cases produced practical implementation examples. The Digital Agency also introduced a Digital Legal Examination process to prevent new analogue regulations from emerging.

rows.headline: British Columbia's OIPC Reports 86% Complaint Surge Linked Partly to AI-Generated Files
rows.title: British Columbia's OIPC Reports 86% Complaint Surge Linked Partly to AI-Generated Files
rows.summary: On 24 April 2026, Commissioner Michael Harvey addressed the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services, reporting that the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for British Columbia received close to 3,900 files in the past year. This represented an 86% rise in privacy and access complaints, a 50% increase in review requests, and a 28% increase in reported breaches. The Commissioner attributed part of the surge to individuals using large language model outputs to draft complaints from Freedom of Information responses. The office closed 36% more files than the prior year and introduced an early resolution investigator role, resolving approximately 104 additional files beyond projections.
rows.authority: Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for BC
rows.body_name: Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for BC

rows.headline: Spain's Four Data Protection Authorities Sign Joint Declaration on GDPR's Tenth Anniversary
rows.title: Spain's Four Data Protection Authorities Sign Joint Declaration on GDPR's Tenth Anniversary
rows.summary: On 24 April 2026, Spain's four data protection authorities, AEPD, APDCAT, AVPD, and CTPDA, signed a joint institutional declaration marking ten years of the GDPR. The declaration commits the four bodies to deeper inter-authority cooperation and common enforcement approaches. It also pledges support for data protection officers and efforts to strengthen a privacy culture across Spain. The authorities identified digital transformation, the data economy, and AI as the key challenges shaping their shared agenda going into the next decade.
rows.authority: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.headline: Catalonia's APDCAT Records 1,374 Privacy Complaints in 2025, Up 21% Year on Year
rows.title: Catalonia's APDCAT Records 1,374 Privacy Complaints in 2025, Up 21% Year on Year
rows.summary: On 24 April 2026, Catalonia's Autoritat Catalana de Protecció de Dades published its 2025 complaints statistics, recording 1,374 privacy complaints and rights claims, a 21% increase on 2024 and 123% above 2022 levels. After transferring cases outside its jurisdiction to Spain's AEPD, the authority investigated 837 cases and opened 138 sanctioning procedures. Of 126 resolutions issued, 110 targeted public-sector entities and carried declarations only. The remaining 12 resolutions targeted private-sector entities and imposed combined fines of EUR 409,352. Ten of those 12 sanctioned entities had paid their fines by the time the report was drafted.
rows.authority: Autoritat Catalana de Protecció de Dades
rows.body_name: Autoritat Catalana de Protecció de Dades

rows.headline: UK NCSC and Allies Issue Advisory on China-Linked Covert Network Tactics
rows.title: UK NCSC and Allies Issue Advisory on China-Linked Covert Network Tactics
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre and international partner agencies published a joint advisory on tactics used by China-linked threat actors to conceal malicious cyber activity. The advisory details how these actors operate covert networks to disguise intrusion campaigns. Partner agencies from multiple countries co-signed the guidance. The publication identifies specific attacker techniques linked to efforts to hide the origin and nature of cyber operations attributed to China-linked groups.

rows.headline: Australia's OAIC and eSafety Commissioner Sign MOU on Privacy and Online Safety Coordination
rows.title: Australia's OAIC and eSafety Commissioner Sign MOU on Privacy and Online Safety Coordination
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner and the eSafety Commissioner executed a Memorandum of Understanding formalising cooperation and information sharing between the two regulators. The agreement establishes formal communication pathways on matters where privacy rights and online safety obligations intersect, including age assurance requirements under Australia's online industry codes and Social Media Minimum Age compliance. Australian Information Commissioner Elizabeth Tydd stated the MOU will support Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind's work and assist the OAIC in monitoring emerging online privacy risks. eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant stated the agreement gives formal recognition to privacy rights within the Social Media Minimum Age regime.
rows.authority: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner
rows.body_name: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

rows.headline: EU Agency for Fundamental Rights Joins Council of Europe CDNET Inaugural Meeting in Strasbourg
rows.title: EU Agency for Fundamental Rights Joins Council of Europe CDNET Inaugural Meeting in Strasbourg
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights published a news item confirming its participation in the Council of Europe's Committee on New and Emerging Technologies inaugural meeting, held in Strasbourg from 15 to 17 April 2026. The three-day session included a one-day thematic conference titled 'Algorithmic Age: Human Rights, Democracy and Rule of Law', at which FRA presented findings from its work on online content moderation and hate speech. The remaining two days were dedicated to organising the committee's work. FRA participated within the framework of the EU delegation. The news item reported no specific conclusions or determinations adopted by the committee during the session.

rows.headline: Germany's DSK and Sector Bodies Discuss Deepfake Draft Law and Rising Complaints at Stuttgart Meeting
rows.title: Germany's DSK and Sector Bodies Discuss Deepfake Draft Law and Rising Complaints at Stuttgart Meeting
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI published a press release reporting on a DSK exchange meeting held in Stuttgart on 22 April 2026. Participants included DSK members and representatives from sector-specific supervisory bodies covering broadcasting, the Landesmedienanstalten, and the churches. The meeting addressed the Federal Ministry of Justice's draft law on digital violence, which targets sexualised deepfakes and unauthorised GPS surveillance. Participants noted that GDPR Articles 5 and 9, the Digital Services Act, and the AI Act already cover deepfake creation and distribution. A second agenda item concerned a pronounced rise in complaint volumes recorded across all participating authorities.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Issues Guidance on China-Nexus Compromised-Device Covert Networks
rows.title: UK NCSC Issues Guidance on China-Nexus Compromised-Device Covert Networks
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published updated guidance on defending against covert networks of compromised infrastructure linked to China-nexus threat actors. The guidance details a widespread shift in tactics, techniques and procedures toward the use of compromised devices as operational relay points. It sets out the methods threat actors use to build and exploit these networks and provides technical recommendations for detecting and mitigating associated activity. The publication follows similar advisories from allied cyber authorities on state-linked infrastructure abuse.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Blog Post Asserting Passkeys Exceed Password Security
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Blog Post Asserting Passkeys Exceed Password Security
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post arguing that passkeys are more secure and more usable than traditional password-based authentication methods. The NCSC stated that passkeys are already supported by most modern devices and platforms, lowering the barrier to adoption. The post positioned passkeys as a practical replacement for passwords rather than a theoretical future standard. The NCSC's guidance forms part of its broader effort to improve baseline authentication security across UK organisations and individuals.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on China-Nexus Covert Networks of Compromised Devices
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on China-Nexus Covert Networks of Compromised Devices
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published an executive summary on defending against China-nexus covert networks built from compromised devices. The guidance addresses threat actors who route malicious traffic through hijacked edge devices, including VPNs and remote access infrastructure, to obscure their origin. The NCSC identified these covert networks as a growing operational tool used in state-linked intrusion campaigns. The publication outlines how such networks function, the device categories most commonly targeted, and the indicators organisations can use to detect covert network traffic on their infrastructure.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Urges Consumer Services to Adopt Passkeys as Default Authentication
rows.title: UK NCSC Urges Consumer Services to Adopt Passkeys as Default Authentication
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published guidance recommending that passkeys replace passwords as the default login method for consumer-facing services. The NCSC stated that passkeys are more secure than passwords and offer a simpler user experience. The guidance positions passkeys as the preferred authentication option, arguing that passwords present avoidable security risks. The NCSC called on service providers to make passkeys the default choice rather than an optional alternative, marking a shift in the agency's public authentication recommendations.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on AI Adoption for National Cyber Defence
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Guidance on AI Adoption for National Cyber Defence
rows.summary: On 23 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post outlining its position on adopting artificial intelligence for cyber defence purposes. The NCSC stated that AI adoption will require time, the development of new capabilities, and careful oversight. The post addresses the operational and governance considerations involved in integrating AI into UK cyber defence. The NCSC did not specify a fixed timeline for implementation or detail particular AI systems under evaluation.

rows.headline: Czech NÚKIB Q1 2026 Report Records Over 70 Incidents, Seven Classified Significant
rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Q1 2026 Report Records Over 70 Incidents, Seven Classified Significant
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the Czech National Cybersecurity Authority (NÚKIB) published its Q1 2026 quarterly threat report, recording more than 70 cybersecurity incidents between January and March 2026, the highest count in twelve months. Seven incidents were classified as significant, with the majority affecting state institutions. A surge in DDoS attacks by pro-Russian hacktivist groups drove January figures, while intrusion incidents remained consistent throughout the quarter. NÚKIB confirmed no ransomware attack affected entities subject to higher obligations under the new Cybersecurity Act. The report also flagged a notable March increase in Czech devices compromised by the REDHEBERG botnet, with over 3,200 affected devices recorded at publication.

rows.headline: Germany's Federal Government Adopts Draft Law Requiring Three-Month IP Address Retention
rows.title: Germany's Federal Government Adopts Draft Law Requiring Three-Month IP Address Retention
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the German Federal Government adopted a draft law requiring internet access providers to retain IP address assignment data, including connection time and duration, for three months. The Federal Ministry of the Interior developed the draft with the Justice and Digital Affairs ministries. The ministry stated that providers currently retain such data for only a few days, by which time investigators have typically lost access. The draft excludes content, location, and communication-history data. It also introduces a preservation order mechanism for cases where no suspect has been identified, and re-authorises cell-site querying for offences of considerable significance following a Federal Court of Justice ruling of 10 January 2024.

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Publishes European Study on S-SDLC and DevSecOps Strategic Priorities
rows.title: France's ANSSI Publishes European Study on S-SDLC and DevSecOps Strategic Priorities
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, France's ANSSI published a European-scope study on secure software development lifecycle and DevSecOps practices, designating both as strategic priorities for 2025–2027. The study draws on public and private sector contributions across Europe and cites software supply chain incidents, including SolarWinds and Log4Shell, as key drivers. ANSSI identifies the Cyber Resilience Act, NIS2, and DORA as regulatory frameworks requiring organisations to reassess development security postures. The study flags AI integration into development pipelines as an emerging concern, calling for a dedicated MLSecOps approach. Accompanying deliverables include technical reference materials, a generic risk analysis framework, and organisational roadmaps.

rows.headline: Japan's AISI Publishes Second Edition of Known Attacks on AI Systems Reference
rows.title: Japan's AISI Publishes Second Edition of Known Attacks on AI Systems Reference
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, Japan's AI Safety Institute published the second edition of "Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems," updating a technical reference first released in March 2025. The document catalogues twelve attack types, designated A through L, organised across four attack surfaces: Training, Model, Input, and Output. It maps each attack to impacts spanning confidentiality, integrity, and availability, including model leakage, safeguard bypass, system compromise, and computational waste. The document carries no enforcement mechanism and imposes no legal obligations. It targets developers, operators, and others involved in AI system deployment, and applies beyond large language models to systems incorporating plugins and external knowledge sources.

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Releases 2026-2027 State Cybersecurity Priority Roadmap Under NIS2 Pressure
rows.title: France's ANSSI Releases 2026-2027 State Cybersecurity Priority Roadmap Under NIS2 Pressure
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, France's national cybersecurity agency ANSSI published its 2026-2027 priority cybersecurity roadmap for state administration. ANSSI chose to release the document publicly, citing an elevated national threat environment and sustained incident pressure observed during 2025. The roadmap sets binding priority cybersecurity efforts for French ministries for the two-year period. It aligns directly with France's implementation of the EU NIS 2 Directive (2022/2555), which expands obligations across public-sector entities. ANSSI issues an annual roadmap to fix cybersecurity priorities across the state, and the 2026-2027 edition marks the first to be publicly disclosed in full.

rows.headline: Spain's AEPD Launches Public Research Network on Privacy and Emerging Technologies
rows.title: Spain's AEPD Launches Public Research Network on Privacy and Emerging Technologies
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, Spain's Agencia Española de Protección de Datos launched a public network integrating approximately 100 research groups, initiatives, and projects focused on privacy, data protection, and emerging technologies including AI. The network operates under the AEPD Laboratory programme. The ValgrAI Foundation, established by the Generalitat Valenciana, five Valencian public universities, and business partners, will handle dissemination and coordination. The initiative connects academic and institutional research efforts across Spain to advance understanding of data protection in the context of rapidly developing technologies.
rows.authority: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.headline: Romania's Constitutional Court Voids Civil Servant Spousal Income Disclosure Rules on Privacy Grounds
rows.title: Romania's Constitutional Court Voids Civil Servant Spousal Income Disclosure Rules on Privacy Grounds
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights published a case law reference for Romania's Constitutional Court Decision no. 297. The court found that provisions requiring civil servants to disclose spousal and dependent children's income in asset declarations are unconstitutional. The court accepted that declarants may lack access to a spouse's confidential salary data. It also struck down requirements to publish asset declarations on institutional and National Integrity Agency websites, citing Article 26 of the Romanian Constitution, Article 8 of the ECHR, and Article 7 of the EU Charter. The court referenced the CJEU's judgment of 1 August 2022 in Case C-184/20, which found EU law precludes public disclosure of personal data relating to a declarant's spouse.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Engineers SilentGlass Device to Block Malicious Display-Link Connections
rows.title: UK NCSC Engineers SilentGlass Device to Block Malicious Display-Link Connections
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre announced SilentGlass, a plug-and-play device designed to block unexpected or malicious HDMI and DisplayPort connections. The NCSC describes SilentGlass as a world-first device it engineered specifically to secure vulnerable display links. The device operates by actively intercepting unauthorised connections at the point of the display interface. SilentGlass requires no software installation and connects directly between a source device and a display. The NCSC published the announcement on its official website alongside technical details of the product.

rows.headline: EDPB Adopts Guidelines 1/2026 on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.title: EDPB Adopts Guidelines 1/2026 on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on processing personal data for scientific research, opening them for public consultation until 25 June 2026. The guidelines identify six factors controllers must consider when determining whether activities qualify as scientific research, including methodical approach, ethical standards, and verifiability. The EDPB confirmed that further processing for scientific research is presumed compatible with original collection purposes, removing the Article 6(4) GDPR compatibility test where original legal bases remain appropriate. The guidelines also address broad and dynamic consent, limitations on erasure and objection rights under Articles 17 and 21 GDPR, and allocation of responsibilities in joint research processing.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: EU's ENISA Releases National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework Tool Version 2.0
rows.title: EU's ENISA Releases National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework Tool Version 2.0
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, ENISA published version 2.0 of its National Cybersecurity Assessment Framework (NCAF) tool, directed at EU Member State national authorities and policymakers. The updated tool is designed to help Member States assess existing national cybersecurity capabilities and identify gaps for improvement. ENISA developed the NCAF 2.0 under its mandate in Regulation (EU) 2019/881, the Cybersecurity Act, and links the tool to Member State obligations under Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2), which requires national cybersecurity strategies and baseline capability standards. ENISA states the tool covers both diagnostic evaluation and improvement planning, though specific domains, indicators, and scoring methodology have not yet been published.

rows.headline: Belgium's DPA and UK ICO Sign Cross-Border Data Protection Cooperation MoU
rows.title: Belgium's DPA and UK ICO Sign Cross-Border Data Protection Cooperation MoU
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority and the UK Information Commissioner's Office signed a Memorandum of Understanding at the Privacy Symposium in Venice, Italy. The MoU establishes a framework for cross-border regulatory cooperation in data protection enforcement and administration. It covers consultation on each authority's regulatory activity, information sharing about potential or ongoing investigations affecting both jurisdictions, and support for organisations meeting data protection obligations. The agreement expressly excludes the sharing of personal data. The Belgian DPA noted that building international partnerships forms a key pillar of its 2026 to 2028 strategic plan.

rows.headline: Australia's OAIC Finds InspectRealEstate's 2Apply Platform Breached Privacy Principles on Excessive Data Collection
rows.title: Australia's OAIC Finds InspectRealEstate's 2Apply Platform Breached Privacy Principles on Excessive Data Collection
rows.summary: On 1 April 2026, Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind issued determination [2026] AICmr 24, finding IRE Pty Ltd, trading as InspectRealEstate, breached Australian Privacy Principles 3.2 and 3.5 through its 2Apply rental application platform. The platform collected unnecessary personal information, including prospective renters' gender, citizenship status, and visa expiry dates, in breach of APP 3.2. The Commissioner also found IRE collected data by unfair means under APP 3.5, citing a material power imbalance worsened by the prevailing rental crisis. No financial penalty was imposed. IRE must cease collecting the specified data categories within 60 days and fund an independent privacy review of its collection practices and data retention policies.
rows.authority: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner
rows.body_name: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

rows.headline: Canada's OPC Raises Privacy Concerns Over Bill S-5 Connected Care Act Before Senate
rows.title: Canada's OPC Raises Privacy Concerns Over Bill S-5 Connected Care Act Before Senate
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, Deputy Commissioner Marc Chénier appeared before the Senate Standing Committee on Social Affairs, Science and Technology to present the Office of the Privacy Commissioner's position on Bill S-5, the Connected Care for Canadians Act. The OPC supports interoperability provisions that preserve applicable privacy law protections but recommends the Bill's data-blocking prohibition include a comparable exception, noting the current draft omits one. The OPC also calls for mandatory consultation on future privacy and security regulations, stresses the need for provincial and territorial alignment, and identifies modernising PIPEDA as important for consistent cross-sector protection of health information.

rows.headline: Albania's IDP Finds Gjirokastër Health Data Media Publication Violates Law No. 124/2024
rows.title: Albania's IDP Finds Gjirokastër Health Data Media Publication Violates Law No. 124/2024
rows.summary: On 21 April 2026, Albania's Information and Data Protection Commissioner's Office determined that media and social network publication of health information identifying residents of Gjirokastër constitutes unlawful processing under Law No. 124/2024. The authority found the publications lacked both a legal basis and a clear, overriding public interest, breaching Article 43 of the law and two commissioner instructions on health data and media processing. No financial penalties were announced. The Commissioner's Office called on media outlets, online portals and social network users to delete all related content immediately, warning that non-compliance may result in administrative measures and sanctions.

rows.headline: EU EDPB Writes to Commission Questioning Israel Adequacy Decision Over NGO Data Rules
rows.title: EU EDPB Writes to Commission Questioning Israel Adequacy Decision Over NGO Data Rules
rows.summary: On 21 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board published a letter to Commissioners Michael McGrath and Hadja Lahbib raising concerns about Israel's NGO registration guidelines. The guidelines, issued by Israel's Ministry for Diaspora Affairs and Combating Antisemitism, require international NGOs providing humanitarian aid in Palestinian territories to submit staff CVs, passport copies, marital status data, and children's personal data, plus donor names and exact donation amounts. The EDPB identified potential conflicts with GDPR proportionality, necessity, transparency, Article 6 legal basis requirements, and Chapter V transfer conditions. The Board asked the Commission to clarify whether such data flows fall within Israel's Article 45 adequacy decision and whether the Commission intends to act.

rows.headline: UK FCA Launches Second Cohort of Eight Firms in AI Live Testing Programme
rows.title: UK FCA Launches Second Cohort of Eight Firms in AI Live Testing Programme
rows.summary: On 21 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority announced at UK FinTech Week that eight firms had commenced testing in the second cohort of its AI Live Testing programme. Participating firms include Barclays, Experian, GoCardless, Lloyds Banking Group (Scottish Widows), UBS, and Palindrome. Technical partner Advai, a London-based AI assurance specialist, is delivering the programme alongside the FCA. Use cases under testing span agentic payments, credit score insights, anti-money laundering detection, and Know Your Customer processes. Testing operates under existing FCA regulatory frameworks, with no new AI-specific rules introduced. Testing will conclude by end-2026, and the FCA will publish an evaluation report in Q1 2027.

rows.headline: EU's CERT-EU Warns AI Tools Have Cut Mean Time to Exploit to Negative Seven Days
rows.title: EU's CERT-EU Warns AI Tools Have Cut Mean Time to Exploit to Negative Seven Days
rows.summary: On 21 April 2026, CERT-EU published an assessment finding that AI-powered vulnerability discovery has collapsed mean time to exploit from 63 days in 2018 to an estimated negative seven days, meaning exploitation typically precedes patch availability. The assessment notes that Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview, a non-public cybersecurity model, autonomously discovered thousands of high- and critical-severity vulnerabilities and produced working shell exploits in 181 of several hundred Firefox 147 test attempts. CERT-EU also records that HackerOne suspended its Internet Bug Bounty programme after AI-generated reports overwhelmed triage capacity. In response, CERT-EU states it has built an internal AI-powered penetration testing pipeline and sets out eight defensive actions for Union entities.

rows.headline: EDPB Adopts Guidelines 1/2026 on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.title: EDPB Adopts Guidelines 1/2026 on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.summary: On 21 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on processing personal data for scientific research under the GDPR. The guidelines establish six factors controllers must apply to determine whether activities qualify as scientific research, including methodological approach, ethical standards, and verifiability. Where all six factors are met, processing may be treated as scientific research. Further processing for research is deemed compatible with the original collection purpose, removing the need for a separate compatibility test. Controllers may rely on broad consent where research purposes are not fully known at collection. The Board also adopted Opinion 14/2026 approving updated Europrivacy certification criteria as a European Data Protection Seal under Article 42(5) GDPR. The guidelines are open for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

rows.headline: Czech NÚKIB Launches EU-Funded Project to Build NIS2 Supervisory Capacity
rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Launches EU-Funded Project to Build NIS2 Supervisory Capacity
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the Czech National Cybersecurity Authority (NÚKIB) inaugurated a European Union-funded project to strengthen its institutional capacity for supervising NIS2 compliance. The project is financed through the European Commission's Technical Support Instrument and delivered by the Estonian e-Governance Academy. NÚKIB states that NIS2 implementation expanded the number of regulated entities in the Czech Republic from approximately 400 to around 6,000, a fifteenfold increase. Planned project outputs include sectoral analysis, cybersecurity threat assessments, supply chain security support, change management support, and capacity-building activities. No monetary value for the grant was disclosed in the source.

rows.headline: Czech Republic's NÚKIB Publishes Cloud Computing Catalogue Registration Guidance Document
rows.title: Czech Republic's NÚKIB Publishes Cloud Computing Catalogue Registration Guidance Document
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) published a guidance document titled 'Guide to Evidencing Requirements for Registration of a Cloud Computing Service'. The Guide supports cloud service providers seeking entry into the national cloud computing catalogue under Decree No. 505/2025 Coll., which entered into force on 1 January 2026. NÚKIB states the Guide introduces key terminology, the legal framework, and general principles of the Decree. It also identifies common deficiencies that cause delays in the registration process. Separate documents covering requirements for individual cloud security levels accompany the Guide, all available on NÚKIB's regulatory support materials webpage.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Reprimands Frontex for Sharing Suspect Data with Europol Without Necessity Assessments
rows.title: EU's EDPS Reprimands Frontex for Sharing Suspect Data with Europol Without Necessity Assessments
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor reprimanded Frontex for transmitting personal data of cross-border crime suspects to Europol without the necessity assessments required by Article 90(2)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1896. The EDPS found that Frontex systematically and proactively shared information gathered during debriefing interviews without assessing whether sharing was strictly necessary for Europol to carry out its mandate. No fine was imposed. The EDPS characterised the breach as severe but cited mitigating factors, including Frontex suspending the data sharing in May 2023 and conducting an individual necessity assessment on the single subsequent occasion it shared such data.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Concludes All Ten Europol and Eurojust Prior Consultations Within Deadlines in 2025
rows.title: EU EDPS Concludes All Ten Europol and Eurojust Prior Consultations Within Deadlines in 2025
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published a blog post reporting that all ten prior consultations submitted by Europol and Eurojust in 2025 were concluded within applicable legal deadlines. The EDPS noted that prior consultation is required where a Data Protection Impact Assessment identifies high risk or where proposed processing involving new technologies poses high risk to individuals' rights. Deadlines are six weeks under the Europol Regulation, extendable by one month, and two months under the Eurojust Regulation. The EDPS also noted that the Europol Regulation permits an urgency procedure allowing processing to begin before the standard deadline, provided the EDPS is notified and its subsequent opinion is considered.

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Back Biotech Act Harmonisation but Demand Health Data Safeguards
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Back Biotech Act Harmonisation but Demand Health Data Safeguards
rows.summary: On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act. The two authorities expressed qualified support for harmonising clinical trials regulation but identified several data protection requirements. The opinion calls for the proposal to clarify whether trial sponsors and investigators act as sole or joint controllers under the GDPR. It also requires the mandatory 25-year retention period to apply only to the clinical trial master file, not all personal data. Where the Act provides a legal basis for further processing, it must define purposes and Article 9(2) GDPR safeguards explicitly.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Issues Supervisory Guidance on DPO Roles Under Regulation 2018/1725
rows.title: EU's EDPS Issues Supervisory Guidance on DPO Roles Under Regulation 2018/1725
rows.summary: On 18 December 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published supervisory guidance on the role, position, and tasks of Data Protection Officers in EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The guidance sets out the EDPS interpretation of how the DPO role applies across those entities, referred to collectively as EUIs, to support independent and consistent application of Union data protection law. The EDPS identified the DPO as a key internal safeguard for personal data protection within EUIs. A related EDPS Decision, governing prior EDPS consent for DPO dismissals, is scheduled for publication in the Official Journal of the European Union in early 2026 and enters into force twenty days after publication.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Opinion 7/2026 Urges Safeguards Before Extending CSAM Interim Regulation
rows.title: EU EDPS Opinion 7/2026 Urges Safeguards Before Extending CSAM Interim Regulation
rows.summary: On 17 February 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 7/2026 on the European Commission's proposal to extend Regulation (EU) 2021/1232 until 3 April 2028. The interim regulation permits providers to take voluntary measures to detect and report child sexual abuse material online. The EDPS found that the extension presents an opportunity to remedy existing shortcomings in the regulation. The Opinion calls for greater legal certainty on the lawfulness of personal data processing under Article 6 GDPR and for effective safeguards against general and indiscriminate scanning, consistent with the principles of necessity and proportionality. Supervisor Wojciech Wiewiórowski stated that temporary measures must not bypass fundamental rights. No fine was imposed.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Publishes Right-of-Access Findings From EDPB Coordinated Enforcement Action
rows.title: EU's EDPS Publishes Right-of-Access Findings From EDPB Coordinated Enforcement Action
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published findings from a survey of 63 EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies examining compliance with Article 17 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS identified five key findings. Request volumes were low, with 58 of 63 entities receiving between zero and 25 access requests in 2023. Many entities lacked centralised systems for managing requests. Entities struggled to distinguish access requests from public-document requests and complaints. Identity verification practices sometimes involved excessive processing of sensitive data. Entities faced difficulties balancing data-copy obligations against third-party rights. No financial penalties were imposed. The EDPS stated the findings would inform its future supervisory and enforcement priorities.

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Adopt Joint Opinion on Digital Omnibus GDPR Simplification Proposal
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Adopt Joint Opinion on Digital Omnibus GDPR Simplification Proposal
rows.summary: On 10 February 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 2/2026 on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus proposal. The opinion identifies concerns across proposed amendments to the GDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, and the broader data legislative framework. The EDPB and EDPS raise issues with changes to the definition of personal data, scientific research exemptions, biometric data processing, and AI model development. The authorities find that a proposed legitimate interest basis for AI and new exemptions for residual special-category data processing lack sufficient safeguards. Proposed limitations on access rights under Article 15 and transparency obligations under Article 13 also draw scrutiny.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Backs Targeted EPPO and OLAF VAT Data Access but Flags Legal Regime Gaps
rows.title: EU EDPS Backs Targeted EPPO and OLAF VAT Data Access but Flags Legal Regime Gaps
rows.summary: On 8 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 1/2026 on the European Commission's proposal to amend Regulation (EU) No 904/2010. The proposal would grant EPPO and OLAF direct centralised access to VAT identification numbers and business-to-business transaction data to combat Missing Trader Intra-Community fraud. The EDPS supported limited access as necessary for cross-border investigations but found the proposal insufficiently distinguished between administrative and criminal law enforcement data processing regimes under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS also found that core legal, technical and organisational safeguards must be defined in the primary legislative act, not deferred to implementing measures.

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on GDPR Simplification Measures for Smaller Enterprises
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion on GDPR Simplification Measures for Smaller Enterprises
rows.summary: On 8 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 01/2025 on the European Commission's proposal to amend the GDPR. The Commission's proposal, issued on 21 May 2025, would extend simplification measures to small mid-cap enterprises and modify the Article 30(5) record-keeping derogation, exempting organisations with fewer than 750 employees unless processing poses high risk to data subjects. The EDPB and EDPS welcomed the high-risk carve-out but found that the proposal lacked a fundamental rights impact assessment, which they considered necessary under Article 52 of the EU Charter.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Joins EDPB Fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action on Right to Erasure
rows.title: EU EDPS Joins EDPB Fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action on Right to Erasure
rows.summary: On 7 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor announced its participation in the European Data Protection Board's fourth Coordinated Enforcement Action, which examines how EU institutions apply the right to erasure under Article 19 EUDPR and Article 17 GDPR. The action runs alongside 30 EEA data protection authorities under the Coordinated Enforcement Framework, established in 2022. The EDPS will issue a questionnaire to controllers within EU institutions, drawing on complaints it has received about erasure requests. Findings will be analysed collectively with participating authorities to determine whether further supervisory or enforcement action is warranted. The EDPB will publish a report once the action concludes.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Publishes Revised Generative AI Guidelines for EU Institutions Under Regulation 2018/1725
rows.title: EU's EDPS Publishes Revised Generative AI Guidelines for EU Institutions Under Regulation 2018/1725
rows.summary: On 28 October 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) released updated guidelines on generative AI use by EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The revised guidelines introduce a refined definition of generative AI, a new compliance checklist, and clarified guidance on controller, joint controller, and processor roles. The EDPS also updated its advice on lawful bases, purpose limitation, and data subjects' rights in generative AI contexts. The revision drew on feedback from EU institutions following the original orientations. The EDPS confirmed the guidelines were issued in its capacity as independent data protection supervisor, not as a market surveillance authority under the EU AI Act.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Urges Organisations to Strengthen Defences Against Severe Cyber Threats
rows.title: UK NCSC Urges Organisations to Strengthen Defences Against Severe Cyber Threats
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post calling on organisational leaders to act on the growing severity of the cyber threat landscape. The NCSC described the threat environment as increasingly serious and emphasised the need for collective resilience across UK public and private sectors. The post outlined the importance of senior leadership engagement in cybersecurity decisions. The NCSC directed organisations to its existing guidance and resources as a basis for improving their defensive posture against current and emerging threats.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Publishes AI Risk Management Guidance for EU Institutions
rows.title: EU's EDPS Publishes AI Risk Management Guidance for EU Institutions
rows.summary: On 11 November 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published guidance titled 'Guidance for Risk Management of Artificial Intelligence Systems', aimed at EU institutions and bodies under its oversight. The document identifies categories of common technical vulnerabilities in AI systems and sets out recommendations for addressing those risks within existing data protection management structures. The EDPS links the guidance to Article 33 of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, covering data security and integrity obligations. The document does not carry binding legal force. It forms part of the EDPS's broader AI work programme, which also engages with the EU AI Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Annual Report 2024 Reveals Record 97 Legislative Opinions and New AI Unit
rows.title: EU's EDPS Annual Report 2024 Reveals Record 97 Legislative Opinions and New AI Unit
rows.summary: On 23 April 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published its Annual Report 2024, covering the final year of its 2020 to 2024 mandate under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The EDPS established a dedicated Artificial Intelligence Unit during 2024 and adopted an AI Strategy covering governance, risk management and supervision. The EDPS also assumed a dual role under EU AI legislation, acting as both competent market surveillance authority and notified body for certain high-risk AI systems. The institution issued 97 legislative opinions across areas including health, justice and home affairs, and digital wallets, describing that figure as a record. The EDPS also reported doubling its supervision and enforcement activities during the year.

rows.headline: Spain's Supreme Court Upholds Tax Agency Use of Third-Party Data in Liquidations
rows.title: Spain's Supreme Court Upholds Tax Agency Use of Third-Party Data in Liquidations
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the Spanish Supreme Court issued judgment STS 4096/2025, dismissing an appeal by a third party against the Spanish Data Protection Agency. The case concerned the State Tax Administration Agency's inclusion of X's name, familial relation, and role as company administrator within tax liquidation documents addressed to a separate taxpayer, Y. The Court held that this constituted lawful processing under Article 6(1)(e) GDPR, grounded in the Tax Agency's legal obligation to justify liquidations under Article 102.2(c) of the General Tax Law and the public interest in combating fraud. The Court confirmed the data were adequate, relevant, and limited to what was necessary, and upheld the AEPD's earlier inadmissibility decision in full.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed Return Regulation Citing Rights Assessment Gap
rows.title: EU's EDPS Issues Opinion on Proposed Return Regulation Citing Rights Assessment Gap
rows.summary: On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion EDPS/2025/06 on the European Commission's proposed Regulation establishing a common system for returning third-country nationals staying illegally in the EU. The EDPS found that the proposal requires an in-depth fundamental rights impact assessment before adoption. It also recommended the proposal be aligned with applicable EU data protection legislation and the Pact on Migration and Asylum. Additional recommendations cover disclosure limits on return decisions, data subject rights notifications, a strict necessity test for transfers of criminal conviction data, and explicit conditions governing transfers of children's personal data to countries of return.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Issues Opinion 24/2025 on Proposed EU-US Security Screening Data Framework
rows.title: EU EDPS Issues Opinion 24/2025 on Proposed EU-US Security Screening Data Framework
rows.summary: On 17 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 24/2025 on the European Commission's recommendation to open negotiations with the United States on a personal data sharing framework for security screenings. The EDPS found the proposed arrangement would be the first EU agreement entailing large-scale sharing of personal data, including biometric data such as fingerprints, for border and immigration control by a third country. The EDPS recommended that both the personal and material scope of data sharing be defined as narrowly as possible, and that transfers from EU large-scale justice and home affairs IT systems be strictly excluded.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Publishes Two-Year AI Act Supervisory Strategy for EU Institutions
rows.title: EU's EDPS Publishes Two-Year AI Act Supervisory Strategy for EU Institutions
rows.summary: On 17 March 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published its Compass document, titled 'Towards Trustworthy AI in the EU Public Administration', setting out its supervisory strategy for 2026 and 2027. The EDPS acts as market surveillance authority and notified body for AI systems used or put into service by EU institutions under Article 59 of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689. The document identifies four strategic pillars: ex ante and ex post supervision of AI systems, AI Board participation, an interinstitutional Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence, and international engagement. The EDPS noted the two-year scope reflects anticipated changes from the 2025 Digital Omnibus proposal (COM(2025) 836 final). No fine or sanction is involved.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Issues Binding DPO Dismissal Consent Rules and Independence Guidance
rows.title: EU's EDPS Issues Binding DPO Dismissal Consent Rules and Independence Guidance
rows.summary: On 16 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Decision 01/2026, establishing binding procedural rules requiring EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies to obtain prior EDPS consent before dismissing a data protection officer before the end of their designation term. The decision was published in the Official Journal on 29 January 2026. The EDPS had also issued supervisory guidance on 18 December 2025, clarifying DPO roles, institutional positioning and independence guarantees under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. Supervisor Wojciech Wiewiórowski stated the measures were intended to ensure independent and effective application of EU data protection law.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS and EDPB Oppose Personal Data Definition Changes in Digital Omnibus Regulation
rows.title: EU's EDPS and EDPB Oppose Personal Data Definition Changes in Digital Omnibus Regulation
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board published a joint opinion on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus Regulation proposal. The two authorities oppose amendments to the GDPR definition of personal data, finding the changes exceed technical correction, fail to reflect Court of Justice jurisprudence, and would narrow the concept of personal data while creating legal uncertainty. They urge co-legislators to reject these amendments. The opinion welcomes a higher risk threshold for mandatory breach notifications, extended notification deadlines, common reporting templates, and machine-readable cookie consent mechanisms. The authorities identify areas needing further refinement, including a proposed derogation for incidental processing of sensitive data in artificial intelligence development.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Closes Microsoft 365 Enforcement Against European Commission After Compliance Confirmed
rows.title: EU's EDPS Closes Microsoft 365 Enforcement Against European Commission After Compliance Confirmed
rows.summary: On 11 July 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor formally closed enforcement proceedings against the European Commission over its use of Microsoft 365. The EDPS concluded, by letter, that the Commission had remedied infringements identified in the March 2024 Decision under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725. The Commission addressed three areas: purpose limitation, third-country transfers, and disclosure notifications. The EDPS received a compliance report in December 2024 and the Commission's final letter on 3 July 2025. The closure covers only provisions examined in the 2024 Decision. The Commission has made its updated Inter-Institutional Licensing Agreement with Microsoft available to other EU institutions and bodies.

rows.headline: Belgium's APD Issues Opinion 44/2026 on Draft Brussels Public Road Infrastructure Ordinance
rows.title: Belgium's APD Issues Opinion 44/2026 on Draft Brussels Public Road Infrastructure Ordinance
rows.summary: On 12 March 2026, the Belgian Data Protection Authority (APD) adopted Opinion 44/2026, responding to a request from Minister Elke Van den Brandt submitted on 4 December 2025. The opinion concerns a draft Brussels-Capital Region ordinance on information exchange relating to public road infrastructure installations. The APD identifies three categories of personal data processing under the draft, including within the CICC system, two Paradigm-managed cadastres, and administrative sanction investigations. The APD found that the requestor failed to answer questions about CICC processing scope. It also identified divergences between draft definitions and those in EU Regulation 2024/1309, and noted textual errors impairing legibility, raising concerns under Article 5(1)(a) GDPR.

rows.headline: EU EDPB and EDPS Adopt Joint Opinion 4/2026 on Cybersecurity Act 2 Proposal
rows.title: EU EDPB and EDPS Adopt Joint Opinion 4/2026 on Cybersecurity Act 2 Proposal
rows.summary: On 18 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 4/2026 on the European Commission's proposals for a Cybersecurity Act 2 and amendments to the NIS 2 Directive. The Commission had issued those proposals on 20 January 2026. The opinion finds that cybersecurity measures can both protect and interfere with personal data rights, requiring necessity and proportionality alongside effectiveness. The EDPB and EDPS support the proposed single-entry point for personal data breach notifications under Article 15 CSA2, finding it would reduce administrative burden without diminishing data-subject protection. The opinion recommends that the EDPS be added explicitly as a body with which ENISA must cooperate.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Publishes First Orientations on Generative AI and Regulation 2018/1725 Compliance
rows.title: EU's EDPS Publishes First Orientations on Generative AI and Regulation 2018/1725 Compliance
rows.summary: On 3 June 2024, the European Data Protection Supervisor published its first Orientations on generative AI and personal data protection for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies. The document addresses how personal data may be affected during input, processing and output phases of generative AI use. It covers data minimisation, purpose limitation and accountability obligations under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, the data protection framework applicable to EU institutions rather than the GDPR. The EDPS framed the Orientations as a compliance facilitation tool, noting increased deployment of generative AI tools across EU administration.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Adopts Decision Requiring Interest Representatives to Register Before Senior Staff Meetings
rows.title: EU's EDPS Adopts Decision Requiring Interest Representatives to Register Before Senior Staff Meetings
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published a formal decision requiring that the Supervisor, the Head of the EDPS Secretariat, and all staff holding managerial responsibilities engage exclusively with interest representatives registered in the EU Transparency Register. The decision, adopted on 24 April 2025, defines eligible meetings as scheduled bilateral encounters covering policy or legislation within EDPS competencies. It also requires the EDPS Agenda to publish details of such meetings, including participants and subjects discussed. The EDPS states the measures are intended to strengthen institutional accountability and improve transparency as a Union entity.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Issues Opinion 1/2026 on EPPO and OLAF Access to VAT Databases
rows.title: EU EDPS Issues Opinion 1/2026 on EPPO and OLAF Access to VAT Databases
rows.summary: On 7 January 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 1/2026 on a European Commission proposal to grant the European Public Prosecutor's Office and the European Anti-Fraud Office direct access to VAT information under amended Regulation (EU) No 904/2010. The EDPS found the proposed access necessary to combat Missing Trader Intra-Community fraud but identified gaps in data protection safeguards. The opinion requires the proposal to clearly distinguish administrative from criminal law enforcement processing, define access parameters in the basic act rather than delegated measures, and incorporate purpose limitation, data minimisation and ex-post review mechanisms under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

rows.headline: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion Calling for European Biotech Act Amendments
rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion Calling for European Biotech Act Amendments
rows.summary: On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act. The opinion responds to the Commission's 16 December 2025 proposal establishing a biotechnology and biomanufacturing framework. The authorities identified deficiencies in proposed amendments to the Clinical Trials Regulation, the Food Law Regulation, and the SoHO Regulation. Specific concerns include insufficient legal bases under GDPR Articles 6 and 9, unclear allocation of controller status between sponsors and investigators, inadequate safeguards for genetic data retention, weak Article 32 technical measures, and AI provisions requiring alignment with GDPR principles. The EDPB and EDPS stated that the identified provisions require amendment before adoption.

rows.headline: EU EDPB and EDPS Welcome Proposed GDPR Record-Keeping Threshold Rise to 750 Employees
rows.title: EU EDPB and EDPS Welcome Proposed GDPR Record-Keeping Threshold Rise to 750 Employees
rows.summary: On 9 July 2025, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor published Joint Opinion 01/2025 on the European Commission's proposal to amend Art. 30(5) GDPR. The proposal would raise the record-keeping derogation threshold from 250 to 750 employees and introduce SME and small mid-cap enterprise definitions into Art. 4 GDPR. Both bodies welcomed the administrative burden reduction but requested that co-legislators clarify why 750 employees is preferred over the 500-employee threshold initially considered. They also recommended that Art. 30(5) cross-reference the new Art. 4 definitions and that the term 'organisation' be clarified to exclude public authorities.

rows.headline: EU EDPB and EDPS Back Cybersecurity Act 2 With Data Protection Recommendations
rows.title: EU EDPB and EDPS Back Cybersecurity Act 2 With Data Protection Recommendations
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor jointly adopted an opinion on the European Commission's proposed Cybersecurity Act 2 and targeted NIS2 Directive amendments. The opinion broadly supports strengthening ENISA's role and expanding cybersecurity certification uptake. The EDPB and EDPS recommend adding the EDPS as a permitted requestor of ENISA advice and requiring EDPS consultation before ENISA adopts personal data processing rules. On certification, the opinion calls for clearer alignment between the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework and GDPR requirements. The bodies also support a single-entry point for personal data breach notifications, consistent with their earlier opinion on the Digital Omnibus Regulation.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Completed Ten Prior Consultations for Europol and Eurojust Within Legal Deadlines in 2025
rows.title: EU's EDPS Completed Ten Prior Consultations for Europol and Eurojust Within Legal Deadlines in 2025
rows.summary: On 20 April 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published details of its prior consultation practice covering Europol and Eurojust processing operations in 2025. The EDPS concluded all ten consultations within prescribed timeframes: six weeks under the Europol Regulation, extendable by one month, and two months under the Eurojust Regulation. Consultations are triggered where planned processing poses high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, even after mitigation. Following each review, the EDPS issues a written opinion recommending modifications or safeguards before processing begins. An urgency procedure permits Europol to initiate processing before the standard deadline expires, provided the EDPS is notified and its advice is considered.

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Joint Opinion Urges Retained Safeguards in Digital Omnibus AI Proposal
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Joint Opinion Urges Retained Safeguards in Digital Omnibus AI Proposal
rows.summary: On 21 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's Digital Omnibus on AI proposal. The opinion supports administrative simplification in principle but identifies several risks to fundamental rights protections. The EDPB and EDPS recommend restricting expanded permissions to process special-category data, including health and ethnicity data, for bias correction to narrowly defined high-risk situations. They oppose removing registration requirements for self-assessed non-high-risk AI systems, warning this creates incentives to avoid public scrutiny. They also recommend EDPB observer status at the European Artificial Intelligence Board and a clear separation between the AI Office's supervisory role and the EDPS's independent oversight of EU institutions.

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Issues Opinion on UN Cybercrime Convention Data Transfer Safeguards
rows.title: EU's EDPS Issues Opinion on UN Cybercrime Convention Data Transfer Safeguards
rows.summary: On 4 September 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 23/2025 on two Council proposals to sign and conclude the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime on the EU's behalf. The EDPS found that Member States must verify, case by case, that transfers of personal data to third countries satisfy the conditions in Chapter V of the Law Enforcement Directive. The EDPS noted the heterogeneous fundamental rights protections across UN member states, making individual verification critical. The EDPS also recommended that data protection experts participate in any future reviews of the Convention's text.

rows.headline: EU EDPS Issues Opinion 9/2025 on Proposed Return Regulation, Flagging Migrant Data Risks
rows.title: EU EDPS Issues Opinion 9/2025 on Proposed Return Regulation, Flagging Migrant Data Risks
rows.summary: On 28 May 2025, the European Data Protection Supervisor published Opinion 9/2025 on the European Commission's proposal for a common EU return system for irregular third-country nationals. The EDPS identified data protection risks for migrants, asylum seekers and children, and called for a fundamental rights impact assessment. It recommended aligning Article 24 information rights with existing migration law, restricting limitations on return-decision reasoning to exceptional cases only, adding safeguards for criminal-conviction data transfers to third countries, and specifying conditions for transferring children's personal data. No financial penalty is associated with the opinion.

rows.headline: Italy's Garante Adopts Tracking Pixel Guidelines for Email Communications
rows.title: Italy's Garante Adopts Tracking Pixel Guidelines for Email Communications
rows.summary: On 17 April 2026, Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted guidelines governing the use of tracking pixels in email communications. The Garante issued Provvedimento n. 284, pending publication in the Gazzetta Ufficiale, in plenary session under President Pasquale Stanzione. The guidelines apply to operators deploying tracking pixels in electronic mail and establish legal requirements under the GDPR and the e-Privacy Directive. The Garante characterised tracking pixels as single-pixel transparent images embedded in emails, describing them as particularly invasive due to their concealed nature. The guidelines are framed alongside the Garante's 2021 cookie guidelines and EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 on the technical scope of Article 5(3) of the e-Privacy Directive.
rows.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Update on NHS Cyber Resilience Collaboration and Detection Efforts
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Update on NHS Cyber Resilience Collaboration and Detection Efforts
rows.summary: On 17 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post outlining its work to strengthen cyber resilience across the National Health Service. The NCSC described efforts to reduce cyber risk and improve threat detection across NHS systems. The post highlighted collaboration with NHS organisations as a central element of the approach. The NCSC also noted work aimed at keeping vital health services operational against cyber threats. No specific incidents, statistics, or named partner organisations were cited in the source material.

rows.headline: Italy's Garante Issues Opinion on Telematic Agency Register Platform Decree
rows.title: Italy's Garante Issues Opinion on Telematic Agency Register Platform Decree
rows.summary: On 17 April 2026, Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali issued an opinion, registered as provvedimento No. 232 of 26 March 2026, on a draft decree prepared by the Directorate General for Motorisation of the Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport. The decree governs the telematic platform used for registration in the Electronic Register of Telematic Agencies (ReAT). The platform handles application submissions by telematic agencies seeking inscription in the ReAT, with access provided via SPID level 2 or the Electronic Identity Card. ReAT registration is a necessary condition for access to the Directorate General's automated information systems under Decree No. 200 of 16 June 2025. The opinion was adopted under GDPR Articles 36 and 58.
rows.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.headline: China's TC 260 Opens Public Comment on Draft AI Application Ethics and Safety Guidance
rows.title: China's TC 260 Opens Public Comment on Draft AI Application Ethics and Safety Guidance
rows.summary: On 17 April 2026, China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization published version 1.0 of its draft technical document, 'Guidelines for AI Application Ethics and Safety,' for public consultation. TC 260 designated the document a voluntary technical guidance file rather than a mandatory standard. The draft sets out an ethics and safety framework intended to guide organisations deploying AI applications within China's information security standardisation landscape. Respondents must submit written comments to the TC 260 Secretariat no later than 24:00 on 26 April 2026. The publication follows TC 260's broader AI standardisation programme, which has previously covered large language model data safety and personal information protection in AI systems.
