rows.authority: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.headline: Germany's Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI Warns Medical Practices of Fraudulent Children's Data Requests
rows.title: Germany's Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI Warns Medical Practices of Fraudulent Children's Data Requests
rows.summary: On 17 April 2026, the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI published a warning to medical practices, particularly paediatric practices, about unknown persons posing as researchers to obtain children's medical data. The authority received a report indicating that an association contacted doctor's practices by email, claiming to conduct a research project and requesting photographs from treatment documentation, sometimes accompanied by age, diagnosis or treatment date. Research design documents appeared authentic but contained links to pornographic websites and vague data-processing statements. The LfDI MV states that any practice that transferred data in response must report the incident as a data breach to the relevant supervisory authority and file a police report.

rows.headline: UK's DSIT Tech Secretary Launches Sovereign AI Fund at Wayve
rows.title: UK's DSIT Tech Secretary Launches Sovereign AI Fund at Wayve
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology Liz Kendall launched the Sovereign AI Fund at a speech held at Wayve. Kendall delivered the announcement in her capacity as Tech Secretary, marking a formal introduction of the fund under the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology. The Sovereign AI Fund represents a named government initiative directed at artificial intelligence development within the United Kingdom. No funding figures, eligibility criteria, or operational details were included in the source materials.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: Belgium's CCB Sets 18 April 2026 Compliance Deadline for NIS2 Essential Entities
rows.title: Belgium's CCB Sets 18 April 2026 Compliance Deadline for NIS2 Essential Entities
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published guidance confirming that essential entities face a binding compliance deadline of 18 April 2026 under the Belgian NIS2 Law of 26 April 2024. The CCB outlines three pathways: CyberFundamentals verification, ISO/IEC 27001 certification, or direct inspection. Entities choosing ISO/IEC 27001 must submit their certification scope, Statement of Applicability and most recent internal audit report by 18 April 2026, with full certification required by April 2027. The CCB states that failure to submit complete or timely information may result in administrative measures, financial penalties or further supervisory action.

rows.headline: UK's DSIT Invites EdTech and AI Firms to Develop Safe AI Tutoring Tools
rows.title: UK's DSIT Invites EdTech and AI Firms to Develop Safe AI Tutoring Tools
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology opened a bidding process for EdTech companies and AI laboratories to develop safe, personalised AI tutoring tools. The initiative targets improved learning outcomes for disadvantaged pupils. Companies and labs are invited to submit bids to build the tools, which are designed to be both safe and tailored to individual learners. The department framed the procurement as part of its broader effort to apply AI to educational equity.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: Belgium's CCB Sets 18 April 2026 Deadline for Essential Entities Under NIS2
rows.title: Belgium's CCB Sets 18 April 2026 Deadline for Essential Entities Under NIS2
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) published guidance confirming that 18 April 2026 is a binding compliance deadline for essential entities under the Belgian NIS2 Law of 26 April 2024. Entities must demonstrate active cybersecurity risk management through one of three recognised pathways: CyberFundamentals (CyFun®) verification at Basic or Important level, ISO/IEC 27001 certification with full completion required by April 2027, or direct CCB inspection with self-assessment submission. The CCB stated that failure to submit complete documentation within the deadline may result in administrative measures, financial penalties, or additional supervisory action. No specific fine amounts were published.

rows.headline: EU EDPB Adopts Scientific Research Guidelines and Approves Europrivacy Certification Criteria
rows.title: EU EDPB Adopts Scientific Research Guidelines and Approves Europrivacy Certification Criteria
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on processing personal data for scientific research under the GDPR at its 118th plenary session. The guidelines set out six factors for determining whether processing qualifies as scientific research, including a methodical approach, ethical adherence, verifiability, and independence. The EDPB opened the guidelines for public consultation until 25 June 2026. It clarified that research processing is compatible with original collection purposes under Article 5(1)(b), removing the compatibility test requirement. The EDPB also established a sprint team to finalise anonymisation guidelines by summer 2026 and approved Europrivacy certification criteria as a European data protection seal, one of which may serve as a GDPR data transfer mechanism.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Flags EDPB Public Consultation on Harmonised European DPIA Template
rows.title: France's CNIL Flags EDPB Public Consultation on Harmonised European DPIA Template
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, France's CNIL published a notice confirming that the European Data Protection Board has opened a public consultation on a draft harmonised template for documenting data protection impact assessments under Article 35 GDPR. The draft, developed under the Helsinki Action Plan, comprises a tabular completion template for controllers and a supporting explanatory document. The consultation closes on 9 June 2026. The CNIL confirmed that existing completed DPIAs remain valid and that controllers face no obligation to adopt the new template. The CNIL's own models, PIA software and methodology resources will remain available alongside any template the EDPB finalises.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK DSIT Awards First Sovereign AI Grants to Drug Discovery and Supercomputing Firms
rows.title: UK DSIT Awards First Sovereign AI Grants to Drug Discovery and Supercomputing Firms
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology announced the first recipients of funding through the Sovereign AI programme. The programme carries a total budget of GBP 500 million and is designed to support homegrown AI companies. Initial backing went to firms working in drug discovery and cheaper supercomputing, among other areas. DSIT stated the programme aims to drive economic growth and create jobs across the United Kingdom. The announcement marks the first wave of awards under the initiative since it was established.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: EU Commission Proposes DMA Measures Requiring Google to Share Search Data
rows.title: EU Commission Proposes DMA Measures Requiring Google to Share Search Data
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the European Commission issued preliminary proposed measures to Google under the Digital Markets Act addressing its gatekeeper obligations. The proposals require Google to share search data, including ranking, query, click and view data, with third-party search engines on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms. The measures cover six areas: beneficiary eligibility, including AI chatbots with search functions; data scope; sharing means and frequency; personal data anonymisation; price-setting parameters; and access governance. The Commission opened a public consultation on 17 April 2026 to assess effectiveness. The preliminary findings do not constitute a final non-compliance determination and remain subject to revision.

rows.headline: EU Special Panel on Child Online Safety Holds Second Meeting on Age Verification
rows.title: EU Special Panel on Child Online Safety Holds Second Meeting on Age Verification
rows.summary: On 16 April 2026, the European Commission's Special Panel on child online safety held its second meeting, reviewing existing EU regulatory frameworks and international measures protecting minors online. The panel, which advises Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, examined the Digital Services Act and other EU instruments alongside international approaches, including Australia's minimum age requirement for social media. On 15 April 2026, the Commission presented a finalised EU age verification application, describing it as the most privacy-preserving tool of its kind globally. President von der Leyen stated that Member States are encouraged to begin customising the application for deployment to citizens.

rows.headline: Estonia's AKI Records 68 Breach Notifications in Q1 2026, a 5% Annual Rise
rows.title: Estonia's AKI Records 68 Breach Notifications in Q1 2026, a 5% Annual Rise
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) published its Q1 2026 breach notification statistics, recording 68 notifications between January and March 2026, a 5 percent increase on the same period in 2025. Notifications rose steadily across the quarter: 19 in January, 23 in February, and 26 in March. Thirty-one of the 68 incidents involved public-sector entities. AKI identified information-stealing malware, including RedLine and Vidar, as a significant concealed threat, noting such tools can steal session cookies to bypass multi-factor authentication. AKI also flagged security risks in AI-assisted code generation, citing findings from national cybersecurity authorities that generated code may introduce vulnerabilities developers without software expertise are unlikely to detect.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Finalised Guidance on Storage and Access Technologies
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Finalised Guidance on Storage and Access Technologies
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published its finalised guidance on Storage and Access Technologies (SATs). The guidance sets out the ICO's expectations for how organisations deploy technologies that store or access information on users' devices, including cookies and similar tools. The ICO also released an accompanying update to its online tracking strategy alongside the SATs guidance. The finalised guidance follows an earlier consultation period and represents the ICO's definitive regulatory position on SATs.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns Frontier AI Is Accelerating Vulnerability Discovery by Attackers
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns Frontier AI Is Accelerating Vulnerability Discovery by Attackers
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post warning that frontier AI capabilities are accelerating vulnerability discovery and lowering barriers for cyber attackers. The NCSC stated that offensive actors are gaining AI-enabled advantages in identifying and exploiting weaknesses in digital systems. The centre argued that defenders must raise security baselines to counter this shift in the threat landscape. The post forms part of NCSC's broader effort to communicate how advanced AI models are changing the balance between attack and defence in cyber security.

rows.headline: EU EDPB Adopts Opinion 11/2026 on Kuwait Petroleum Group Controller BCRs
rows.title: EU EDPB Adopts Opinion 11/2026 on Kuwait Petroleum Group Controller BCRs
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 11/2026 under Article 64(1)(f) GDPR, assessing the Belgian supervisory authority's draft decision to approve Kuwait Petroleum Group's Controller Binding Corporate Rules. The BCRs serve as a transfer mechanism for international personal data flows under Article 47 GDPR. The opinion evaluates whether the submitted BCRs meet Article 47(2) requirements and align with EDPB Recommendations 1/2022 on Controller BCRs, adopted June 2023, which supersede Article 29 Working Party documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264. The opinion does not address other GDPR obligations or supplementary measures exporters may need to adopt.

rows.headline: UK DSIT Writes to Business Leaders on AI Cyber Threats
rows.title: UK DSIT Writes to Business Leaders on AI Cyber Threats
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology published an open letter to business leaders on AI-related cyber threats. The letter was co-signed by DSIT Secretary of State Liz Kendall and Security Minister Dan Jarvis. It addressed the growing threat posed by AI-enabled cyber attacks to UK businesses. The publication forms part of the government's broader effort to alert business leaders to emerging risks in the AI and cybersecurity landscape.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: UK FCA Consults on Cryptoasset Perimeter Guidance Ahead of October 2027 Regime
rows.title: UK FCA Consults on Cryptoasset Perimeter Guidance Ahead of October 2027 Regime
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published consultation paper CP26-13, seeking feedback on which cryptoasset activities will fall within the scope of the United Kingdom's forthcoming regulatory regime under SI 2026/102. The FCA identifies five regulated activities: issuing qualifying stablecoins, operating trading platforms, dealing and arranging deals in qualifying cryptoassets, safeguarding cryptoassets, and staking. The consultation closes on 3 June 2026, with a final policy statement due in autumn 2026. The authorisation gateway opens on 30 September 2026, and the full regime comes into force in October 2027. Until then, cryptoassets remain largely unregulated in the United Kingdom except for financial promotions and financial crime purposes.

rows.headline: UK NCSC CEO Warns of 'Perfect Storm' as Nation-State Incidents Rise and AI Scales Threats
rows.title: UK NCSC CEO Warns of 'Perfect Storm' as Nation-State Incidents Rise and AI Scales Threats
rows.summary: On 22 April 2026, NCSC CEO Dr Richard Horne addressed the CYBERUK conference in Glasgow, setting out the centre's assessment of the current threat landscape. The NCSC reported that the majority of nationally significant incidents it handles now originate directly or indirectly from nation states, with Russia applying battlefield lessons from Ukraine to cyber operations. The authority stated that frontier AI is rapidly enabling vulnerability discovery and exploitation at scale. The NCSC also expanded its definition of cyber security to encompass robotics, autonomous systems, and body-integrated technology, describing these as far beyond how the discipline was understood a decade ago.

rows.headline: UK's MHRA Sets Out Strategy for Seizing AI and Technology Opportunities
rows.title: UK's MHRA Sets Out Strategy for Seizing AI and Technology Opportunities
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency published a statement by Dame Jennifer Dixon on how the agency intends to capture growing opportunities presented by artificial intelligence and technology. The piece outlines the MHRA's forward-looking position on integrating AI and technology within its regulatory remit. No specific policy changes, enforcement actions, or binding guidance were announced alongside the statement. The publication reflects the agency's broader interest in positioning itself to respond to technological developments in the medicines and healthcare products sector.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: EU EDPB Adopts Opinion 9/2026 on Dutch SA Approval of Jacobs Douwe Egberts Controller BCRs
rows.title: EU EDPB Adopts Opinion 9/2026 on Dutch SA Approval of Jacobs Douwe Egberts Controller BCRs
rows.summary: On 15 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Opinion 9/2026 under Art. 64(1)(f) GDPR, reviewing the Dutch Supervisory Authority's draft decision approving controller Binding Corporate Rules submitted by the Jacobs Douwe Egberts Group. The opinion assesses whether the BCRs meet all Art. 47 GDPR requirements and EDPB Recommendations 1/2022, which supersede Article 29 Working Party documents WP256 rev.01 and WP264. The scope excludes other GDPR obligations and any supplementary transfer measures. The EDPB recalled that responsibility for compliance with transfer obligations rests with the exporting entity, citing CJEU Case C-311/18. No financial penalties are associated with the opinion.

rows.headline: UK FCA Publishes Open Finance Roadmap Targeting Regulatory Framework by End of 2027
rows.title: UK FCA Publishes Open Finance Roadmap Targeting Regulatory Framework by End of 2027
rows.summary: On 14 April 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority published an open finance roadmap setting out its programme to enable consumers and businesses to share financial data securely across financial services providers. The roadmap identifies SME credit access and mortgage management as priority areas for early exploration. The FCA will consult on a long-term regulatory framework for open banking before end of 2026, separate from the broader open finance framework it must develop with HM Treasury by end of 2027. Industry and consumer engagement will run through 2026 via the FCA's Smart Data Accelerator and PRISM Taskforce. Open banking currently has approximately 17 million users in the United Kingdom.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Launches FantomApp to Teach Teenagers About Social Media Privacy Rights
rows.title: France's CNIL Launches FantomApp to Teach Teenagers About Social Media Privacy Rights
rows.summary: On 14 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés released FantomApp, a digital application aimed at helping adolescents understand their personal data rights on social media platforms. The tool covers GDPR rights of access, rectification and erasure under Articles 15, 16 and 17. The CNIL positions FantomApp alongside existing resources, including its "Cyber Réflexes" guidance on password security and software updates. The release also follows the authority's earlier "Agence Privacy" graphic novel, published in April 2025 under ISBN 978-2-11-179444-3 and licensed under Creative Commons BY-NC-ND 4.0. No financial penalties or enforcement proceedings are associated with this initiative.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes Final Recommendation on Email Tracking Pixels Under Article 82
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Final Recommendation on Email Tracking Pixels Under Article 82
rows.summary: On 14 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its final recommendation on the use of tracking pixels in emails under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act. The recommendation applies to all private and public organisations deploying tracking pixels, and to their technical service providers. It distinguishes between pixels requiring prior consent and those exempt from that requirement, including individual deliverability measurement linked to a service requested by the recipient. The CNIL followed a public consultation launched in June 2025 and consulted professional associations, civil society organisations and data protection authorities before finalising the text.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Fines Mobile Operator EUR 20 Million Across Two Decisions for GDPR Failures
rows.title: France's CNIL Fines Mobile Operator EUR 20 Million Across Two Decisions for GDPR Failures
rows.summary: On 8 January 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés issued two decisions against a French mobile telephony operator. The first imposed a EUR 15 million fine for failures in data retention, data security under Article 32 GDPR, and breach notification to individuals under Article 34 GDPR, accompanied by an injunction. The second imposed a EUR 5 million fine for failures to inform individuals of their rights and non-compliance with the right of access under Article 15 GDPR, also with an injunction. Both injunctions require the operator to bring its processing into compliance.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Issues Final Recommendation on Email Tracking Pixels
rows.title: France's CNIL Issues Final Recommendation on Email Tracking Pixels
rows.summary: On 14 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its final recommendation on tracking pixels, known as pixels espions, in email communications. The recommendation follows a public consultation and sets out that prior consent is generally required under ePrivacy rules and their French transposition. CNIL specifies conditions for valid consent collection, information obligations toward recipients, and technical requirements for pixel deployment. The guidance targets email-marketing senders, transactional message senders, and email platform operators. CNIL had previously issued draft guidance before refining the text through consultation feedback.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Fines NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1.7 Million for Article 32 Security Failures
rows.title: France's CNIL Fines NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1.7 Million for Article 32 Security Failures
rows.summary: On 22 December 2025, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés fined NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1,700,000 (USD 1.9 million) for failing to implement adequate technical and organisational security measures in its PCRM software, breaching Article 32 of the GDPR. Investigations began in late November 2022 after customers reported users accessing third-party documents via the PCRM portal, used by social services bodies including Departmental Houses for the Disabled. The CNIL found vulnerabilities had been flagged in multiple audit reports before the breaches occurred but were corrected only after. The restricted committee treated the failures as aggravated because NEXPUBLICA FRANCE specialises in IT systems and software consulting. The CNIL issued no compliance order, finding the company had taken corrective measures post-breach.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: EU Court of Justice Hears Belgian Blanket Communications-Data Retention Challenge
rows.title: EU Court of Justice Hears Belgian Blanket Communications-Data Retention Challenge
rows.summary: On 14 April 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union heard oral argument in Case C-661/24, Académie Fiscale and Others, a preliminary reference from the Belgian Constitutional Court. The case concerns whether Belgian legislation requiring operators to retain traffic and location data from all electronic communications users is compatible with EU law on privacy and personal data protection. The Belgian law cites fraud prevention, network misuse prevention and network security as justifications. Several professional organisations, fundamental rights associations and individuals initiated the proceedings before the Belgian Constitutional Court, arguing the retention regime is overly broad. No fine is at issue.
rows.authority: General Court of the European Union
rows.body_name: General Court of the European Union

rows.headline: Europol Coordinates Operation PowerOFF Action Week Targeting 75,000 DDoS-for-Hire Users Across 21 Countries
rows.title: Europol Coordinates Operation PowerOFF Action Week Targeting 75,000 DDoS-for-Hire Users Across 21 Countries
rows.summary: On 13 April 2026, Europol announced the conclusion of an Operation PowerOFF coordinated action week targeting distributed denial-of-service-for-hire infrastructure across 21 countries. The operation resulted in 4 arrests, 53 domain takedowns, and 25 search warrants. Europol provided national authorities with data on over 3 million criminal user accounts drawn from seized databases. Prevention measures included targeted search engine advertisements aimed at young people, removal of over 100 URLs advertising DDoS-for-hire services, and warning messages transmitted via blockchains used by criminals to process payments. Participating countries included Australia, Germany, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the United States, among others.
rows.authority: European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation
rows.body_name: European Union Agency for Law Enforcement Cooperation

rows.headline: Philippines NPC Issues Advisory 2026-01 on Scraping Publicly Available Personal Data
rows.title: Philippines NPC Issues Advisory 2026-01 on Scraping Publicly Available Personal Data
rows.summary: On 13 April 2026, the Philippine National Privacy Commission issued Advisory No. 2026-01, setting out guidelines on the data scraping of publicly available personal data. The Advisory clarifies the scope of the Data Privacy Act as it applies to scraping practices. It establishes conditions under which scraping of publicly available personal data may constitute lawful processing. The guidance addresses obligations for entities that collect personal data through automated extraction from public sources. The NPC published the Advisory to provide clarity on compliance expectations under existing Philippine data protection law.

rows.headline: Italy's ACN Sets NIS Compliance Timelines for 2026-Registered Entities and Updates Platform Rules
rows.title: Italy's ACN Sets NIS Compliance Timelines for 2026-Registered Entities and Updates Platform Rules
rows.summary: On 13 April 2026, Italy's Agenzia per la Cybersicurezza Nazionale published two determinations under the NIS framework. Determination 127434/2026 establishes compliance timelines for entities first registered in the NIS register in 2026, requiring CSIRT contact-point designation by end-2026, incident-notification obligations from 1 January 2027, and baseline security measures by July 2027. Determination 127437/2026 updates access terms for the ACN digital platform and introduces Article 18, covering the listing of relevant suppliers to identify those designable as important or essential entities. The determinations were examined at the eighth NIS Roundtable meeting. ACN states no financial penalties attach to either determination.

rows.headline: EU Agency for Fundamental Rights Publishes Reference Page on Article 52 Charter Scope and Rights Interpretation
rows.title: EU Agency for Fundamental Rights Publishes Reference Page on Article 52 Charter Scope and Rights Interpretation
rows.summary: On 10 April 2026, the EU Agency for Fundamental Rights published a dedicated reference page for Article 52 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union. Article 52 governs the scope and permissible limitations of Charter rights and principles. The Charter requires that any limitation be provided for by law, respect the essential content of the right, and satisfy the principle of proportionality. Where Charter rights correspond to European Convention on Human Rights guarantees, their meaning and scope must align with that Convention. The FRA page also addresses the distinction between rights and principles, noting that provisions containing principles may only be invoked before courts to interpret or review the legality of implementing acts.

rows.headline: UK DSIT Proposes Criminal Sanctions for Incest and Age-Disguise Pornography
rows.title: UK DSIT Proposes Criminal Sanctions for Incest and Age-Disguise Pornography
rows.summary: On 10 April 2026, the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology announced proposed legislation to criminalise the possession and publication of pornography depicting incest or adults simulating minors. The measures would also introduce personal liability for technology executives whose platforms host such material. DSIT stated the changes form part of broader efforts to address harmful online content. The proposals were published alongside the government's existing Online Safety Act framework, extending criminal reach to categories of material not previously covered by specific possession offences.
rows.authority: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology
rows.body_name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology

rows.headline: EU Commission Publishes First Monitoring Results Under Revised Hate Speech Code+
rows.title: EU Commission Publishes First Monitoring Results Under Revised Hate Speech Code+
rows.summary: On 10 April 2026, the European Commission published findings from the inaugural monitoring cycle under the revised Code of Conduct on Countering Illegal Hate Speech Online+. The exercise, a mystery-shopping stress-test run from early November to mid-December 2025, assessed five signatory platforms: Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, X, and YouTube. All five met the threshold of reviewing at least 50 percent of notifications within 24 hours and provided systematic feedback to reporters. However, all platforms except X classified a considerable share of cases as disputed or errors, with many errors linked to incorrect reporting channels used by monitoring reporters. Self-assessment reports from all 12 signatories were published alongside the results.

rows.headline: EDPB's 2025 Annual Report Records EUR 1.15 Billion in EU Member State Fines
rows.title: EDPB's 2025 Annual Report Records EUR 1.15 Billion in EU Member State Fines
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board published its annual report covering 2025 activities. EU member state authorities imposed a combined EUR 1.15 billion in fines during the year. The EDPB recorded 414 cross-border cases initiated and 1,299 one-stop-shop procedures under Article 60 GDPR, of which 572 resulted in a final decision. The Board adopted three new guideline sets covering pseudonymisation, blockchain, and DSA-GDPR intersections. It also issued five adequacy-related opinions on the UK, Brazil, and the European Patent Organisation. A central development was the adoption of the Helsinki Declaration, aimed at increasing GDPR clarity and stakeholder cooperation.

rows.headline: China's TC 260 Consults on Draft Blockchain System Security Implementation Standard
rows.title: China's TC 260 Consults on Draft Blockchain System Security Implementation Standard
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization opened a public consultation on a draft national standard titled 'Cybersecurity Technology: Blockchain System Security Implementation Guide.' The draft is versioned as of 23 March 2026 and accompanied by compilation explanatory notes dated 25 March 2026. TC 260 invited relevant organisations and experts across China to submit written comments via its online platform. The designated contact for the process is Zhang Boran at the TC 260 Secretariat, located at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute in Beijing. The consultation closes on 8 June 2026.
rows.authority: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.headline: Andalusia's CTPDA Updates Public-Sector AI Methodology to Address Agentic Systems
rows.title: Andalusia's CTPDA Updates Public-Sector AI Methodology to Address Agentic Systems
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, the Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía updated its public-sector AI data protection methodology to cover agentic AI systems. Unlike assistive AI, agentic systems execute actions with direct effects on citizens. The update builds on AEPD's orientation on agentic AI and has been integrated into the application available to all Andalusian public administrations. One illustrative example involves an agentic social-benefits system that consults municipal census, tax, and employment data and generates a draft determination.
rows.authority: Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía
rows.body_name: Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía

rows.headline: Italy's Konecta Italia and SLC-CGIL Sign First Company-Level AI Agreement Covering 7,000 Workers
rows.title: Italy's Konecta Italia and SLC-CGIL Sign First Company-Level AI Agreement Covering 7,000 Workers
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, Partnership on AI published a case study on Italy's first company-level AI agreement, signed in March 2025 between business process outsourcing firm Konecta Italia and communications union SLC-CGIL. The agreement covers approximately 7,000 employees and establishes safeguards over how worker data is collected and used for AI training. Partnership on AI found that Italy's existing labour framework, including sector-level bargaining provisions and technology negotiation requirements, gave SLC-CGIL meaningful leverage. The parties framed AI as a tool rather than a worker replacement. Partnership on AI noted that longer-term risks, including generative AI and structural job loss, remain difficult to address through collective bargaining alone.

rows.headline: Partnership on AI Case Study Examines Pennsylvania and SEIU Local 668 AI Protections Deal
rows.title: Partnership on AI Case Study Examines Pennsylvania and SEIU Local 668 AI Protections Deal
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, the Partnership on AI published a case study examining a binding agreement between the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania and SEIU Local 668. Reached in March 2025, the agreement established AI protections for nearly 10,000 state employees. Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro issued a letter outlining eight AI protections, while the Governor's Office of Administration negotiated a binding side letter with SEIU Local 668. The agreement created a Generative AI Labour and Management Collaboration Group for ongoing AI governance. The case study identifies seven key insights, including building on established labour-management trust and involving workers as subject matter experts in AI policy development.

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Launches Public Consultation on Security-Supervision Reference Architectures and Technologies
rows.title: France's ANSSI Launches Public Consultation on Security-Supervision Reference Architectures and Technologies
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, France's ANSSI launched a public consultation seeking comments on reference architectures and technologies intended to underpin a new security-supervision doctrine. The consultation targets two distinct groups: beneficiaries of security-supervision services and providers of related products and services. ANSSI will use responses to shape forthcoming guidance documents on security supervision. The agency published the call for comments on its official website, cyber.gouv.fr. No deadline for submissions was stated in the source material. The results are intended to inform the structure and technical content of the doctrine ANSSI is developing.

rows.headline: EU AI Office Publishes Two Reports Marking AI Continent Action Plan Anniversary
rows.title: EU AI Office Publishes Two Reports Marking AI Continent Action Plan Anniversary
rows.summary: On 9 April 2026, the EU AI Office published a report on AI adoption in public administration and a Joint Research Centre Science for Policy brief covering a decade of EU AI policymaking. Both documents mark one year since the European Commission launched its AI Continent Action Plan. Over that year, the Commission reports 19 AI Factories and 13 AI Factory antennas now operational, 76 responses to its AI Gigafactories call across 16 Member States, and an Apply AI Strategy generating calls worth up to EUR 1 billion. The public administration report is structured around three activities: anchoring AI in EU policy, adapting public-sector capabilities, and applying AI in high-impact domains.

rows.headline: EU's CERT-EU Tracks 174 Threat Actors and Nine Significant Incidents in 2025 Report
rows.title: EU's CERT-EU Tracks 174 Threat Actors and Nine Significant Incidents in 2025 Report
rows.summary: On 8 April 2026, CERT-EU published its Threat Landscape Report 2025, covering cyber threats observed across EU institutions throughout the year. The report found that tracked threat actors rose to 174 from 110 in 2024, with five actors posing critical exposure to Union entities. CERT-EU responded to nine significant incidents, seven attributed to vulnerability exploitation including two zero-days. Targeted software products reached 198, an 80% increase on 2024. Cyberespionage and prepositioning accounted for 38% of observed activity. Social engineering expanded beyond email to include voice phishing, AI-generated deepfakes and OAuth abuse. For the first time, the report assessed threats to 90 partner organisations, with public administrations representing 60% of recorded activity.

rows.headline: Poland's Supreme Administrative Court Upholds UODO Order Against Debt Fund for Unlawful Heir Data Processing
rows.title: Poland's Supreme Administrative Court Upholds UODO Order Against Debt Fund for Unlawful Heir Data Processing
rows.summary: On 8 April 2026, Poland's Naczelny Sąd Administracyjny dismissed the cassation appeal of BEST Towarzystwo Funduszy Inwestycyjnych S.A., confirming a 2021 UODO order requiring deletion of a complainant's personal data. UODO had found that BEST S.A. and BEST III NSFIZ lacked a lawful basis under Article 6 GDPR to process the personal data of a deceased debtor's potential heir. The court agreed that a debtor's death does not render descendants automatically liable for that debtor's obligations. UODO also ruled that identifying heirs requires formal court proceedings, not unilateral creditor determinations. No financial penalty was imposed.

rows.headline: EU's HaDEA Publishes Portfolio Overview of AI Projects Across Three Funding Programmes
rows.title: EU's HaDEA Publishes Portfolio Overview of AI Projects Across Three Funding Programmes
rows.summary: On 8 April 2026, the European Health and Digital Executive Agency (HaDEA) published an overview of its AI-related projects funded under Horizon Europe, Digital Europe and EU4Health. The Commission has committed EUR 200 billion to AI development across Europe, including EUR 1 billion annually across those two programmes. Highlighted projects include COMPASS AI, launched in October 2025 under EU4Health, which brings together clinicians, patients and regulators to develop evidence-based guidelines for clinical AI deployment. The concluded Horizon Europe project PRAESIIDIUM used machine learning and electronic health records from over 300,000 patients to predict individual prediabetes risk. HaDEA situates the portfolio within the AI Act, adopted in 2024, and the AI Continent Action Plan, adopted in April 2025.
rows.authority: European Health and Digital Executive Agency
rows.body_name: European Health and Digital Executive Agency

rows.headline: EU's CERT-EU Publishes Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework for Union Institutions
rows.title: EU's CERT-EU Publishes Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework for Union Institutions
rows.summary: On 8 April 2026, CERT-EU published its Cyber Threat Intelligence Framework under TLP:CLEAR classification, making it available to constituents and the public. The framework establishes analytical and operational standards for classifying, assessing, and prioritising malicious cyber activities affecting EU institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies. It introduces core concepts including malicious activities of interest, ecosystem mapping, threat actor levels, tactics, techniques and procedures, and scoring mechanisms for adversaries and mitigation. CERT-EU defines the ecosystem concept to extend threat visibility beyond constituent networks to include countries of operation, sectors, geopolitical events, partners, providers, and shared systems. CERT-EU states the framework aligns with EU cybersecurity regulations, FIRST, NATO, and industry practices, and may evolve in response to regulatory changes and stakeholder feedback.

rows.headline: UK's MHRA Secures GBP 36 Million to Expand AI Airlock Programme
rows.title: UK's MHRA Secures GBP 36 Million to Expand AI Airlock Programme
rows.summary: On 8 April 2026, the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency announced GBP 36 million in funding to expand its AI Airlock programme over three years. The AI Airlock is a regulatory sandbox that allows developers to test AI-powered medical devices under MHRA supervision before market approval. The multi-year funding will increase the programme's capacity to assess AI products entering the UK healthcare market. MHRA first launched the AI Airlock as a pilot initiative to build regulatory frameworks for AI in medicine.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Work Programme Covering AI, Consent and Sector Guidance
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Work Programme Covering AI, Consent and Sector Guidance
rows.summary: On 7 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its indicative work programme for 2026. The CNIL confirmed planned outputs across artificial intelligence governance, cross-domain consent, call centre transcription tools, and social housing. On AI, the authority will finalise workplace and health-sector guidance addressing algorithmic bias, and is preparing to be designated as a market surveillance authority under the EU AI Act. The CNIL will also clarify how GDPR applies to non-anonymous AI models. On consent, the authority will examine conditions for collecting a single consent across multiple sites or media. Separately, the CNIL will produce operational resources for data protection officers, developed with DPO associations. The programme is indicative and subject to change.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Is Exploiting Vulnerable Edge Devices for Cyber Operations
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Is Exploiting Vulnerable Edge Devices for Cyber Operations
rows.summary: On 7 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published an advisory attributing a campaign of router and edge-device exploitation to APT28, a cyber threat group linked to Russian military intelligence. The advisory states that APT28 actors hijacked vulnerable routers to support malicious cyber operations. The NCSC identified the group as operating on behalf of Russia's GRU military intelligence service. The advisory details how compromised edge devices were used as infrastructure to conduct and obscure the group's activities.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Work Programme for GDPR and EU AI Act Compliance Support
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Work Programme for GDPR and EU AI Act Compliance Support
rows.summary: On 7 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its 2026 work programme for professional compliance support. The programme covers both public and private organisations and addresses obligations under GDPR and relevant parts of the EU AI Act, including the AI regulatory impact assessment framework. CNIL developed the programme using a multi-stakeholder consultation methodology, involving sector federations and civil society groups. The authority noted that priorities may be updated throughout the year as circumstances evolve.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Exploits Routers to Conduct DNS Hijacking
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns APT28 Exploits Routers to Conduct DNS Hijacking
rows.summary: On 7 April 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published an alert warning that Russian cyber actor APT28 is exploiting vulnerable routers to hijack DNS. The technique enables adversary-in-the-middle attacks against targeted networks. Attackers use this access to intercept traffic and steal passwords and authentication tokens. The NCSC attributed the activity to APT28, a threat actor widely linked to Russian military intelligence. The advisory urges router operators to review device security to reduce exposure to the identified attack methods.

rows.headline: India's IRDAI Replaces 2023 Cyber Rules With Updated Insurance Sector Guidelines
rows.title: India's IRDAI Replaces 2023 Cyber Rules With Updated Insurance Sector Guidelines
rows.summary: On 6 April 2026, India's Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority issued Circular IRDAI/GA&HR/CIR/MISC/51/4/2026, replacing the 2023 cyber-security guidelines for the insurance sector with effect from FY 2026-27. The revised guidelines introduce mandatory quarterly Insurance Security Risk Management Committee meetings, an IT Steering Committee, and an Independent External Expert on the Risk Management Committee. Insurers must also comply with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act. A tiered exceptions framework is included. Executive Director A. R. Nithiyanantham of the GA&HR division signed the circular.
rows.authority: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India
rows.body_name: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India

rows.headline: Poland's CERT Polska Publishes 2025 Annual Report on Incidents, APT Activity and Fraud
rows.title: Poland's CERT Polska Publishes 2025 Annual Report on Incidents, APT Activity and Fraud
rows.summary: On 3 April 2026, CERT Polska published its Annual Report 2025, covering incident-handling activities and threat intelligence findings across the preceding calendar year. The report also marked the team's 30th anniversary since its 1996 establishment. CERT Polska documented widespread fraud campaigns, mobile malware, ransomware attacks and advanced persistent threat group activity observed during 2025. The report covers operational programmes including the Warning List, SMS fraud countermeasures and coordinated vulnerability disclosure under the #BezpiecznyPrzemysł programme. It also describes CERT Polska's contribution to Poland's Presidency of the Council of the European Union and participation in the Locked Shields 2025 international exercise.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Names Recruitment, Electoral Roll, and Sports Federations as 2026 Priority Enforcement Themes
rows.title: France's CNIL Names Recruitment, Electoral Roll, and Sports Federations as 2026 Priority Enforcement Themes
rows.summary: On 3 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés announced its three priority enforcement themes for 2026: recruitment practices, the Single Electoral Roll (Répertoire Electoral Unique), and sports federations. CNIL conducts several hundred controls per year, with approximately 20% targeting annual priority themes and the remainder driven by complaints, triggers, and current events. The authority also indicated that cybersecurity themes would follow as an additional area of focus.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Records 739 Reports and Four Investigations in 2026 Municipal Elections Observatory
rows.title: France's CNIL Records 739 Reports and Four Investigations in 2026 Municipal Elections Observatory
rows.summary: On 3 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its post-election observatory report covering the March 2026 municipal elections. The CNIL received 739 signalements during the campaign period, with SMS prospecting accounting for 405 reports, or 63 percent of the total. Postal mail generated 98 reports, email 85, telephone canvassing 45, and social media prospecting 9. The authority processed 81 complaints, primarily concerning the origin of personal data used by candidates and suspected détournement de finalité. Four formal investigations were opened alongside one simplified procedure. The CNIL noted an overall decrease in signalement volume compared with the 2020 municipal elections. No fines have been reported at this stage.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes Retention Period Reference Framework for HR Personal Data
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Retention Period Reference Framework for HR Personal Data
rows.summary: On 2 April 2026, France's CNIL published a reference framework setting out retention periods for personal data processed in human resources management. The framework applies to all private and public employers whose personnel are subject to French labour law. It covers recruitment, personnel administration, remuneration, workplace safety, company vehicles, telephone recording, collective labour relations, workplace accidents and litigation. The CNIL classifies the framework as soft law, making general compliance non-mandatory. However, certain retention periods derive directly from the Labour Code, Social Security Code, General Tax Code and other legislation, and those specific periods are legally binding. The framework does not cover retention periods for individual public servant files, which remain governed by the arrêté of 21 December 2012.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes Reference Document on HR Data-Retention Periods
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Reference Document on HR Data-Retention Periods
rows.summary: On 2 April 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published a référentiel setting out data-retention periods for human resources management. The document covers five HR lifecycle areas: recruitment, payroll, training, leave management, and end-of-employment. Retention periods are based on legal requirements, operational needs, and accountability obligations. CNIL developed the référentiel in collaboration with public and private professional networks. The guidance is intended to help organisations determine appropriate retention durations for personal data processed in an HR context, grounding those durations in existing French and EU legal frameworks rather than discretionary organisational choice.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK MHRA and US FDA Deepen Medical Device Regulatory Cooperation
rows.title: UK MHRA and US FDA Deepen Medical Device Regulatory Cooperation
rows.summary: On 2 April 2026, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency and the US Food and Drug Administration announced strengthened cooperation on medical device regulation. The agreement builds on an existing wider pharmaceutical partnership between the two agencies. Both regulators stated the collaboration aims to support faster patient access to safe and innovative medical technologies in the United Kingdom and the United States. The MHRA described the move as an extension of ongoing transatlantic regulatory alignment efforts covering the medical devices sector specifically.
rows.authority: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
rows.body_name: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

rows.headline: Tanzania's PDPC Reminds Mwanza Content Creators of Consent and Registration Duties Under Cap. 44
rows.title: Tanzania's PDPC Reminds Mwanza Content Creators of Consent and Registration Duties Under Cap. 44
rows.summary: On 1 April 2026, Tanzania's Personal Data Protection Commission held an outreach session in Mwanza, addressing online content creators in the Lake Zone region on their obligations under the Personal Data Protection Act, Cap. 44. The Commission's Head of Public Relations, Innocent Mungy, stated that creators must obtain consent before featuring individuals in content and may only use personal data for its originally collected purpose. Covered data includes names, telephone numbers, photographs, locations and financial information. The Commission also reminded creators to register with the PDPC before the voluntary registration deadline of 8 April 2026, warning that non-compliance may result in legal consequences under the Act.

rows.headline: Togo's IPDCP Issues First CCTV Declaration Receipts and Pictograms via Online Portal
rows.title: Togo's IPDCP Issues First CCTV Declaration Receipts and Pictograms via Online Portal
rows.summary: On 1 April 2026, Togo's Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel (IPDCP) processed the first video surveillance declarations submitted through its new online registration platform at service-public.gouv.tg, launched on 27 March 2026. The authority issued official receipts and regulatory pictograms to the initial applicants, comprising one company and two private individuals registering installations for private use. IPDCP President Colonel BELEI Bédiani conducted a formal handover of the documents. The IPDCP requires all data controllers, including public bodies, companies, associations, and private individuals, to register video surveillance systems through the dedicated platform. The pictogram is a mandatory requirement for informing data subjects and ensuring transparency of processing operations.
rows.authority: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel
rows.body_name: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel

rows.headline: Togo's IPDCP Launches Online Declaration Platform for Video Surveillance Devices
rows.title: Togo's IPDCP Launches Online Declaration Platform for Video Surveillance Devices
rows.summary: On 27 March 2026, Togo's Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel (IPDCP) inaugurated an online platform for declaring video surveillance and video protection devices, replacing paper-based procedures. The launch took place at the Hôtel Sarakawa in Lomé and was attended by IPDCP President Colonel Bédiani Beléi and a representative of Minister Cina Lawson. The platform is designed to streamline declaration procedures, reduce processing times, and increase file traceability. The launch included a practical demonstration covering the online declaration steps and introduced two compliance elements: a declaration receipt and a regulatory pictogram. No financial penalties are associated with this measure.
rows.authority: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel
rows.body_name: Instance de Protection des Données à Caractère Personnel

rows.headline: UK CMA Announces Remedies Package for Business Software and Cloud Services Markets
rows.title: UK CMA Announces Remedies Package for Business Software and Cloud Services Markets
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the UK Competition and Markets Authority announced a package of actions targeting business software and cloud services markets. The CMA stated the measures aim to create greater choice for UK businesses and public sector organisations. The announcement follows the authority's review of competition conditions in these sectors. The CMA identified concerns about market dynamics at a time of significant advances in artificial intelligence. The package of actions covers both business software and cloud services, though specific remedies and timelines were not detailed in the source material.

rows.headline: US NIST Schedules AI Incident Management Workshop for May 2026
rows.title: US NIST Schedules AI Incident Management Workshop for May 2026
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology published notice of a one-day workshop on AI incident management, scheduled for 14 May 2026 at its Gaithersburg, Maryland campus. The event will bring together AI developers, service providers, incident responders, critical infrastructure partners, academics, and government stakeholders. NIST stated that the agenda will cover a roadmap for AI incident response standards, stakeholder review of existing playbooks, definitions and taxonomies for AI-related incidents, and gaps in current guidance. Outcomes will inform future work by NIST's Information Technology Laboratory and Centre for AI Standards and Innovation, including updates to existing guidelines and new recommendations under America's AI Action Plan.
rows.authority: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation
rows.body_name: US Center for AI Standards and Innovation

rows.headline: Estonia's AKI Reports Record Complaints and Apotheka GDPR Fine in 2025 Annual Review
rows.title: Estonia's AKI Reports Record Complaints and Apotheka GDPR Fine in 2025 Annual Review
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) published its annual report for 2025, recording a record number of complaints received during the year. The Apotheka customer data breach case, which concluded in 2025, was resolved out of court with a record-level financial penalty under Article 32 GDPR, though AKI did not specify the exact amount. AKI identified key supervisory themes including employee monitoring and sensitive data exposure in local authority document registers. The Inspectorate also expanded preventive activity, organising regional training and directing attention to artificial intelligence use in schools.

rows.headline: US NIST Schedules May 2026 Workshop on AI Incident Management Standards
rows.title: US NIST Schedules May 2026 Workshop on AI Incident Management Standards
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the National Institute of Standards and Technology published details of a workshop on AI incident management, scheduled for 14 May 2026 at its Gaithersburg campus. The event will run from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm EDT and will convene stakeholders from government, industry, academia and the cybersecurity community. NIST states that the agenda will cover incident definitions, taxonomies and lifecycles, gaps in existing risk management guidance, and misuse scenarios beyond cybersecurity. Outcomes will inform future work by the Information Technology Laboratory and the Center for AI Standards and Innovation, including updates to existing guidelines, as part of implementing America's AI Action Plan.
rows.authority: National Institute of Standards and Technology
rows.body_name: National Institute of Standards and Technology

rows.headline: China's TC260 Consults on Security Guidelines for OpenClaw-Class Agent Deployment
rows.title: China's TC260 Consults on Security Guidelines for OpenClaw-Class Agent Deployment
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC260) issued notice Wangan Mi Zi [2026] No. 33, opening a public consultation on a draft Cybersecurity Standards Practice Guide covering the deployment and use of OpenClaw-class intelligent agents. TC260 states the guide was compiled to address security risks arising from OpenClaw-class agent deployment and to improve management capabilities of deploying organisations. The consultation closes on 15 April 2026. Stakeholders must submit comments to the TC260 Secretariat, with Wang Hansheng listed as the designated contact. The draft guide is available as a downloadable attachment on the TC260 portal.
rows.authority: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns High-Risk Individuals of Messaging App Targeting Threat
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns High-Risk Individuals of Messaging App Targeting Threat
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre issued a warning about targeted attacks against messaging apps affecting high-risk individuals. The NCSC published specific actions for people it considers at elevated risk from such attacks. The guidance addresses threats directed at messaging applications used by individuals who may be subject to state-sponsored or other sophisticated targeting. The NCSC urged those in at-risk categories to review and apply the recommended steps to reduce their exposure.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Revised Automated Decision-Making Guidance Under DUA Act 2025
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Revised Automated Decision-Making Guidance Under DUA Act 2025
rows.summary: On 31 March 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office published updated guidance on automated decision-making and profiling, reflecting amendments to the UK GDPR introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025. The revised guidance consolidates obligations under the newly inserted articles 22A to 22D, which govern solely automated decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects on individuals. New sections address when restrictions on ADM apply, required conditions, mandatory safeguards, and individual rights. The ICO states the guidance will inform a forthcoming statutory code of practice on artificial intelligence and ADM. Previous guidance remains available as a PDF pending finalisation of a concurrent consultation.

rows.headline: EU's BEREC Flags Centralisation and Legal Uncertainty Risks in Digital Networks Act Proposal
rows.title: EU's BEREC Flags Centralisation and Legal Uncertainty Risks in Digital Networks Act Proposal
rows.summary: On 30 March 2026, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications published its early assessment of the European Commission's proposed Digital Networks Act. BEREC welcomed the proposal's updated objectives but identified concerns across several regulatory domains. The Single Passport mechanism, which concentrates enforcement in the Member State of first notification, risks forum shopping and uneven supervisory burdens. On spectrum management, BEREC found that a proposed Commission veto represents a trend toward centralisation. BEREC also flagged that unlimited licence durations and automatic renewals could favour existing rights holders. Regarding end-user protections, BEREC warned that narrowing their scope risks consumer rights, accessibility and affordability.
rows.authority: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.headline: UK NCSC Warns Cyber Defenders to Prepare for Frontier AI Threats
rows.title: UK NCSC Warns Cyber Defenders to Prepare for Frontier AI Threats
rows.summary: On 30 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre published a blog post warning that frontier AI is reshaping the cyber threat landscape. The NCSC stated that adversaries are already exploring frontier AI capabilities to enhance attacks, and that defenders must understand these developments to stay ahead. The post identified frontier AI as a material risk to existing cyber defences. The NCSC called on cyber defenders to monitor how frontier AI evolves and to factor its capabilities into threat assessments and defensive planning.

rows.headline: Greece's National Cyber Security Authority Joins ENISA Cyber Europe 2026 Transport Infrastructure Exercise
rows.title: Greece's National Cyber Security Authority Joins ENISA Cyber Europe 2026 Transport Infrastructure Exercise
rows.summary: On 27 March 2026, Greece's National Cyber Security Authority announced its participation in the design and delivery of Cyber Europe 2026, a two-day pan-European cyber crisis exercise scheduled for June 2026. Organised by ENISA in collaboration with national cybersecurity authorities across EU member states, the exercise will focus on cyber crisis scenarios affecting transport sector critical infrastructure, including ports and railway networks. The authority stated that its involvement aims to strengthen collective EU capacity to prevent, detect and respond to complex cyber threats, and to reinforce protection of critical infrastructure at both national and EU level.

rows.headline: Estonia's AKI Opens Own-Initiative Proceedings Over ettevaatust.ee Personal Data Publication
rows.title: Estonia's AKI Opens Own-Initiative Proceedings Over ettevaatust.ee Personal Data Publication
rows.summary: On 27 March 2026, Estonia's Data Protection Inspectorate (AKI) announced it had opened own-initiative supervisory proceedings concerning ettevaatust.ee, a website that publishes personal data alongside references to court judgments. No fine has been issued at this stage. AKI will address all potential steps collectively in respect of the site as a whole and will not open separate proceedings for each individual complaint received. The authority directed affected individuals to contact the website operator directly in the first instance, as the operator bears responsibility under Articles 12, 16, 17, and 77 GDPR to explain the legal basis for processing and to respond to data subject requests within one month.

rows.headline: Germany's LfDI MV and LKA MV Warn BDH-Klinik Greifswald Patients of Extortion Emails
rows.title: Germany's LfDI MV and LKA MV Warn BDH-Klinik Greifswald Patients of Extortion Emails
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, the LfDI Mecklenburg-Vorpommern and LKA Mecklenburg-Vorpommern published a joint advisory confirming that patient data stolen in the February 2026 cyber attack on BDH-Klinik Greifswald is being used to extort former patients via email, telephone and messenger services. The authorities stated that a large volume of patient data has already been published online. They warned that making payments provides no guarantee of deletion and typically prompts further demands. The advisory directed affected individuals to avoid responding to or paying extortion communications, not to open attachments or links, and to report attempts via the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern police online portal at portal.onlinewache.polizei.de.

rows.headline: China's TC 260 Launches AI Safety and International Cybersecurity Standards Working Groups
rows.title: China's TC 260 Launches AI Safety and International Cybersecurity Standards Working Groups
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization issued notice 20260327-网安字【2026】6号, formally constituting two new subordinate bodies. The AI Safety Standards Working Group (WG9) is mandated to survey AI safety standardisation trends, develop a standards framework, and draft AI safety standards. Zhou Bowen, Director of the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, was designated WG9 chair. The Cybersecurity International Standards Study Group (SG1) is tasked with tracking international cybersecurity standardisation and developing China's proposals for international standards. Lin Ning of the China Electronics Standardisation Institute was designated SG1 chair. Both groups took effect on 27 March 2026.
rows.authority: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.headline: Belgium's CCB Reports 70% Rise in Incident Notifications and Five-Day Exploit Windows in 2025 Review
rows.title: Belgium's CCB Reports 70% Rise in Incident Notifications and Five-Day Exploit Windows in 2025 Review
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published its annual cyber threat review covering 2025. The CCB recorded 635 total incident notifications, of which 556 were cyber-related, a 58% year-on-year increase. Account compromise was the most reported incident type, with 144 cases. The Safeonweb BePhish platform processed approximately 10 million suspicious emails. Ransomware volumes remained stable at 105 cases, down from 109 in 2024, though attackers escalated to combined encryption, exfiltration and extortion tactics. The average time-to-exploit fell to five days, with one-third of vulnerabilities exploited within 24 hours. The CCB conducted 103 emergency response operations and issued 32,005 spear warnings, a 42% increase on the prior year.

rows.headline: Italy's Garante Reprimands Municipality for Unlawful Sick-Leave Certificate Disclosure
rows.title: Italy's Garante Reprimands Municipality for Unlawful Sick-Leave Certificate Disclosure
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali issued a reprimand against an Italian municipality under decision no. 201, finding it had unlawfully disclosed its secretary-general's electronic sick-leave certificate to external recipients. The municipality attached the certificate to a formal request sent to the Prefettura and a second municipality's secretary seeking authorisation for a temporary replacement. The Garante found no adequate legal basis for transmitting health data to those recipients and no basis under Art. 9 GDPR for processing it at all. Internal access by a protocol officer and secretariat staff compounded the breach of Arts. 5(1)(a), 5(1)(c), 6 and 9 GDPR.
rows.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.headline: Italy's Garante Fines Fix It S.R.L.S. for Unlawful Marketing Emails and Access Request Failure
rows.title: Italy's Garante Fines Fix It S.R.L.S. for Unlawful Marketing Emails and Access Request Failure
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted Decision No. 211 against Fix It S.r.l.s., operator of the megamta.com platform. The authority found the company sent unsolicited commercial emails without demonstrating a lawful basis for processing, breaching Articles 5(1)(a), 5(2), and 6 GDPR and Article 130 of the Italian Privacy Code. The Garante rejected the company's claim that its platform only contacts consenting recipients, finding no specific evidence that the complainant's consent was obtained. The authority separately found Fix It S.r.l.s. failed to respond to a data subject access request submitted on 30 July 2025, breaching Articles 12 and 15 GDPR.
rows.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.headline: Tanzania's PDPC Sets 8 April 2026 Hard Deadline for Mandatory Data-Controller Registration
rows.title: Tanzania's PDPC Sets 8 April 2026 Hard Deadline for Mandatory Data-Controller Registration
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, Tanzania's Personal Data Protection Commission issued a final public reminder that the voluntary registration window for all data-collecting entities closes on 8 April 2026. Enforcement under the Personal Data Protection Act (Cap. 44) begins 9 April 2026. The deadline was extended twice, with the current date set by Communications Minister Angellah Kairuki. Penalties are tiered: individuals face fines up to USD 7,600 or ten years imprisonment, while institutions face fines ranging from USD 380 to USD 1.9 million. The law covers government ministries, banks, hospitals, universities, telecoms, political parties, and NGOs, among other sectors.

rows.headline: Italy's Garante Orders Facebook Removal of Criminal Decree, Finds Unlawful Judicial Data Processing
rows.title: Italy's Garante Orders Facebook Removal of Criminal Decree, Finds Unlawful Judicial Data Processing
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali issued Provvedimento n. 215, ordering Nicolò Cristaldi to remove a criminal penalty decree he had posted on his personal Facebook profile. The Garante found the publication breached the principles of lawfulness, fairness and data minimisation under Article 5(1)(a) and (c) GDPR, as well as Article 137 of the Italian Privacy Code and Article 6 of the Deontological Rules for journalistic processing. Cristaldi had argued the post was a lawful exercise of the right to report on matters of public interest. The Garante rejected that defence, finding the content exceeded what the essentiality principle permits in journalistic activity. The authority also found that Cristaldi's failure to respond to an Article 157 information request constituted a separate administrative infringement.
rows.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.headline: Italy's Garante Reprimands Il Gazzettino for Failing to Answer Rights Request
rows.title: Italy's Garante Reprimands Il Gazzettino for Failing to Answer Rights Request
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, Italy's Garante issued a reprimand against Il Gazzettino S.p.A. under Article 58(2)(b) GDPR for failing to respond to a data subject's rights request within the one-month period required by Article 12(3) GDPR. The complainant, a general practitioner, had asked the publisher to address its use of her full name in an article linking her to a refusal to treat two patients. Il Gazzettino attributed the delay to the complainant using a certified email address rather than its dedicated privacy channel, summer holidays, and the illness and death of the responsible journalist. The Garante rejected these as sufficient justification. Following the complaint, the publisher removed the complainant's name from the digital archive and subsequently took the article offline entirely. No financial penalty was imposed.
rows.authority: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.headline: EU Court of Justice Rules Victims' Rights Directive Does Not Apply to Crimes Committed Outside the EU
rows.title: EU Court of Justice Rules Victims' Rights Directive Does Not Apply to Crimes Committed Outside the EU
rows.summary: On 26 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union ruled in Case C-239/24 (Aurnois) that Directive 2012/29/EU on victims' rights does not apply where the underlying criminal offence occurred outside EU territory. The Third Chamber responded to a preliminary ruling request from the Brussels Court of Appeal, filed in December 2023. The case involved Belgian proceedings brought by family members of a Saudi women's rights activist against Saudi leaders for alleged crimes against humanity. The Court found that the Directive's legal basis under Article 82(2) TFEU does not extend EU victim-protection standards to Member State proceedings exercising extraterritorial jurisdiction over non-EU offences.

rows.headline: Germany's Datenschutzkonferenz Publishes Version 2.0 Tenant Data Collection Guidance for Landlords
rows.title: Germany's Datenschutzkonferenz Publishes Version 2.0 Tenant Data Collection Guidance for Landlords
rows.summary: On 24 March 2026, the Datenschutzkonferenz released Version 2.0 of its joint guidance on personal data collection from prospective tenants, coordinated by LDI NRW. The ten-page document applies nationally across all German jurisdictions. It sets out permissible data collection by rental stage: landlords may not request financial information at viewings, may ask about insolvency proceedings once an applicant is under active consideration, and may request creditworthiness evidence only after selecting a tenant, limited to what is strictly necessary under Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR. Comprehensive credit-reference reports containing surplus information may not be demanded. The guidance also specifies conditions under which collected data must be erased.

rows.headline: UK NCSC Publishes Cloud-Hosted SCADA Guidance for Operational Technology Organisations
rows.title: UK NCSC Publishes Cloud-Hosted SCADA Guidance for Operational Technology Organisations
rows.summary: On 24 March 2026, the UK National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) published guidance on migrating SCADA systems to cloud environments. The guidance targets operational technology organisations, including critical national infrastructure operators. It identifies three assessment areas organisations must address before migration: business drivers and cloud opportunities, organisational readiness, and technology and cloud solution suitability. The NCSC noted a clear industry shift toward cloud adoption for industrial applications, where such adoption was previously dismissed due to perceived risk. The guidance was developed with input from industry bodies, CNI operators, and SCADA vendors, and is intended for use alongside existing NCSC cloud security guidance.

rows.headline: Spain's AEPD Publishes Updated Guidance on Article 33 GDPR Breach Notification
rows.title: Spain's AEPD Publishes Updated Guidance on Article 33 GDPR Breach Notification
rows.summary: On 24 March 2026, the Agencia Española de Protección de Datos published updated guidance on personal data breach notification requirements under Article 33 GDPR. The guidance sets out the 72-hour notification deadline, running from the moment a controller becomes aware of a breach. The AEPD is competent for controllers whose sole or main EU establishment is in Spain, and for non-EU controllers whose affected individuals are located in Spain. Notifications must be submitted electronically via the Sede Electrónica. Where a controller determines no risk exists, documentation of the breach remains obligatory. Failure to notify where the obligation applies constitutes an infringement under the GDPR.
rows.authority: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.headline: Spain's Four Data Protection Authorities Issue Joint Decalogue for Educational Platforms
rows.title: Spain's Four Data Protection Authorities Issue Joint Decalogue for Educational Platforms
rows.summary: On 23 March 2026, Spain's AEPD, APDCAT, AVPD, and CTPDA jointly published a decalogue of ten principles for personal data protection on digital educational platforms. The document targets education administrations, schools, and EdTech vendors. Authorities cited the large volumes of minors' personal data processed on these platforms and their non-optional use within educational activity as key justifications. The decalogue takes a preventive approach and applies across all autonomous communities in Spain.
rows.authority: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Consults on Reference Architectures for Security Supervision Doctrine
rows.title: France's ANSSI Consults on Reference Architectures for Security Supervision Doctrine
