rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, France's ANSSI launched a public call for comments on reference architectures underpinning its forthcoming security supervision doctrine, with responses due by 7 May 2026. The consultation targets four respondent categories: beneficiaries of ANSSI support such as operators of vital importance and ministries, security supervision service providers including PDIS and MSSP operators, hardware and software solution editors, and other experts. ANSSI is drafting two technical guides, titled 'Architectures and Deployment' and 'Technologies and Implementation', which will form the basis of a new PDIS qualification framework. The agency confirmed the consultation does not affect existing regulation or the current PDIS v2.0 framework.

rows.headline: EU's CJEU Rules Article 15 Access Requests Made to Engineer Compensation Claims May Be Refused as Abusive
rows.title: EU's CJEU Rules Article 15 Access Requests Made to Engineer Compensation Claims May Be Refused as Abusive
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-526/24 (Brillen Rottler), ruling that a first GDPR Article 15 access request may be refused as abusive under Article 12(5) where the controller demonstrates the request was made to artificially create conditions for an Article 82 compensation claim rather than to verify lawful processing. The Court identified relevant factors including whether the data subject voluntarily provided personal data, the time elapsed before submitting the request, and a pattern of requests to multiple controllers followed by compensation claims. The Court also held that Article 82 compensation requires proof of actual damage and cannot be awarded where the data subject's own conduct caused that damage.

rows.headline: EU's CJEU Rules Controllers Cannot Refuse a First GDPR Access Request as Excessive
rows.title: EU's CJEU Rules Controllers Cannot Refuse a First GDPR Access Request as Excessive
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union ruled in Case C-526/24 that a controller cannot refuse an initial Article 15 GDPR access request as excessive under Article 12(5) GDPR. The Court held that an "excessive" request presupposes repetitive character or manifest bad faith, neither of which a single first request can establish. The controller bears the burden of demonstrating that threshold is met. The Court also held that unlawful refusal may give rise to non-material damage in the form of loss of control over personal data, though data subjects must demonstrate actual damage suffered, as compensation under Article 82(1) GDPR is compensatory rather than punitive.

rows.headline: CJEU Advocate General Finds EU Law Permits German Refund Claims Against Unlicensed Tipico
rows.title: CJEU Advocate General Finds EU Law Permits German Refund Claims Against Unlicensed Tipico
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union published Advocate General Nicholas Emiliou's Opinion in Case C-530/24 (Tipico). The Opinion finds that EU law does not preclude Germany from applying civil-law consequences, including contract nullity and stake refunds, against a sports betting operator that provided services without a German licence. Tipico held a Maltese licence but not a German one while accepting bets between 2013 and October 2020. The Advocate General identified one exception: where national authorities gave an operator precise, unconditional and consistent assurances that the licence requirement would not be enforced, imposing civil-law consequences would be disproportionate. The Opinion is not binding on the Court.

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Back Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS2 Amendment Proposals with Conditions
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Back Cybersecurity Act 2 and NIS2 Amendment Proposals with Conditions
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor adopted a Joint Opinion on the European Commission's proposed Cybersecurity Act 2 and targeted NIS2 amendments. The two bodies expressed support for ENISA's reinforced role, cybersecurity certification uptake, and ICT supply-chain risk measures. They recommended that the EDPS be designated as a requestor of ENISA cybersecurity advice alongside the EDPB. The opinion also called for clarification of the European Cybersecurity Certification Framework's relationship with the GDPR and welcomed a single-entry point for personal data breach notifications, citing reduced administrative burden for notifying organisations.

rows.headline: EU CJEU Advocate General Proposes Member States May Ban High-Risk Telecoms Vendors
rows.title: EU CJEU Advocate General Proposes Member States May Ban High-Risk Telecoms Vendors
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, Advocate General Tamara Ćapeta issued an Opinion in Case C-354/24 (Elisa Eesti) proposing that EU Member States may exclude telecoms hardware and software from manufacturers classified as national security risks. The case arose after Estonian authorities refused Elisa Eesti AS authorisation to deploy Huawei equipment in its 2G-5G networks in 2022. The Opinion proposes that exclusions must be subject to judicial review and proportionality assessment, and cannot rest on general suspicion alone. Authorities may draw on EU-level risk assessments, including the Commission's 2023 5G Cybersecurity Toolbox Communication. The Opinion is not binding on the Court of Justice, which will deliver judgment at a later date.

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Deepens NIS2 Support Programme and Launches ReCyF Cyber Reference Framework
rows.title: France's ANSSI Deepens NIS2 Support Programme and Launches ReCyF Cyber Reference Framework
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, France's ANSSI convened institutional, sectoral, and public actors at Campus Cyber to strengthen its NIS2 implementation support programme. ANSSI presented ReCyF, the Référentiel Cyber France, a new reference framework setting out security measures recommended by the authority to meet NIS2 security objectives. The framework also provides guidance on implementing good practices. The event marked a continued effort by ANSSI to deepen coordination across public and private stakeholders as France advances its national NIS2 rollout.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Joins EDPB 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Action on GDPR Transparency Obligations
rows.title: France's CNIL Joins EDPB 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Action on GDPR Transparency Obligations
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board launched its 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action, directing 25 data protection authorities, including France's CNIL, to assess controller compliance with Articles 12, 13 and 14 of the GDPR. The selected topic, transparency and information obligations, requires controllers to inform individuals when their personal data is processed. Participating authorities will contact controllers across multiple sectors through enforcement actions or fact-finding exercises. In the second half of 2026, authorities will share findings collectively. The EDPB will then publish a consolidated report to enable follow-up actions at national and EU levels. No fines have been issued at this stage.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's CNIL Coordinates EDPB 2026 Enforcement Action on GDPR Transparency Obligations
rows.title: France's CNIL Coordinates EDPB 2026 Enforcement Action on GDPR Transparency Obligations
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, France's CNIL published details of the European Data Protection Board's 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action, which focuses on controllers' obligations to inform data subjects under Articles 12, 13 and 14 of the GDPR. The CNIL will serve as coordinator for the action, in which 25 European data protection authorities will participate. Participating authorities will contact controllers across various sectors by questionnaire or formal investigation. No financial penalties have been issued at this stage. In the second half of 2026, authorities will exchange findings and prepare a consolidated report for EDPB adoption, enabling follow-up at national and EU level.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Reports 3,586 Cyber Security Events in 2025 Panorama, Down 18%
rows.title: France's ANSSI Reports 3,586 Cyber Security Events in 2025 Panorama, Down 18%
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, France's ANSSI published its annual Panorama de la Cybermenace, reporting 3,586 security events handled during 2025, an 18% year-on-year decline. ANSSI attributed part of the 2024 baseline elevation to signal peaks generated by the Paris Olympics. Russia and China remained the dominant state-aligned threat-actor categories targeting French interests in 2025. Espionage and pre-positioning were identified as the principal modes of operation for state-sponsored actors. The report covers events handled directly by ANSSI across public and private sector entities.

rows.headline: CJEU Rules Police Biometric Data Collection Must Be Strictly Necessary Per Directive 2016/680
rows.title: CJEU Rules Police Biometric Data Collection Must Be Strictly Necessary Per Directive 2016/680
rows.summary: On 19 March 2026, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered judgment holding that national police authorities may collect biometric data during criminal investigations only where strictly necessary under Article 10 of Directive 2016/680. The case arose from the May 2020 detention of an individual, HW, who refused to provide fingerprints and photographs during custody in Paris and was fined EUR 300 for refusal, despite acquittal on the underlying offence. The Court found that reasonable suspicion alone does not justify collection, that every such decision must state reasons, and that systematic collection without case-by-case necessity assessment violates EU law. Criminal penalties for refusal remain permissible provided they satisfy Charter proportionality requirements.

rows.headline: Spain's INCIBE and CONETIC Publish 2025 Cybersecurity Industry Study Showing Sector Growth
rows.title: Spain's INCIBE and CONETIC Publish 2025 Cybersecurity Industry Study Showing Sector Growth
rows.summary: On 18 March 2026, Spain's Instituto Nacional de Ciberseguridad (INCIBE) and the Spanish Confederation of ICT Companies (CONETIC) published the Estudio sobre la industria de la ciberseguridad en España 2025. The study identified cybersecurity as one of the most dynamic sectors in Spain's digital economy, recording growth in employment, revenue, and number of firms, alongside projected further expansion. The report also flagged a persistent female-participation gap in the sector. INCIBE Director General Félix Barrio was cited in connection with the findings on gender representation.

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Seeks Trial Participants for PANAME Joint AI-Model GDPR Audit Tool
rows.title: France's ANSSI Seeks Trial Participants for PANAME Joint AI-Model GDPR Audit Tool
rows.summary: On 18 March 2026, France's ANSSI issued a call for trial participants in PANAME, an open-source library designed to unify GDPR auditing of AI models. The project is a joint initiative between ANSSI, CNIL, PEReN, and PEPR-IPoP. PANAME tests AI models for training-data extraction risks, addressing confidentiality requirements under GDPR. The tool aims to standardise audit methods across participating organisations. ANSSI invited interested parties to apply to join the trial phase through its official website.

rows.headline: Brazil's President Lula Signs Three Decrees Implementing ECA Digital Child Protection Law
rows.title: Brazil's President Lula Signs Three Decrees Implementing ECA Digital Child Protection Law
rows.summary: On 18 March 2026, President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva signed three decrees implementing Lei nº 15.211/2025, the Estatuto Digital da Criança e do Adolescente. The first decree regulates the ECA Digital framework. The second establishes a National Child Protection Centre within the Federal Police. The third strengthens the ANPD's mandate to regulate and supervise compliance. The government allocated BRL 100 million (approximately USD 18 million) through Finep to fund child protection technology. The law requires reliable age verification, default safety settings for minors, prompt removal of illegal content, and judicial authorisation for child influencer commercial activity.

rows.headline: Singapore's CSA and Japan's METI Sign IoT Cybersecurity Labelling Mutual Recognition Agreement
rows.title: Singapore's CSA and Japan's METI Sign IoT Cybersecurity Labelling Mutual Recognition Agreement
rows.summary: On 18 March 2026, Singapore's Cyber Security Agency (CSA) and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) signed a Memorandum of Cooperation formalising mutual recognition between Singapore's Cybersecurity Labelling Scheme (CLS) and Japan's Cyber STAR (JC-STAR) scheme. The agreement takes effect on 1 June 2026. Under the arrangement, smart devices certified under either scheme will be recognised in both jurisdictions, with manufacturers able to apply for the other country's label through a streamlined process. Covered devices include smart home assistants, IoT gateways, and home automation systems. Japan becomes the fifth country to hold such an arrangement with Singapore, following Finland, Germany, South Korea, and the United Kingdom.

rows.headline: Canada's OPC Reaches Compliance Agreement with WADA over Athletes' Sensitive Data Use
rows.title: Canada's OPC Reaches Compliance Agreement with WADA over Athletes' Sensitive Data Use
rows.summary: On 17 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada announced a compliance agreement with the World Anti-Doping Agency following a PIPEDA investigation. The investigation arose from a complaint alleging WADA had shared athletes' personal information with international sporting federations, which used it to assess sex-based eligibility without athletes' knowledge or consent. The OPC found this use fell outside the purposes for which the data was originally collected. Under the agreement, WADA committed to implementing measures preventing federations and other anti-doping organisations from using athlete data for purposes unrelated to anti-doping. No financial penalty was imposed. Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne welcomed WADA's commitments.
rows.authority: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada
rows.body_name: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

rows.headline: EU's EDPS Publishes AI Act Supervisory Compass for 2026–2027
rows.title: EU's EDPS Publishes AI Act Supervisory Compass for 2026–2027
rows.summary: On 17 March 2026, the European Data Protection Supervisor published its Compass document setting out a strategic supervisory approach to AI systems used by EU institutions under the AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689). The document defines the EDPS's role strictly as market surveillance authority and notified body, not as a data protection authority. Four strategic pillars guide the approach: ex ante and ex post supervision of EU institutions' AI systems, AI Act governance participation including the AI Board, interinstitutional knowledge exchange through a proposed Trustworthy AI Centre of Excellence, and international engagement. The EDPS limited the Compass to 2026–2027 citing the evolving AI Act and possible amendments under the 2025 Digital Omnibus proposal (COM(2025) 836 final).

rows.headline: Germany's DSK Calls for Targeted GDPR Amendments After Commission Digital Fitness Check
rows.title: Germany's DSK Calls for Targeted GDPR Amendments After Commission Digital Fitness Check
rows.summary: On 17 March 2026, Germany's Conference of Independent Data Protection Authorities (DSK), coordinated under the Mecklenburg-Vorpommern LfDI, published a formal position paper proposing targeted GDPR amendments in response to the European Commission's Digital Fitness Check. The DSK found that core principles in Articles 5 and 6 have proved effective and must remain intact. It proposed extending data protection responsibility under Article 25 to hardware and software manufacturers, removing the Article 37(7) obligation to register DPO contact details with supervisory authorities, prohibiting personalised advertising to children, and establishing specific legal bases for AI model development and operation.

rows.headline: Germany Enacts KRITIS-Dachgesetz Transposing CER Directive Into Federal Law
rows.title: Germany Enacts KRITIS-Dachgesetz Transposing CER Directive Into Federal Law
rows.summary: On 16 March 2026, Germany promulgated the KRITIS-Dachgesetz in the Federal Law Gazette, transposing EU Directive 2022/2557 on critical entity resilience into national law. The Federal Ministry of the Interior published the statute, which establishes uniform physical protection requirements for operators across energy, water, transport, banking and health sectors. The law introduces four core elements: identification of critical installation operators and entities of particular European significance, national risk analyses for critical services, minimum resilience measures for operators, and an incident reporting regime. The legislation complements separate national rules transposing the NIS 2 Directive on cybersecurity. The legislative procedure began on 1 September 2025.

rows.headline: UK ICO Sets Out Data Protection Framework for Police Facial Recognition Use
rows.title: UK ICO Sets Out Data Protection Framework for Police Facial Recognition Use
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published a blog post explaining why data protection sits at the centre of responsible police use of facial recognition technology. The ICO outlined its position on how data protection law applies when law enforcement agencies deploy facial recognition systems. The post addressed the conditions under which such technology can be used lawfully and the safeguards required to protect individuals' rights. The ICO stated that strong data protection standards and effective policing are not mutually exclusive goals.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Police Scotland GBP 66,000 and Issues Reprimand for Data Mishandling
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Police Scotland GBP 66,000 and Issues Reprimand for Data Mishandling
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined Police Scotland GBP 66,000 and issued a formal reprimand for serious failures in the handling of sensitive personal information. The ICO determined that Police Scotland mishandled personal data in a manner serious enough to warrant both a financial penalty and a reprimand. The dual sanction reflects the ICO's assessment of the severity of the data protection failures involved.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Open Letter Urging Platforms to Strengthen Children's Age Assurance
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Open Letter Urging Platforms to Strengthen Children's Age Assurance
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published an open letter to social media and video-sharing platforms operating in the United Kingdom. The letter calls on platforms to strengthen age assurance measures to prevent young children from accessing services not designed for them. The ICO directed the letter at firms subject to UK data protection law, urging them to ensure robust age checks are in place. The open letter forms part of the ICO's broader effort to enforce children's data protections under existing UK rules.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Birmingham Pendant Alarm Firm GBP 100,000 for Unsolicited TPS Calls
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Birmingham Pendant Alarm Firm GBP 100,000 for Unsolicited TPS Calls
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined a Birmingham-based pendant alarm company GBP 100,000 for making unsolicited marketing calls. The company placed over 260,000 calls to numbers registered on the Telephone Preference Service, breaching direct marketing rules. Registering with the TPS signals that consumers do not wish to receive such calls. The ICO determined the calls constituted a clear violation of the applicable legal framework governing electronic marketing communications.

rows.headline: UK ICO Publishes Jobseeker Guidance on Automated Recruitment Decisions
rows.title: UK ICO Publishes Jobseeker Guidance on Automated Recruitment Decisions
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published guidance explaining what jobseekers should know about automated decision-making in recruitment. The guidance sets out that organisations using automated tools to screen or rank candidates must inform applicants when such systems are used. Jobseekers retain the right to request human review of automated decisions that significantly affect them. The ICO stated that employers must be transparent about the logic involved in automated processes and cannot rely solely on automated outcomes without providing meaningful recourse. The publication forms part of the ICO's broader work on artificial intelligence and data protection in employment contexts.

rows.headline: UK ICO and Ofcom Publish Joint Statement on Age Assurance Regulatory Overlap
rows.title: UK ICO and Ofcom Publish Joint Statement on Age Assurance Regulatory Overlap
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office and Ofcom published a joint statement identifying the main areas of interaction between online safety law and data protection law as they relate to age assurance. The statement sets out where the two regulatory frameworks overlap and how each authority's remit applies. The ICO leads on data protection obligations, while Ofcom holds responsibility for online safety duties. The joint statement aims to clarify how the two regimes interact for services that deploy age assurance measures.

rows.headline: Egypt's MCIT Publishes Version 2.0 of National AI Governance Framework Guide
rows.title: Egypt's MCIT Publishes Version 2.0 of National AI Governance Framework Guide
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, Egypt's Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, acting through the National Council for Artificial Intelligence, Quantum Computing, and Emerging Technologies, published Version 2.0 of the Guide to Egypt's National AI Governance Framework, authorised by Minister Raafat Hindy. The Egyptian Centre for Responsible AI prepared the document following a drafting process that began in August 2025, included a public consultation in January 2026, and incorporated feedback from government bodies, private sector participants, and international organisations. The Guide defines a risk-tier classification system, a 'Dual-Check' compliance logic, and a 'State as Orchestrator' governance philosophy. It applies to all entities operating AI systems within Egypt's public and private sectors. No financial penalties are associated with this publication.
rows.authority: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology
rows.body_name: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology

rows.headline: UK FCA, ICO, SRA and ASA Form Joint Taskforce on Motor Finance Claims
rows.title: UK FCA, ICO, SRA and ASA Form Joint Taskforce on Motor Finance Claims
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the Financial Conduct Authority, Information Commissioner's Office, Solicitors Regulation Authority and Advertising Standards Authority announced a joint taskforce targeting poor practice in motor finance claims handling. The four regulators agreed to coordinate their efforts against claims management companies and law firms identified as handling motor finance complaints poorly. The FCA leads financial conduct oversight, the SRA covers solicitor conduct, the ICO addresses data protection concerns, and the ASA focuses on advertising standards. The taskforce will combine each regulator's existing powers to address misconduct across the sector.

rows.headline: UK ICO Calls on Businesses to Review Automated Decision-Making in Recruitment
rows.title: UK ICO Calls on Businesses to Review Automated Decision-Making in Recruitment
rows.summary: On 15 March 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published guidance calling on businesses to review their use of automated decision-making in recruitment. The ICO stated that automated decisions can streamline hiring but must include appropriate protections for candidates. The regulator directed businesses to assess whether their current processes meet the required legal safeguards under data protection law. The ICO did not announce fines or enforcement action, framing the publication as a proactive call to review existing practices.

rows.headline: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Levies €775,000 in GDPR Fines Across First Nine Months of 2025
rows.title: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Levies €775,000 in GDPR Fines Across First Nine Months of 2025
rows.summary: On 30 September 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit published an interim review disclosing €775,000 in fines across 15 concluded proceedings. The largest penalty, €492,000, was levied against an unnamed financial sector company for Article 22 GDPR failures after it rejected credit card applications through fully automated processes without human intervention and failed to meet its information and access obligations under Articles 13, 14 and 15. A retail company received €195,000 for prolonged failure to respond to data subject rights requests under Article 12. Three further companies were each fined in the lower five-figure range for sending promotional emails without valid consent under Article 6(1).
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Urges Federal Government to Designate EU AI Act Market Surveillance Authorities
rows.title: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Urges Federal Government to Designate EU AI Act Market Surveillance Authorities
rows.summary: On 31 July 2025, HmbBfDI Commissioner Thomas Fuchs issued a public appeal calling on the German federal government to designate competent market surveillance authorities under the EU AI Act without delay. Germany missed the 2 August 2025 statutory deadline, leaving businesses and public bodies without a binding compliance contact. Fuchs stated the situation disadvantages Germany as an AI innovation location. Under Article 74(8) of the AI Act, data protection authorities including the HmbBfDI are already designated for high-risk AI systems in sensitive areas such as law enforcement and migration. New transparency obligations for AI model providers also took effect on 2 August 2025.
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Confirms Data Act Supervisory Role for Personal Data from 12 September 2025
rows.title: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Confirms Data Act Supervisory Role for Personal Data from 12 September 2025
rows.summary: On 9 September 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) published a press release confirming its supervisory competence under Art. 37(3) of the EU Data Act for matters involving personal data, effective 12 September 2025. The Data Act creates access rights to sensor data from connected devices, covering users and eligible third parties. The HmbBfDI's remit includes access to personal data held by manufacturers, cloud-provider switching, technical and organisational confidentiality measures, and transparency obligations. The authority may issue orders and impose fines for infringements. Complaints may be lodged where a Hamburg-based undertaking is alleged to have breached Data Act rights over personal data.
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Adopt Joint Opinion 3/2026 on European Biotech Act Clinical Trials Data
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Adopt Joint Opinion 3/2026 on European Biotech Act Clinical Trials Data
rows.summary: On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the European Commission's proposed European Biotech Act. The authorities support the proposal's goal of reducing fragmentation in the Clinical Trials Regulation but identified areas requiring stronger safeguards. The opinion calls on the Biotech Act to clarify whether trial sponsors and investigators act as sole or joint controllers, to limit the mandatory 25-year retention period to the clinical trial master file, to define purposes and safeguards for further processing of trial data, and to explicitly mandate pseudonymisation where directly identifiable data need not be processed.

rows.headline: EU Court of Justice Rules Hosting Providers Are GDPR Controllers for User-Published Content
rows.title: EU Court of Justice Rules Hosting Providers Are GDPR Controllers for User-Published Content
rows.summary: On 2 December 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-492/23 Russmedia, finding that a hosting provider can bear joint controllership under the GDPR for unlawful content published by users on its platform. The case arose from an advertisement placed on an online marketplace without consent, using a woman's photograph and telephone number to offer alleged sexual services. The Court held that platform-operating decisions concerning purposes and means of data processing were sufficient to engage Article 4(7) GDPR obligations. The Court also confirmed that the DSA notice-and-action liability privilege does not limit data subjects' rights under the GDPR.
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: Argentina's AAIP and 60-Plus Authorities Issue Joint Statement on AI Imagery Privacy
rows.title: Argentina's AAIP and 60-Plus Authorities Issue Joint Statement on AI Imagery Privacy
rows.summary: On 12 March 2026, Argentina's Agencia de Acceso a la Información Pública and more than 60 data protection authorities published a joint declaration on AI-generated audiovisual content. Produced under the Global Privacy Assembly's International Enforcement Cooperation Working Group, the statement addresses systems that generate realistic images or videos of identifiable individuals without their knowledge or consent. The declaration sets out four shared regulatory expectations: implementing security measures against non-consensual intimate imagery, disclosing system capabilities and acceptable uses, providing accessible content-removal mechanisms with rapid responses, and offering age-appropriate information protecting minors. The statement does not impose binding legal obligations.
rows.authority: Agencia de Acceso a la Informacion Publica
rows.body_name: Agencia de Acceso a la Informacion Publica

rows.headline: EDPB and EDPS Back EU Biotech Act Harmonisation but Demand Stronger Genetic Data Safeguards
rows.title: EDPB and EDPS Back EU Biotech Act Harmonisation but Demand Stronger Genetic Data Safeguards
rows.summary: On 12 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and the European Data Protection Supervisor published Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the Commission's proposed European Biotech Act. The two authorities supported the proposal's goal of reducing fragmentation in the application of the Clinical Trials Regulation and welcomed its aim to establish a single legal basis for data processing by sponsors and investigators under Article 9 GDPR. The authorities cautioned, however, that harmonisation must not lower protections for health and genetic data. They recommended that any unified legal basis be accompanied by specific, proportionate safeguards reflecting the sensitivity of clinical trial data.

rows.headline: Canada's OPC Tables ArriveCAN Special Report Finding No Privacy Act Contraventions
rows.title: Canada's OPC Tables ArriveCAN Special Report Finding No Privacy Act Contraventions
rows.summary: On 12 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) tabled a Special Report in Parliament concluding that the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) committed no contraventions of the Privacy Act in its handling of personal information collected through the ArriveCAN application. The OPC identified shortcomings in CBSA contracting practices, including inadequate security-requirements assessments, unclear task descriptions in contractor authorisations, and insufficient restriction of contractor access to personal information. The CBSA accepted all of the Commissioner's recommendations and committed to strengthening privacy and security standards within its contracting framework.
rows.authority: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada
rows.body_name: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

rows.headline: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Sets Out GPAI Provider Obligations Under EU AI Act
rows.title: Hamburg's HmbBfDI Sets Out GPAI Provider Obligations Under EU AI Act
rows.summary: On 31 July 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) published a statement confirming that transparency and compliance obligations for general-purpose AI (GPAI) model providers under the EU AI Act took effect on 2 August 2025. Providers must create technical documentation, declare copyright compliance, publish training-data summaries, and supply functionality information. High-capability models presenting systemic risk face additional vulnerability testing and incident-reporting duties. Deployers integrating GPAI models may demand that information from providers to meet their own compliance obligations. Providers of models already on the market before 2 August 2025 have a transition period until 2 August 2027. The AI Office may open investigations from August 2025, while Commission fines apply only from 2 August 2026.
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: France's ANSSI Reports 3,586 Cyber Security Events in 2025 Panorama, Down 18 Percent
rows.title: France's ANSSI Reports 3,586 Cyber Security Events in 2025 Panorama, Down 18 Percent
rows.summary: On 11 March 2026, France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information published its annual cyber threat overview for 2025, recording 3,586 security events, an 18 percent decrease from 2024. ANSSI attributed part of the decline to elevated reporting volumes during the Paris Olympic and Paralympic Games. Of total events, 2,209 were notifications and 1,366 were incidents. Education and research accounted for 34 percent of sector exposure, followed by ministries and local authorities at 24 percent. ANSSI confirmed that only 42 percent of 460 possible data-leak events constituted actual breaches. The agency identified Russia and China as the principal state-linked actors targeting French interests for espionage and infrastructure pre-positioning.

rows.headline: France's CNIL and HAS Sign Partnership Agreement on Digital Health and AI
rows.title: France's CNIL and HAS Sign Partnership Agreement on Digital Health and AI
rows.summary: On 11 March 2026, France's CNIL published a partnership convention signed with the Haute Autorité de santé on 10 March 2026. The agreement commits both authorities to coordinating guidance on personal data protection and AI across the health, social and medico-social sectors. Four collaboration areas are identified: promoting data protection rights in digital and AI tools, adapting European rules to the French health system, improving data security practices among sector actors, and integrating AI requirements into establishment certification processes. The two authorities expect to publish a first joint recommendation in Q2 2026, addressing AI use in care settings and specifying the applicable legal and regulatory framework.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Italy's AgID Opens Public Consultation on Draft AI Development Guidelines for Public Administration
rows.title: Italy's AgID Opens Public Consultation on Draft AI Development Guidelines for Public Administration
rows.summary: On 11 March 2026, Italy's Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale published Version 1.0 of draft guidelines titled 'Linee Guida per lo sviluppo di sistemi di Intelligenza Artificiale nella pubblica amministrazione', opened for public consultation. The guidelines were issued pursuant to the D.P.C.M. of 12 January 2024 and are not a final binding instrument. AgID sets out seven general principles drawn from Law 132/2025, covering fundamental rights, transparency, data quality, human-centredness, democratic sovereignty, EU AI Act coherence, cybersecurity and accessibility. The draft also defines a five-layer AI reference architecture and classifies applicable AI system families, including machine learning, deep learning and generative AI.

rows.headline: Italy's AgID Publishes Draft AI Procurement Guidelines for Public Administrations
rows.title: Italy's AgID Publishes Draft AI Procurement Guidelines for Public Administrations
rows.summary: On 11 March 2026, Italy's Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (AgID) published Version 1.0 of draft guidelines on AI procurement by public administrations for public consultation. The document applies to all entities within the scope of Article 2, paragraph 2, of the Codice dell'Amministrazione Digitale. AgID structured the guidelines around five areas, covering general acquisition frameworks, metrics, costs, monitoring, and contract management. AgID introduced a Levelised Cost of AI methodology as an alternative to traditional economic appraisal approaches. The guidelines also address cybersecurity risk management, hardware neutrality, data considerations, interoperability, and inter-administrative cooperation. AgID issued the document pursuant to the D.P.C.M. of 12 January 2024 establishing the three-year public administration IT plan for 2024 to 2026.

rows.headline: EU's EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion 3/2026 on European Biotech Act Data Rules
rows.title: EU's EDPB and EDPS Issue Joint Opinion 3/2026 on European Biotech Act Data Rules
rows.summary: On 10 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board and European Data Protection Supervisor adopted Joint Opinion 3/2026 on the proposed European Biotech Act. The two bodies supported the proposal's goal of harmonising clinical trials regulation and establishing a unified legal basis for data processing by sponsors and investigators. They raised concerns that simplifications must not reduce protections for health and genetic data. Recommendations included clarifying controller roles for sponsors and investigators, limiting the mandatory 25-year retention period to main trial documentation only, specifying purposes and safeguards for data reuse in research, requiring pseudonymisation where direct identification is unnecessary, and ensuring regulatory sandboxes provide an explicit legal basis for processing special category data.

rows.headline: Germany's Bremen LfDI Annual Report Records 48% Complaints Rise and Cloud Sovereignty Concern
rows.title: Germany's Bremen LfDI Annual Report Records 48% Complaints Rise and Cloud Sovereignty Concern
rows.summary: On 16 March 2026, Bremen's LfDI published its eighth GDPR annual report, covering the 2025 reporting year. Complaints rose 48 percent, from approximately 517 to 765, with video surveillance accounting for 19 percent of submissions. Reported personal data breaches increased from 205 to 260. The authority identified reliance on cloud services outside GDPR territorial scope as a continuing concern. Notable incidents included unauthorised registration-register queries by Bremen District Court employees, a cyberattack compromising patient and staff data at a hospital group, and repeated mishandling of employee sick notes. The authority also flagged an unresolved supervisory-competence gap over the Bremen Public Prosecution Office, noting the Bremen Senate committed to preparing corrective legislation.

rows.headline: Egypt's NTRA and EG-CERT Launch Safe Internet Campaign and Arabic Digital-Citizenship Platform
rows.title: Egypt's NTRA and EG-CERT Launch Safe Internet Campaign and Arabic Digital-Citizenship Platform
rows.summary: On 25 February 2026, Egypt's National Telecom Regulatory Authority launched a public awareness campaign on safe and responsible internet use, coinciding with Safer Internet Day. The campaign debuted alongside wa3i.net, described as one of the first integrated Arabic-language platforms dedicated to digital citizenship and online protection. The platform provides content categorised by age group, covering children, adolescents, parents, teachers, and older persons. The launch was attended by Communications Minister Raouf Handey, the Chairperson of the National Council for Childhood and Motherhood, and representatives from UNICEF Egypt and the UNDP. Campaign materials address personal data protection, electronic fraud, cybersecurity risks, and parental oversight of children's internet use.
rows.authority: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology
rows.body_name: Ministry of Communications and Information Technology

rows.headline: Germany's Bundesverwaltungsgericht Upholds Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI Order on Insurer Diagnosis Data
rows.title: Germany's Bundesverwaltungsgericht Upholds Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI Order on Insurer Diagnosis Data
rows.summary: On 6 March 2026, Germany's Bundesverwaltungsgericht upheld a 2022 Rheinland-Pfalz LfDI corrective order requiring a private health insurer to obtain explicit consent before processing diagnosis data for preventive programme recommendations. The court found no adequate legal basis under Article 9 GDPR for the insurer's secondary use of health data originally submitted for cost reimbursement. Lower courts in Mainz and Koblenz had initially sided with the insurer before the case reached federal level. No financial penalty was imposed. LfDI Prof. Dr. Dieter Kugelmann stated the ruling provides legal certainty and strengthens insured persons' right of self-determination over their health data.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Closes Injunction Against KASPR After Full GDPR Remediation
rows.title: France's CNIL Closes Injunction Against KASPR After Full GDPR Remediation
rows.summary: On 4 March 2026, France's CNIL adopted a deliberation formally closing the injunction it had imposed on data intelligence company KASPR on 5 December 2024. The original order accompanied a EUR 240,000 (USD 261,000) fine for four GDPR breaches, including unlawful collection of LinkedIn users' contact data, excessive retention periods, inadequate transparency, and insufficient responses to access requests. A daily penalty of EUR 10,000 for non-compliance was available but not enforced. KASPR erased its entire database, ceased LinkedIn data collection, removed automatic retention-renewal triggers, and began providing data-subject information in all official EU languages. The CNIL confirmed these measures satisfied the injunction's requirements.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: US NTIA Asks FCC to Seek Public Comment on GPS L1 Protection Rules for D2D Satellite Devices
rows.title: US NTIA Asks FCC to Seek Public Comment on GPS L1 Protection Rules for D2D Satellite Devices
rows.summary: On 6 March 2026, the National Telecommunications and Information Administration transmitted a formal recommendation letter to the Federal Communications Commission requesting a public comment process on interference protections for the GPS L1 signal at 1575.42 MHz. The recommendation targets Mobile Satellite Service Direct-to-Device user equipment operating in the adjacent 1610–1660.5 MHz band. NTIA also recommends that D2D devices unable to meet certain emission limits be rendered incapable of operating aboard aircraft, with specific numerical thresholds to be developed through the FCC's forthcoming comment process. No fine or enforcement action accompanies the recommendation, which is advisory in nature.

rows.headline: Germany's Bremen LfDI Publishes Eighth Annual GDPR Report for 2025
rows.title: Germany's Bremen LfDI Publishes Eighth Annual GDPR Report for 2025
rows.summary: On 16 March 2026, the Bremen Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information, Dr Timo Utermark, published his eighth annual report under Art. 59 GDPR, covering supervisory activity across 2025. The report addresses two enforcement cases involving GPS tracking by a former intimate partner and unauthorised data queries by an employee. It examines AI training, EU AI Act implementation in Germany, and Microsoft 365 use by public bodies. Additional topics include video surveillance, fax use in public administration, data protection officer notifications, and the authority's formal opinion on the draft Bremen Police Act.

rows.headline: France's CNIL and HAS Open Public Consultation on Draft AI-in-Healthcare Guide
rows.title: France's CNIL and HAS Open Public Consultation on Draft AI-in-Healthcare Guide
rows.summary: On 5 March 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de Santé launched a public consultation on a joint draft guide titled 'Accompagner le bon usage des systèmes d'intelligence artificielle en contexte de soins'. The guide covers all AI systems with a direct impact on patient care, regardless of classification under the EU AI Act, the Medical Device Regulation or the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation. It applies to all healthcare sector actors, including hospitals and independent practitioners. The document comprises twelve fiches and classifies recommendations into three levels. The consultation closes on 16 April 2026.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Canada's Privacy Commissioner Expands X Investigation to Grok Amid Deepfake Concerns
rows.title: Canada's Privacy Commissioner Expands X Investigation to Grok Amid Deepfake Concerns
rows.summary: On 5 March 2026, Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne delivered keynote remarks at the Victoria International Privacy and Security Summit, outlining the Office's enforcement and policy priorities across AI governance, children's privacy and law reform. Dufresne confirmed that in January 2026 the Office expanded its existing investigation into X to include the Grok chatbot, citing concern over explicit synthetic images created without consent. The Office also signed a joint declaration on AI-generated content with more than 50 global data protection authorities, convened by the Global Privacy Assembly. A separate Office survey found nine in ten Canadians expressed concern about privacy protection.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Tables Informatique et Libertés, Opening Decisional Practice to External Audiences
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Tables Informatique et Libertés, Opening Decisional Practice to External Audiences
rows.summary: On 2 March 2026, France's CNIL released version 2.3 of its Tables Informatique et Libertés, dated 20 February 2026. The thematically organised reference document consolidates jurisprudence from the CJEU, European Court of Human Rights, Conseil d'État, Cour de cassation, Conseil Constitutionnel, the EDPB, and CNIL itself, spanning periods from 1978 to 2026. The CNIL identified two objectives: supporting internal doctrinal consistency across an authority handling approximately 20,000 written information requests and 1,500 complex legal consultations annually, and making previously unpublished positions, including legal reminders, formal notices and complaint rejections, accessible to data protection officers, lawyers, consultants and academics. The CNIL stated the Tables will be updated regularly and invited professionals to submit corrections.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Ecuador's SPDP Publishes Version 2 of Risk Management and Data Protection Impact Assessment Guide
rows.title: Ecuador's SPDP Publishes Version 2 of Risk Management and Data Protection Impact Assessment Guide
rows.summary: On 2 March 2026, Ecuador's Superintendencia de Protección de Datos Personales (SPDP) released version 2 of its Guide on Risk Management and Impact Assessment of Personal Data Processing. The guide establishes structured quantitative and qualitative methodologies for controllers and processors under the Ley Orgánica de Protección de Datos Personales. It covers context establishment, risk identification, risk analysis, DPIA evaluation, and risk treatment, and includes an annexed DPIA template. The SPDP states the guide is scalable to micro, small, medium and large organisations. No fines or enforcement orders are associated with the publication.
rows.authority: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales
rows.body_name: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales

rows.headline: Singapore's CSA Mandates Cyber Trust Mark Certification for CII Owners and Licensed Providers
rows.title: Singapore's CSA Mandates Cyber Trust Mark Certification for CII Owners and Licensed Providers
rows.summary: On 2 March 2026, the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore announced mandatory Cyber Trust Mark certification requirements for Critical Information Infrastructure Owners, CII auditors, and licensed cybersecurity service providers under section 49 of the Cybersecurity Act. CII Owners must attain CTM Level 5 for non-CII systems supporting business operations, with a compliance deadline of end-2027. CII auditors face the same Level 5 requirement at the organisational level, with a deadline of end-2026. Licensed penetration testing and managed security operations centre monitoring providers must obtain CTM Promoter (Tier 3) certification by 31 December 2026, a requirement implemented in February 2026 following a public consultation that drew 17 responses.

rows.headline: Denmark's Agency for Society Security Publishes Guidance on Amended Security Clearance Circular
rows.title: Denmark's Agency for Society Security Publishes Guidance on Amended Security Clearance Circular
rows.summary: On 2 March 2026, the Danish Agency for Society Security published guidance for public authorities on amendments to Circular No. 10338, effective 1 March 2026. The amended circular introduces two mechanisms. First, a public authority may grant a security clearance to an individual based on an existing clearance held in another capacity, removing the requirement to submit a new investigation request to the Danish Security and Intelligence Service. Second, where an authority submits a renewal request before an existing clearance expires, the expiry is suspended for up to 12 months while the application is processed. No financial penalties are associated with this regulatory update.

rows.headline: France's ANSSI and CNIL Seek Testers for PANAME GDPR Audit Tool for AI Models
rows.title: France's ANSSI and CNIL Seek Testers for PANAME GDPR Audit Tool for AI Models
rows.summary: On 2 March 2026, ANSSI published a call for expressions of interest in testing PANAME, a GDPR audit tool for artificial intelligence models developed jointly with CNIL, PEReN and the IPoP project under France's PEPR Cybersecurity programme. The call is open from 26 February to 28 March 2026 and targets public and private entities established in EU member states, including companies, startups and research institutions that use or develop AI models. PANAME aims to produce a software library, partly open-source, to standardise privacy evaluation of AI models and support compliance assessment under Regulation (EU) 2016/679. ANSSI will organise a workshop for respondents.

rows.headline: Croatia's AZOP Fines Real Estate Agency EUR 100,000 for Three GDPR Violations
rows.title: Croatia's AZOP Fines Real Estate Agency EUR 100,000 for Three GDPR Violations
rows.summary: On 19 February 2026, Croatia's Agencija za zaštitu osobnih podataka fined an unnamed real estate agency EUR 100,000 for breaching Articles 5(1)(e), 5(1)(c), 6(1) and 32(4) GDPR. AZOP found the controller retained personal data belonging to 11,887 clients beyond the required period. The agency also processed 914 copies of identification and financial documents, including 898 identity cards and 6 passports, without a demonstrated legal basis. AZOP further determined that the controller failed to train and supervise employees with access to personal data adequately. No harm to data subjects was established in this case.

rows.headline: Cape Verde's CNPD Co-Signs 61-Authority Joint Declaration on AI-Generated Content Risks
rows.title: Cape Verde's CNPD Co-Signs 61-Authority Joint Declaration on AI-Generated Content Risks
rows.summary: On 27 February 2026, Cape Verde's Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados endorsed a joint declaration on AI-generated images coordinated by the Global Privacy Assembly's International Enforcement Cooperation Working Group, originally issued on 23 February 2026. The declaration, signed by 61 data protection authorities, identifies serious privacy risks where AI systems generate realistic images or videos of identifiable individuals without consent. Signatories express particular concern about harm to children depicted in such content. The declaration sets out requirements for organisations developing or deploying AI content-generation systems, covering security measures against non-consensual intimate imagery, transparency about system functionality, accessible content-removal mechanisms, and enhanced child-specific safeguards. Co-signatories plan to share approaches on enforcement, policy, and education.

rows.headline: Catalonia's APDCAT Signs 61-Authority GPA Statement on AI-Generated Image Privacy Risks
rows.title: Catalonia's APDCAT Signs 61-Authority GPA Statement on AI-Generated Image Privacy Risks
rows.summary: On 23 February 2026, Catalonia's Autoritat Catalana de Protecció de Dades subscribed to a joint statement coordinated by the Global Privacy Assembly's International Enforcement Working Group. The statement, signed by 61 data protection authorities, addresses privacy risks posed by AI-generated images and videos depicting identifiable individuals without their consent. The statement sets expectations for organisations that develop and deploy AI image-generation systems. Co-signatories committed to sharing enforcement, policy, and education approaches across their respective jurisdictions.
rows.authority: Autoritat Catalana de Protecció de Dades
rows.body_name: Autoritat Catalana de Protecció de Dades

rows.headline: Sweden's IMY Sets 2026 Supervisory Priorities: AI, Children's Data, and Law Enforcement Tools
rows.title: Sweden's IMY Sets 2026 Supervisory Priorities: AI, Children's Data, and Law Enforcement Tools
rows.summary: On 23 February 2026, Sweden's Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten (IMY) published its supervisory and guidance priorities for 2026 under the GDPR, identifying three focus areas: AI use in the public sector, data protection for children and young people, and law enforcement tools. IMY selected these areas because they present elevated privacy risks or require increased compliance knowledge. On public-sector AI, IMY noted that citizens often cannot avoid AI-driven data processing when using government services. On children, IMY cited Recital 38 GDPR and plans to raise awareness among minors, guardians, and schools. On law enforcement, IMY will examine tools including secret coercive measures and biometric data collection. No financial penalties are associated with this announcement.

rows.headline: Andalusia's CTPDA Finds Strong Public-Sector Compliance in EDPB Erasure-Right Action
rows.title: Andalusia's CTPDA Finds Strong Public-Sector Compliance in EDPB Erasure-Right Action
rows.summary: On 19 February 2026, the Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía published its analysis of the EDPB's 2025 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action on the right to erasure, focusing on Andalusian public-sector bodies. The report found a low volume of erasure requests and generalised formal compliance across the sector. Assessors identified notable good practices in identity-verification procedures and citizen-facing request pathways. The analysis also recorded demand among public bodies for practical guidance, standardised resolution templates, and tools to help locate personal data held across systems.
rows.authority: Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía
rows.body_name: Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía

rows.headline: Brandenburg's LDA Joins EDPB's Fourth CEF Action on Article 17 Erasure Rights, Reviewing Ten Housing Firms
rows.title: Brandenburg's LDA Joins EDPB's Fourth CEF Action on Article 17 Erasure Rights, Reviewing Ten Housing Firms
rows.summary: On 18 February 2026, the European Data Protection Board published results of its 2025 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action on the right to erasure under Article 17 GDPR. Brandenburg's Landesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz was among 32 supervisory authorities participating, selecting ten larger Brandenburg-based housing companies for review. The EDPB identified recurring controller failures including absent internal deletion rules, insufficient staff training, and uncertainty around back-up system deletion and anonymisation requirements. Brandenburg Commissioner Dagmar Hartge noted the housing companies showed high cooperation. The authority will follow up by sending individual advisory letters to participating companies. This was the fourth CEF initiative, following earlier actions on data protection officers in 2023 and the right of access in 2024.

rows.headline: Philippines Supreme Court Sets Risk-Based AI Governance Framework for Judiciary
rows.title: Philippines Supreme Court Sets Risk-Based AI Governance Framework for Judiciary
rows.summary: On 18 February 2026, the Philippines Supreme Court En Banc issued A.M. No. 25-11-28-SC, establishing a risk-based governance framework for artificial intelligence use across the judiciary. The resolution defines four risk tiers: prohibited, high-risk, limited-risk, and minimal-risk. High-risk applications require prior authorization and ongoing monitoring. All AI systems must remain auditable. The resolution prohibits AI from serving as the sole, primary, or determinative basis of any adjudicatory outcome, reserving final judicial decision-making for human judges.

rows.headline: MLCommons Publishes Taxonomy-Driven Benchmarking Methodology for LLM Jailbreak Attacks
rows.title: MLCommons Publishes Taxonomy-Driven Benchmarking Methodology for LLM Jailbreak Attacks
rows.summary: On 16 February 2026, MLCommons released a methodology paper proposing a mechanism-first taxonomy for benchmarking single-turn jailbreak attacks on large language models. The paper, authored by researchers from the Alan Turing Institute, Google DeepMind, NVIDIA, Meta, and Singapore's Infocomm Media Development Authority, identifies three deficiencies in existing benchmarks: lack of defensibility, coverage gaps across attack families, and reproducibility failures caused by overlapping or inconsistently abstracted categories. The proposed taxonomy builds on MLCommons infrastructure including AILuminate v1.0 and the MLCommons Jailbreak Benchmark v0.5. The paper references ISO/IEC 42001 as a governance alignment standard and frames jailbreak resistance as an operational and auditability requirement.

rows.headline: UK ICO and Global Authorities Publish Joint Statement on AI-Generated Imagery Privacy Risks
rows.title: UK ICO and Global Authorities Publish Joint Statement on AI-Generated Imagery Privacy Risks
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the Information Commissioner's Office and data protection authorities from multiple jurisdictions published a joint statement on the privacy risks of AI-generated imagery. The statement sets out the authorities' collective position on how AI image generation technologies interact with data protection obligations. The ICO joined regulators from across the globe in issuing the document, marking a coordinated international effort to address privacy concerns arising from AI-generated visual content.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Reddit GBP 14.47 Million for Children's Privacy Failures
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Reddit GBP 14.47 Million for Children's Privacy Failures
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office issued Reddit, Inc. a GBP 14.47 million fine for unlawfully using children's personal information. The ICO's investigation found that Reddit processed children's data in breach of UK data protection law. The regulator determined Reddit failed to implement adequate measures to prevent children from accessing content and having their personal information used without a lawful basis. The fine represents one of the ICO's more significant enforcement actions against a major social media platform for child privacy violations.

rows.headline: UK ICO Reports Progress on Economic Growth Commitments to Government
rows.title: UK ICO Reports Progress on Economic Growth Commitments to Government
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published an update to government on its economic growth commitments. In January 2025, the ICO had responded to a government call for proposals aimed at boosting the UK economy. The February update sets out progress made against those commitments. The ICO framed its regulatory approach as a contributor to economic growth, reporting on actions taken since the original January 2025 submission.

rows.headline: UK ICO Marks Commencement of the Data Use and Access Act
rows.title: UK ICO Marks Commencement of the Data Use and Access Act
rows.summary: On 5 February 2026, the next phase of the Data (Use and Access) Act implementation commenced in the United Kingdom. The Information Commissioner's Office issued a statement marking the development. The DUAA represents a significant update to the UK's data regulation framework, succeeding prior legislation. The ICO's statement acknowledged the commencement date and signalled the authority's role in the Act's ongoing implementation. No enforcement actions, fines, or specific operational guidance were announced alongside the statement.

rows.headline: UK ICO Opens Formal Investigations into X and X.AI Over Grok Data Processing
rows.title: UK ICO Opens Formal Investigations into X and X.AI Over Grok Data Processing
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office opened formal investigations into X Internet Unlimited Company and X.AI LLC. Both investigations cover the companies' processing of personal data in connection with the Grok artificial intelligence system. The ICO also cited Grok's potential to produce harmful sexualised image and video content as part of its inquiry scope. The investigations are separate actions targeting the two entities individually.

rows.headline: UK ICO Wins Court of Appeal Ruling Against DSG Retail Limited
rows.title: UK ICO Wins Court of Appeal Ruling Against DSG Retail Limited
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office succeeded in its Court of Appeal case against DSG Retail Limited. The Court of Appeal overturned the earlier Upper Tribunal decision, ruling in favour of the ICO. The case concerned DSG Retail Limited, a major UK electronics retailer. The ICO had originally taken action against DSG and subsequently appealed after the Upper Tribunal ruled against it. The Court of Appeal's decision restores the ICO's original position in the matter.

rows.headline: UK ICO Responds to MPs' February Letter Regarding Tattle Life Platform
rows.title: UK ICO Responds to MPs' February Letter Regarding Tattle Life Platform
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office published its response to a letter sent by MPs on 9 February 2026 concerning Tattle Life. The ICO addressed the concerns raised by the MPs about the platform. Tattle Life is a website that has been the subject of parliamentary attention in the United Kingdom. The ICO's response sets out the regulator's position on the matters raised in the MPs' correspondence.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Imgur Owner MediaLab for Children's Privacy Violations
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Imgur Owner MediaLab for Children's Privacy Violations
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office fined MediaLab, the owner of image-sharing platform Imgur, for unlawfully processing children's personal data. The ICO's investigation found that MediaLab collected and used data belonging to child users without a lawful basis under UK data protection law. The regulator concluded that MediaLab failed to implement adequate safeguards to prevent children from being exposed to privacy risks on the platform. The ICO published its findings alongside the penalty notice on its official media centre.

rows.headline: UK ICO Reprimands Staines Health Group for Excessive Medical Disclosure to Insurer
rows.title: UK ICO Reprimands Staines Health Group for Excessive Medical Disclosure to Insurer
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office reprimanded Staines Health Group, a GP surgery, for sending excessive medical history about a terminally ill patient to the patient's insurance company. The ICO found that the volume of medical detail disclosed exceeded what was necessary for the insurance purpose. The reprimand marks a formal finding against the surgery for failing to apply data minimisation principles when responding to the insurer's request. No financial penalty was issued alongside the reprimand.

rows.headline: UK ICO Secures Guilty Verdicts Against Eight Men in Largest Nuisance Call Investigation
rows.title: UK ICO Secures Guilty Verdicts Against Eight Men in Largest Nuisance Call Investigation
rows.summary: On 15 February 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office secured guilty verdicts against eight men following its largest ever nuisance call investigation. The men were convicted for unlawfully accessing and obtaining personal information from vehicle repair garages. That data was then used to generate potential leads for personal injury claims. The ICO brought the prosecution under laws prohibiting the unlawful obtaining of personal data. The case represents the largest criminal investigation the ICO has undertaken related to nuisance call activity and data theft from the automotive repair sector.

rows.headline: John Edwards speaks at IAPP UK Intensive 2026
rows.title: John Edwards speaks at IAPP UK Intensive 2026
rows.summary: The Information Commissioner gives the keynote speech.

rows.headline: Florida Attorney General Issues 186 Notices Under Digital Bill of Rights in 2025
rows.title: Florida Attorney General Issues 186 Notices Under Digital Bill of Rights in 2025
rows.summary: On 10 February 2026, the Florida Attorney General's Office published its Florida Digital Bill of Rights Annual Enforcement Report covering 1 January to 31 December 2025. The Department of Legal Affairs issued 186 Notices of Alleged Violation to controllers during the period, resolving 64 without litigation and pursuing one active court case. No financial penalties were issued or collected. The Department received 1,496 consumer complaints and inquiries, closing 685 as outside scope and placing 811 under active review. Access and confirmation rights generated the largest complaint volume, at 730. A further 568 complaints were closed for legal insufficiency.

rows.headline: Spain's INCIBE Detected 122,000 Cybersecurity Incidents and Handled 142,767 Helpline Queries in 2025
rows.title: Spain's INCIBE Detected 122,000 Cybersecurity Incidents and Handled 142,767 Helpline Queries in 2025
rows.summary: On 9 February 2026, Spain's Instituto Nacional de Ciberseguridad published its 2025 activity figures. INCIBE-CERT managed more than 122,000 cybersecurity incidents across the year. The agency also proactively detected and notified 237,028 vulnerable systems exposed on the internet. For NIS2-designated essential operators, INCIBE handled 401 incidents. The 017 helpline, 'Tu Ayuda en Ciberseguridad', received 142,767 queries, a 44.9 percent increase year on year. Nearly half of all helpline contacts, 49 percent, were classified as preventive in nature rather than responses to active incidents.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Reports 83 Sanctions and EUR 486.8 Million in Fines for 2025
rows.title: France's CNIL Reports 83 Sanctions and EUR 486.8 Million in Fines for 2025
rows.summary: On 9 February 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its annual enforcement review for 2025, recording 259 total decisions: 83 sanctions, 143 formal notices, 31 reminders of legal obligations, and 2 warnings. Of the 83 sanctions, 78 were fines totalling EUR 486,839,500, three were astreinte liquidation decisions, and two were reprimands. The restricted formation imposed fines of EUR 325 million and EUR 150 million on two entities for cookie and tracker violations. Sixteen organisations were sanctioned for unlawful employee video surveillance. Under the simplified procedure, inadequate data security, failure to cooperate, and non-compliance with data subject rights requests each affected 14 organisations.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Japan's J-AISI Publishes Japanese-Language Guide Cataloguing Eleven AI System Attack Types
rows.title: Japan's J-AISI Publishes Japanese-Language Guide Cataloguing Eleven AI System Attack Types
rows.summary: On 6 February 2026, the Japan AI Safety Institute (J-AISI) published a Japanese-language guidance document cataloguing eleven categories of attacks against AI systems. Authors Naoto Kiribuchi, Kengo Zenitani and Takayuki Semitsu of J-AISI and IPA produced the guide, designated a detailed Japanese translation of a prior J-AISI reference corresponding to arXiv:2506.23296v1. The document covers both predictive and generative AI systems, mapping attack types including model poisoning, prompt injection and rowhammer attacks to confidentiality, integrity and availability impacts. J-AISI directed the guide at researchers, developers, security practitioners and policymakers, including those without specialist AI security expertise. The document references NIST AI 100-2e2025 and MITRE ATLAS as contextual frameworks.

rows.headline: France's CNIL Publishes Eight-Workstream Economic Analysis Programme for 2026–2028
rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Eight-Workstream Economic Analysis Programme for 2026–2028
rows.summary: On 2 February 2026, France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its economic analysis work programme covering 2026 to 2028. The programme sets out eight workstreams, including competition and contestability in digital markets, further quantification of GDPR benefits for businesses and individuals, integration of economic elements into sanction-setting methodology, and analysis of generative artificial intelligence's economic impact across its value chain. The CNIL established its economic analysis unit in early 2023. On prior GDPR economic impact work, the CNIL found that compliance costs were real but largely temporary and lower than anticipated, and that research shows no evidence the regulation reduced turnover or profitability.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Bermuda PrivCom Co-Sponsors Two AI Resolutions at 47th Global Privacy Assembly
rows.title: Bermuda PrivCom Co-Sponsors Two AI Resolutions at 47th Global Privacy Assembly
rows.summary: On 29 January 2026, Bermuda's Office of the Privacy Commissioner published details of its co-sponsorship of two resolutions adopted at the 47th Global Privacy Assembly, which convened in Seoul in September 2025. The first resolution, led by the Australian Information Commissioner, affirms that AI training must comply with existing data protection laws and warns against indiscriminate scraping of publicly available personal data. The second resolution, submitted by Canada's Privacy Commissioner, reaffirms that individuals must not be subject to fully automated decisions without meaningful human review. Both resolutions call for clear governance frameworks and stronger cross-border enforcement cooperation among the assembly's 130-plus member authorities.

rows.headline: Sweden's IMY Fines Sportadmin SEK 6 Million for Article 32 Breach After 2.1 Million Records Exposed
rows.title: Sweden's IMY Fines Sportadmin SEK 6 Million for Article 32 Breach After 2.1 Million Records Exposed
rows.summary: On 28 January 2026, Sweden's Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten (IMY) fined sports administration platform Sportadmin i Skandinavien AB SEK 6 million (EUR 530,000) for violating Article 32 GDPR. A cyber attack in January 2025 exposed personal data of more than 2.1 million individuals, predominantly children and young people, including names, contact details, personal identity numbers, health data, and sports club affiliations. Attackers subsequently published the data on the Darknet. IMY found that Sportadmin had been aware of system weaknesses before the attack, lacked routines for detecting security deficiencies, and operated no real-time intrusion detection system.

rows.headline: Philippines NPC Opens Applications for APEC and Global CBPR Accountability Agents
rows.title: Philippines NPC Opens Applications for APEC and Global CBPR Accountability Agents
rows.summary: On 27 January 2026, the Philippine National Privacy Commission opened applications for recognition as an Accountability Agent under both the APEC Cross-Border Privacy Rules System and the Global CBPR System. The initial application period ran from 27 January to 27 February 2026, later extended to 20 March 2026. The nominations period was extended to 16 April 2026. Applicants must submit separate applications for each system, as recognition under one does not confer recognition under the other. Accountability Agents certify whether participating organisations meet CBPR requirements.

rows.headline: Andalusia's CTPDA Launches Responsible AI Methodology Tool for Public Administrations
rows.title: Andalusia's CTPDA Launches Responsible AI Methodology Tool for Public Administrations
rows.summary: On 26 January 2026, the Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía published a methodological tool to support Andalusian public administrations in deploying AI systems in compliance with the GDPR and the EU AI Act. The tool provides a step-by-step guide that adapts to each project's type and complexity. It applies graduated levels of scrutiny, ranging from simple document management to higher-risk systems making decisions on pupil curricular adaptation or identifying isolated older adults. The tool targets smaller public bodies lacking the resources to build internal compliance frameworks independently.
rows.authority: Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía
rows.body_name: Consejo de Transparencia y Protección de Datos de Andalucía

rows.headline: EU's BEREC Welcomes Digital Networks Act Proposal, Confirms Technical Advisory Role
rows.title: EU's BEREC Welcomes Digital Networks Act Proposal, Confirms Technical Advisory Role
rows.summary: On 22 January 2026, the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications (BEREC) issued a statement welcoming the European Commission's Digital Networks Act (DNA) proposal. The DNA consolidates several existing instruments, including the European Electronic Communications Code, the BEREC Regulation, the Open Internet Regulation, the ePrivacy Directive and the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme. BEREC stated that core ex-ante market regulation principles must be retained where market failures persist. BEREC confirmed it will examine the proposals and advise legislators throughout the process. It will host its annual Stakeholder Forum on 31 March 2026, where Executive Vice-President Henna Virkkunen will deliver a keynote address.
rows.authority: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications
rows.body_name: Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications

rows.headline: EU EDPB Adopts LED Evaluation Report and Processor BCR Recommendations
rows.title: EU EDPB Adopts LED Evaluation Report and Processor BCR Recommendations
rows.summary: On 15 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted two instruments: a contribution to the European Commission's evaluation of Directive 2016/680 and Recommendations 1/2026 on processor binding corporate rules. The LED report identifies gaps including unclear scope at the GDPR boundary, AI-related challenges in law enforcement, and under-resourced supervisory authorities. Recommendations 1/2026 consolidate WP265 and WP257 into a single revised document governing BCR-ST approvals. The EDPB confirmed that BCR-ST constitute a transfer tool under Article 46 GDPR and that processor members holding BCR-ST need not conclude separate sub-processing contracts with other covered group members. The Commission must submit its own evaluation to the European Parliament and Council by 6 May 2026.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: UK ICO Signs Memorandum of Understanding With His Majesty's Government on Data Protection Standards
rows.title: UK ICO Signs Memorandum of Understanding With His Majesty's Government on Data Protection Standards
rows.summary: On 8 January 2026, the UK Information Commissioner's Office and His Majesty's Government signed a Memorandum of Understanding committing the government to raising data protection standards. The MOU formalises the relationship between the ICO and HM Government, setting out shared expectations for how government bodies will handle personal data. The agreement marks a formal commitment by government to meet data protection obligations and work cooperatively with the ICO. The ICO published a statement about the signing on 15 January 2026.

rows.headline: UK ICO Fines Two Companies GBP 225,000 for Unlawful Unsolicited Marketing Messages
rows.title: UK ICO Fines Two Companies GBP 225,000 for Unlawful Unsolicited Marketing Messages
