rows.headline: HmbBfDI Confirms Court Ruling on Guest Checkout Exception for Online Marketplaces
rows.title: HmbBfDI Confirms Court Ruling on Guest Checkout Exception for Online Marketplaces
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has confirmed that the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht Hamburg upheld a ruling that online marketplace operators such as Otto.de are not required to offer guest checkout where compensatory data minimisation measures are in place (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR).

On 12 March 2025, the HmbBfDI published a press release reporting that the Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht Hamburg ruled on 27 February 2025 (Az. 5 U 30/24) in favour of Otto.de, dismissing an action by Verbraucherzentrale Nordrhein-Westfalen seeking to compel guest ordering. The court confirmed a first-instance judgment of the Landgericht Hamburg.

The HmbBfDI finds that a general rule established by the Konferenz der unabhängigen Datenschutzaufsichtsbehörden des Bundes und der Länder (DSK) on 24 March 2022 requires online retailers to offer guest ordering to comply with the data minimisation principle. The authority notes that compelling customers to create permanent accounts is typically incompatible with data protection law, as such accounts contain unnecessary personal data and expose information to security risks.

The HmbBfDI determines that Otto.de satisfied conditions for an exception to the guest checkout obligation as an online marketplace hosting third-party sellers. The authority finds that Otto.de demonstrated that customer service, including returns organisation, operates more efficiently through a unified customer account. The HmbBfDI notes that Otto.de adopted alternative data minimisation measures, including automated account deletion following inactivity and data removal after warranty expiry. The authority states that whilst the DSK resolution is not binding on courts, both judicial decisions treated it as soft law. The HmbBfDI finds that other platforms claiming marketplace status must substantiate that claim with concrete usage data, as general assertions do not establish entitlement to the exception.
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: CNIL Publishes GDPR Recommendations for AI System Development
rows.title: CNIL Publishes GDPR Recommendations for AI System Development
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its first set of recommendations on the application of the General Data Protection Regulation to the development of artificial intelligence systems, covering purpose definition, controller responsibilities, and training data use.

On 22 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published guidance directed at designers and developers of AI systems, addressing how GDPR obligations apply during the development phase, defined as conception, dataset compilation, and model training, prior to deployment. No fine was issued; the publication is a guidance document carrying no monetary penalty.

The CNIL determines that the requirement to establish a lawful purpose (Art. 5(1)(b) GDPR) applies to AI development and is not incompatible with the nature of machine learning, including systems that may develop unanticipated characteristics during training. The authority finds that purpose must be determined, explicit, and legitimate, and that an objective stated as broadly as "development and improvement of an AI system" is insufficient. For general-purpose AI systems, the CNIL states that developers are required to specify the type of system being built, its technically foreseeable capabilities, foreseeable high-risk functionalities, functionalities excluded by design, and known deployment conditions such as open-source release, commercialisation, or API availability. For AI developed for scientific research purposes, the authority finds that a less detailed initial purpose definition is permissible, provided that supplementary information is added as the project progresses.

The CNIL further determines that the authority finds organisations using personal data for AI training are required to identify their role under the GDPR, whether as controller, joint controller, or processor, as this determines the applicable compliance obligations (Art. 4(7), Art. 26, Art. 28 GDPR). The authority notes that the recommendations have been elaborated to complement the EU AI Act, adopted in summer 2024, given that both instruments apply concurrently when personal data are processed during AI development. The CNIL states that a checklist document has been made available alongside the recommendations to assist professionals in verifying compliance at each development stage.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: France PEReN and International Network Publish Joint Evaluation Report on Agentic AI Safety Testing Methodologies
rows.title: France PEReN and International Network Publish Joint Evaluation Report on Agentic AI Safety Testing Methodologies
rows.summary:
France's Pôle d'Expertise de la Régulation Numérique (PEReN), alongside AI Safety Institutes and government-mandated offices from Singapore, Japan, Australia, Canada, the European Commission, Kenya, South Korea and the UK AI Security Institute, has published a joint evaluation report advancing methodologies for testing AI agents across risk domains including sensitive information leakage, fraud and cybersecurity.

On 17 July 2025, the International Network published the report, titled "Advancing Methodologies for Agentic Evaluations Across Domains", following a joint testing exercise conducted across the participating jurisdictions. The exercise was structured around two strands: leakage of sensitive information and fraud, led by Singapore's AI Safety Institute, and cybersecurity evaluations, led by the UK AI Security Institute.

The report notes that, given the nascency of agentic testing, test results and individual model capabilities are not the primary focus of the exercise. Rather, the network finds that the intent is to identify methodological issues in conducting agentic testing in order to inform future evaluation efforts. The exercise assessed two key aspects of agent safety testing: how safe models are when operating as agents, and how effective models are as judges of agent behaviour. The network finds that two models were tested as agents, with two further models serving as judges, and that human annotation was subsequently conducted with judge-LLM evaluations available as a reference point.

Across the leakage and fraud strand, the report finds that tasks covering two risk categories were assessed in eight languages, with agents provided with a selection of tools they could call to complete assigned tasks. The network finds that agent trajectories, comprising task prompts, tool calls with reasoning and final outputs, were evaluated on a pass or fail basis tied to specific scenarios. For malicious tasks, the report identifies two failure scenarios: complete execution of a harmful activity and partial execution of a harmful activity. The report finds that metrics centred on pass rates for tested models and discrepancy rates between judge-LLM evaluations and human annotation. Language-specific analysis was conducted across Farsi, French, Hindi, Japanese, Kiswahili, Korean, Mandarin Chinese and Telugu.

rows.headline: CNIL Sets Out GDPR Requirements for Bank App Call-Detection Anti-Fraud Tools
rows.title: CNIL Sets Out GDPR Requirements for Bank App Call-Detection Anti-Fraud Tools
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published guidance stating that banking applications which detect whether a customer is engaged in a telephone call during a transaction must comply with the General Data Protection Regulation, requiring freely given and informed consent before accessing call-related data.

On 15 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published a public information notice addressing the collection of telephone call data, specifically call existence and call duration, by certain banking applications. The CNIL determines that such data constitutes personal data and that its collection is therefore subject to the RGPD (GDPR), including its consent requirements under Art. 7 GDPR.

The CNIL finds that the purpose of these tools is to detect in real time whether a customer may be acting under the influence of a fraudster during sensitive operations such as adding a new payee or initiating a transfer. The authority notes that the mechanism does not listen to or record the content of telephone conversations, but detects only whether a call is in progress. The CNIL determines that, where a bank deems a situation abnormal, the application may display an alert or, depending on the bank's configuration, block the operation entirely.

The CNIL states that banks are required to obtain free and informed consent before accessing call-related information, and that refusal of consent must not result in degraded service or increased charges. Where a customer declines, the authority finds that access to non-sensitive features such as account consultation must remain available within the application, while higher-risk functions such as transfers or payee additions may be restricted. The CNIL recommends that commonly used operations, including transfers, remain accessible via the bank's website, with less frequent operations available by telephone or in branch. The authority further states that customers who have given consent are entitled to withdraw it at any time via a clear and accessible mechanism, such as a link within the application. The CNIL acknowledges that banks operating under European anti-fraud obligations may lawfully refuse certain erasure or objection requests where retention is legally required, and states that banks must clearly explain any such refusal. Customers who remain dissatisfied are directed to lodge a complaint with the CNIL.
rows.authority: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.headline: Nigeria's NDPC Fines MultiChoice $500,000 for Cross-Border Data Transfers
rows.title: Nigeria's NDPC Fines MultiChoice $500,000 for Cross-Border Data Transfers
rows.summary: On 6 July 2025, the Nigeria Data Protection Commission imposed a ₦766,242,500 sanction on MultiChoice Nigeria for breaching the Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023. The investigation, which began in Q2 2024, found unauthorised processing of personal data of subscribers and non-subscribers, plus cross-border transfer of Nigerians' data abroad without due process. The Commission has ordered a wider investigation across all MultiChoice data collection outlets in Nigeria.

rows.headline: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark Issues Sector-Specific NIS 2 Guidance for Danish Municipalities
rows.title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark Issues Sector-Specific NIS 2 Guidance for Danish Municipalities
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark, operating through the Agency for Public Security (Styrelsen for Samfundssikkerhed), has published sector-specific guidance on the application of the Danish NIS 2 Act to the country's 98 municipalities, addressing scope, supervisory arrangements and risk-based security measures.

On 1 July 2025, the Agency for Public Security published the first edition of its sector-specific NIS 2 guidance for municipalities, forming part of Denmark's implementation of the EU NIS 2 Directive. The agency states that the guidance does not carry binding legal force; the agency states that it is the supervisory authority in each case that determines whether an entity meets its obligations under the NIS 2 Act.

The agency notes that Danish municipalities are established as independent entities with their own political and administrative leadership, and that their responsibilities span multiple sectors, from schools and childcare institutions to technical and environmental services. The agency finds that entities associated with a municipality to varying degrees, including municipal companies, independent care homes and similar bodies, may fall within the scope of the NIS 2 Act if they provide services within one or more of the sectors listed in Annexes I and II of the Act. The agency further finds that such entities are subject to the competent authority for the sector in which they operate, rather than to the municipal supervisory framework directly.

On the question of scope, the agency explains that a municipality has relatively broad discretion in how it organises its service delivery. Some tasks are carried out within the municipality itself, while others are handled by affiliated entities such as joint municipal communities (section 60 communities) covering waste incineration and natural gas supply, or by limited companies. The agency notes that an entity under the NIS 2 Act is defined as a natural or legal person established and recognised under national law, capable of exercising rights and assuming obligations in its own name, encompassing businesses, associations, organisations and public authorities. The agency states that it does not address NIS 2 legislation applicable to the telecommunications, energy or financial sectors, and directs readers to the cross-sectoral NIS 2 guidance for general measures, including requirements for cybersecurity risk management under a risk-based approach.

rows.headline: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark Rates Cyber Espionage and Cybercrime Threats to Danish Space Sector as Very High
rows.title: Centre for Cyber Security Denmark Rates Cyber Espionage and Cybercrime Threats to Danish Space Sector as Very High
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cyber Security Denmark, operating under the Agency for Societal Security (SAMSIK), has published its first threat assessment for the Danish space sector, rating the threats from both cyber espionage and cybercrime as VERY HIGH, with the assessment explicitly contextualised within the NIS2 Directive, which SAMSIK notes also covers the space domain.

On 26 June 2025, SAMSIK published the threat assessment titled "Cybertruslen mod Rumsektoren" (The Cyber Threat Against the Space Sector), marking the first time the authority has produced a dedicated assessment for this sector. SAMSIK states the document is intended to support risk owners in the Danish space sector and forms part of the basis for risk assessment work across the sector.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cyber espionage against the Danish space sector as VERY HIGH. The authority finds that foreign states, in particular Russia and China, conduct cyber espionage against space sector organisations with the aim of gaining insight into other states' space activities, acquiring new and innovative technology, and obtaining knowledge of national defence capabilities. SAMSIK highlights examples of cyber espionage directed at space organisations in Japan and the United States as illustrative of this pattern. The authority further finds that the dual-use nature of space technology, serving both civilian and military purposes, sustains a persistent foreign state interest in the sector.

SAMSIK assesses the threat from cybercrime against the Danish space sector as VERY HIGH, with the authority finding that criminal actors primarily employ extortion techniques, including compromising networks and encrypting valuable data, or stealing data and threatening to publish it. The authority assesses the threat from cyber activism, principally distributed denial-of-service attacks, as MEDIUM, a level SAMSIK attributes to cyber activists likely placing lower symbolic value on the space sector compared with other sectors. The authority assesses the threat from destructive cyber attacks as MEDIUM. SAMSIK finds that this threat comes primarily from Russia, which the authority states has demonstrated increased willingness since early 2024 to employ hybrid methods with destructive effects against European NATO members, including destructive cyber attacks. The authority assesses the threat from cyber terrorism against the Danish space sector as NONE, finding that militant extremists currently lack the requisite capabilities and that intent is extremely limited.

rows.headline: ENISA publishes updated European Cybersecurity Skills Framework consolidating 12 professional role profiles for EU workforce alignment
rows.title: ENISA publishes updated European Cybersecurity Skills Framework consolidating 12 professional role profiles for EU workforce alignment
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published updated materials for the European Cybersecurity Skills Framework (ECSF), consolidating 12 cybersecurity professional role profiles intended to serve as the EU reference point for defining and assessing cybersecurity skills across the public and private sectors.

On 26 June 2025, ENISA published the current state of the ECSF, a practical framework tool originally presented at the first ENISA Cybersecurity Skills Conference in September 2022. The framework carries no monetary penalty and involves no fine amounts. ENISA describes the ECSF as an open European tool designed to establish a common understanding of cybersecurity professional role profiles, alongside clear mappings to the skills, knowledge and competences required for each.

ENISA finds that the ECSF summarises cybersecurity-related roles into 12 distinct profiles, each individually analysed according to responsibilities, skills, synergies and interdependencies. The agency states that the framework facilitates recognition of cybersecurity skills and supports the design of cybersecurity-related training programmes. The Commission's April 2023 Communication on a Cybersecurity Skills Academy designated the ECSF as the basis upon which the Academy will define and assess relevant skills, monitor the evolution of skill gaps and provide indications on emerging needs.

ENISA notes that the 3rd Skills Conference, hosted under the Hungarian Presidency of the Council of the EU, confirmed the framework's maturity and versatility, demonstrating how the ECSF supports workforce planning aligned with obligations under the NIS2 Directive, adapts to the AI domain and enables skills attestation for cybersecurity professionals. The agency further states that the ECSF 2025 workshop showcased how EU co-funded projects are leveraging the framework across diverse sectors and use cases, supporting harmonisation of cybersecurity skills and professional roles across the EU. ENISA has also published a mapping between the ECSF and ESCO, the multilingual classification of European Skills, Competences and Occupations, as well as an application of the ECSF to the aviation sector developed by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency. ENISA notes that NIS2 obligations are mapped to ECSF roles within the accompanying documentation, and the framework is available in interactive, xlsx and JSON formats.

rows.headline: ICO Places PECR Guide Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act Coming into Force
rows.title: ICO Places PECR Guide Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations under review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force in the United Kingdom.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Guide to the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations is subject to review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into law on that date. The ICO notes that the guidance may be subject to change and directs organisations to its Plans for New and Updated Guidance page for information on which guidance will be updated and when.

The ICO states that the guide is intended for organisations that send electronic marketing messages by phone, fax, email or text, use cookies, or provide electronic communication services to the public. The guide explains how to apply the Regulations through practical examples and answers to frequently asked questions. The ICO confirms that, pending the review, the existing guidance remains the current reference point for compliance with the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations.

The ICO's announcement reflects the broader legislative change introduced by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which the authority identifies as the basis for placing existing PECR guidance under review. The ICO has not specified a timetable for the completion of that review but indicates that updates will be communicated through its dedicated guidance plans page. The ICO has not announced any amendments to the substantive obligations under the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations in connection with this review notice.

rows.headline: ICO Places Children's Code Guidance Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Coming into Force
rows.title: ICO Places Children's Code Guidance Under Review Following Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 Coming into Force
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has placed its Children's code guidance and associated resources under formal review following the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on 19 June 2025, with the ICO noting that existing materials may be subject to change.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office announced that its Children's code guidance, which covers the age-appropriate design code of practice for online services likely to be accessed by children, is under review as a consequence of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 entering into law on that date. The ICO notes that no financial penalty is associated with this announcement.

The ICO states that the Children's code sets out fifteen standards with which providers of information society services likely to be accessed by children, including apps, online games, and social media sites, are required to conform. The ICO notes that the guidance applies to organisations of all sizes and across all sectors. The ICO states that resources available under the existing framework include a self-assessment risk toolkit enabling organisations to assess how both the UK GDPR and the Children's code apply to their digital services, Data Protection Impact Assessment templates, and sector-specific guidance covering edtech, the digital news industry, and games designers.

The ICO further states that its published Children's Code Strategy, together with a progress update issued in March 2025, sets out the authority's enforcement priorities and actions taken to date in relation to children's privacy online. The ICO's Commissioner's opinion on age assurance details how the Commissioner expects businesses to meet the code's age-appropriate application standard and sets out expectations for age assurance data protection compliance. The ICO also notes that it has co-signed a joint statement on a common international approach to age assurance, establishing shared principles across signatory regulators. The ICO states that its plans for new and updated guidance page is the reference point for information on which guidance will be updated and the timeline for those updates.

rows.headline: ICO Publishes AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit Version 1.1
rows.title: ICO Publishes AI and Data Protection Risk Toolkit Version 1.1
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published version 1.1 of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, providing a practical spreadsheet-based resource for organisations processing personal data through AI systems under the UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released an updated iteration of its AI and data protection risk toolkit, available for download as a Microsoft Excel file. The publication coincides with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its broader AI and data protection guidance is currently under review in light of that legislation and may be subject to change, with updates to be signalled via the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the toolkit is designed to provide practical support to organisations seeking to reduce risks to individuals' rights and freedoms arising from their own AI systems. The resource is framed as supplementary material sitting alongside the ICO's existing guidance on AI and data protection, which the ICO states addresses obligations under the UK GDPR including requirements relating to lawful basis, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and accountability (Art. 5, Art. 6, Art. 24 UK GDPR). The ICO presents the toolkit as a means of helping organisations work through data protection risk considerations in a structured, documented format consistent with the accountability principle.

The ICO confirms it is no longer seeking case study submissions from organisations in connection with the toolkit, contrary to an earlier call for contributions referenced in accompanying guidance materials. The toolkit is offered as a downloadable spreadsheet compatible with Microsoft Excel or equivalent programmes, and the ICO notes that organisations on networks that restrict access to video platforms such as Vimeo may be unable to view associated instructional video content and are advised to use an alternative device. The ICO states that no specific compliance deadline or enforcement context is attached to the toolkit's publication.

rows.headline: ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on AI and Data Protection, Adding New Chapters on Fairness and Transparency
rows.title: ICO Publishes Updated Guidance on AI and Data Protection, Adding New Chapters on Fairness and Transparency
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published updated guidance on artificial intelligence and data protection under the UK GDPR, restructuring existing content and introducing new chapters on fairness, transparency, and lawfulness in AI systems.

On 15 March 2023, the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) released a revised version of its Guidance on AI and Data Protection, with the document remaining subject to further review following the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025. The ICO states that the guidance carries no financial penalty and imposes no fines; it is a compliance guidance instrument addressing obligations under the UK GDPR.

The ICO states that the update was issued in response to requests from UK industry for clarification on fairness requirements in AI, and that it delivers on a commitment under the ICO25 strategic plan to help organisations adopt new technologies whilst protecting individuals and vulnerable groups. The ICO finds that the guidance has been restructured around data protection principles, which provides a more editorially coherent and operationally sustainable framework given the pace of technological development.

The ICO notes that the revised guidance introduces several new and reorganised chapters. The ICO states that a standalone chapter on transparency sets out how the transparency principle (Art. 5(1)(a) UK GDPR) applies to AI, with detailed explainability content retained within the ICO's separate Explaining Decisions Made with AI product. The ICO notes that a new chapter on lawfulness includes added content on AI-generated inferences, affinity groups, and special category data (Art. 9 UK GDPR). The ICO states that it also introduces a dedicated fairness chapter covering the distinction between data protection fairness, algorithmic fairness, bias, and discrimination, as well as technical approaches to bias mitigation and the relationship between automated decision-making safeguards and fairness considerations (Art. 22 UK GDPR). The ICO notes that a new Annex A addresses fairness across the full AI lifecycle, from problem formulation through to decommissioning, identifying sources of bias arising from underlying assumptions, target variable selection, and reliance on quantifiable proxies. The ICO states that statistical accuracy content, previously located within the lawfulness and fairness chapter, has been relocated to a dedicated accuracy chapter reflecting its closer alignment with the accuracy principle (Art. 5(1)(d) UK GDPR). The ICO notes the guidance will continue to be updated to reflect forthcoming regulatory reform and the UK government's pro-innovation approach to AI regulation.

rows.headline: ICO Issues Guidance on Personal Data Use in Political Campaigning Under UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR
rows.title: ICO Issues Guidance on Personal Data Use in Political Campaigning Under UK GDPR, DPA 2018 and PECR
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published guidance on the use of personal data in political campaigning, setting out compliance expectations for controllers under the UK General Data Protection Regulation, the Data Protection Act 2018 and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (PECR).

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published its finalised guidance for the use of personal data in political campaigning, following a consultation launched in October 2019 on a draft framework code of practice. The ICO notes that the guidance is currently under review in light of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025, which came into force on the same date, and may be subject to change. The ICO states that no fines are associated with this guidance publication.

The ICO states that the guidance is directed at controllers processing personal data for political campaigning purposes, defined as activity in support of, or against, a political party, a referendum campaign, a candidate standing for election, or the success or failure of a recall petition. The ICO confirms this includes registered political parties, candidates, referendum campaigners, non-party campaigners and recall petition campaigners as defined under the Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000 (ss. 23, 88, 105), the Representation of the People Act 1983 (s. 118A), and the Recall of MPs Act 2015 (Schedule 3, Part 5). The ICO notes that the guidance does not apply to internal party processes such as leadership elections.

The ICO finds that political campaigning has become increasingly sophisticated through the use of digital technologies and commercial marketing techniques, and that the often invisible nature of these techniques risks undermining public trust in the democratic process. The ICO states that the guidance does not introduce any new obligations or responsibilities beyond those already imposed by existing data protection and electronic marketing law, but is intended to provide clarity and practical advice to ensure that all organisations involved in political campaigning handle personal data in a manner compliant with the UK GDPR, the DPA 2018 and PECR, regardless of the method of communication or future technological developments.

rows.headline: ICO Publishes Sample DPIA for Children's Mobile Gaming Apps Under Age-Appropriate Design Code
rows.title: ICO Publishes Sample DPIA for Children's Mobile Gaming Apps Under Age-Appropriate Design Code
rows.summary:
The Information Commissioner's Office has published a sample Data Protection Impact Assessment for a fictional mobile gaming application aimed at children in the United Kingdom, intended to illustrate good practice compliance with Standard 2 of the Children's Code (Age-Appropriate Design Code) under UK GDPR.

On 19 June 2025, the Information Commissioner's Office published the sample DPIA, coinciding with the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 coming into force on the same date. The ICO notes that its guidance, including this resource, is under review and may be subject to change as a result of that legislation, with updates to be tracked on the ICO's Plans for New and Updated Guidance page.

The ICO states that the sample DPIA is adapted from its own DPIA template and is intended to help companies developing mobile gaming apps for children in the UK understand and apply Standard 2 of the Children's Code, which the ICO notes requires Information Society Services likely to be accessed by children under the age of 18 to carry out a DPIA (Art. 35 UK GDPR). The document uses a fictional product, the Cooking Numbers mobile app, developed by the controller named as The Mobile Game Company, based on a product specification commissioned from Fundamentally Games for demonstration purposes only. The ICO confirms that the fictional product will not be developed into an actual game.

The ICO states that the sample follows the process set out in its DPIA guidance and the Children's Code, and recommends reading it alongside the code's dedicated DPIA guidance and the criteria for an acceptable DPIA established in European guidelines. The ICO further notes that its design guidance, which includes gaming-specific worked examples of data privacy moments and age-appropriate mindsets developed from the Fundamentally Games product specification, is available to assist practitioners in identifying what data is being processed and any associated risks. The authority invites feedback and recommendations for improvements to the sample DPIA by email.

rows.title: New guidance to help smart product manufacturers get data protection right
rows.summary: We are calling on all manufacturers and developers of smart products to prioritise people’s privacy with new guidance published today (16 June).

rows.headline: European Court of Human Rights Finds Russia Violated Articles 8, 10 and 11 in Selishcheva and Others v. Russia
rows.title: European Court of Human Rights Finds Russia Violated Articles 8, 10 and 11 in Selishcheva and Others v. Russia
rows.summary:
The European Court of Human Rights has found that the Russian Federation violated Articles 8, 10 and 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights through unlawful collection of political data and denial of electoral candidacy based on vague extremist provisions.

On 27 May 2025, the European Court of Human Rights delivered its judgment in Selishcheva and Others v. Russia (Applications nos. 39056/22 and nine others), concerning ten Russian nationals refused registration as candidates in Berdsk municipal elections in 2021.

Under Article 8, the Court finds that police authorities collected and stored the applicants' political data without a clear legal framework. The Court finds that the applicants had no means to access or challenge the data, no retention periods or deletion rules existed, and no independent review mechanism was in place. The Court finds the interference was not "in accordance with law" and not "necessary in a democratic society."

Regarding Articles 10 and 11, the Court finds that electoral candidacy was denied under Federal Law no. 157-FZ on the basis of alleged "involvement" with organisations designated as extremist. The Court finds that the applicants' activities, including participation in protests and provision of legal advice to detained protesters, occurred when the relevant organisations operated legally. The extremist designation was issued on 9 June 2021, after the conduct in question. The Court finds that "involvement" was impermissibly vague, no meaningful judicial interpretation existed to limit its scope, and no genuine extremist links were established. The Court finds the measures imposed a retroactive burden and created a chilling effect on political participation, and were neither "prescribed by law" nor "necessary in a democratic society."

rows.headline: CJEU Orders Bulgaria to Pay Lump Sum for Failure to Transpose Open Data Directive 2019/1024
rows.title: CJEU Orders Bulgaria to Pay Lump Sum for Failure to Transpose Open Data Directive 2019/1024
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union has ruled against the Republic of Bulgaria in infringement proceedings brought by the European Commission for failure to transpose Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information, ordering payment of a lump sum under Article 260(3) TFEU.

On 22 May 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union (Tenth Chamber) delivered its judgment in Case C-237/23, European Commission v Republic of Bulgaria. The Commission had initiated proceedings under Article 258 TFEU on 14 April 2023, seeking a declaration that Bulgaria had failed to adopt the legislative, regulatory and administrative provisions necessary to comply with Directive 2019/1024, or had at minimum failed to communicate those measures to the Commission, in breach of Article 17 of that Directive. The Commission also sought a lump sum calculated at EUR 1,800 per day from the expiry of the transposition deadline, subject to a minimum of EUR 504,000, and a penalty payment of EUR 9,720 per day for any continuing infringement beyond the date of judgment.

The Court finds that Bulgaria failed to fulfil its obligations under Article 17 of Directive 2019/1024, which required Member States to bring into force the necessary transposition measures. The Court notes that the Commission sought the higher of two lump sum amounts: the daily rate of EUR 1,800 multiplied by the number of days of infringement, or the minimum flat sum of EUR 504,000. The proceedings record a partial withdrawal by the Commission, indicating that Bulgaria had taken some transposition steps during the course of the litigation, though the Court's ruling confirms the infringement was established.

Directive 2019/1024, adopted on 20 June 2019, replaced Directive 2003/98/EC and updated the framework governing re-use of public sector information, including provisions addressing real-time access to dynamic data, extension of re-use obligations to public undertakings and research bodies, and considerations relevant to artificial intelligence applications. The Court finds that Bulgaria's failure to communicate complete transposition measures within the prescribed deadline engaged the lump sum mechanism under Article 260(3) TFEU. The judgment records that costs were also sought against Bulgaria by the Commission.

rows.headline: Court of Justice Rules Netherlands Failed to Transpose Open Data Directive 2019/1024, Orders Lump Sum Payment
rows.title: Court of Justice Rules Netherlands Failed to Transpose Open Data Directive 2019/1024, Orders Lump Sum Payment
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union (Tenth Chamber) has ruled that the Kingdom of the Netherlands failed to fulfil its obligations under Directive (EU) 2019/1024 on open data and the re-use of public sector information by not adopting and immediately communicating the necessary transposition measures, ordering the payment of a lump sum under Art. 260(3) TFEU.

On 22 May 2025, the Court of Justice of the European Union delivered its judgment in Case C-213/23, European Commission v Kingdom of the Netherlands. The infringement proceedings were brought by the European Commission under Art. 258 TFEU and Art. 260(3) TFEU, following the Netherlands' failure to transpose Directive 2019/1024 by the prescribed deadline and to communicate those measures to the Commission as required by Art. 17 of that Directive. The Commission had initially sought a lump sum calculated at EUR 13,900 per day from the expiry of the transposition deadline until the infringement was remedied, subject to a minimum lump sum of EUR 3,892,000, as well as a daily penalty of EUR 75,060 from the date of judgment if the infringement persisted.

The Court finds that the Netherlands failed to adopt the legislative, regulatory and administrative provisions necessary to comply with Directive 2019/1024 and failed to communicate those provisions to the Commission immediately, in breach of Art. 17 of the Directive. The judgment notes that the Commission filed a partial withdrawal, indicating that elements of the infringement had been remedied during the course of proceedings, a circumstance the Court takes into account in its assessment of the financial penalties to be imposed under Art. 260(3) TFEU.

Directive 2019/1024, adopted on 20 June 2019, establishes rules facilitating the re-use of public sector information across the European Union, including provisions on real-time access to dynamic data and the supply of high-value datasets. The Court finds that the Netherlands' incomplete transposition and failure to communicate transposition measures constituted a failure of a Member State to fulfil its obligations, warranting the imposition of a lump sum payment.

rows.headline: Indonesia Adopts First Age-Appropriate Design Regulation in Asia, Drawing on IEEE Standards
rows.title: Indonesia Adopts First Age-Appropriate Design Regulation in Asia, Drawing on IEEE Standards
rows.summary:
Indonesia has enacted a binding government regulation on the governance of electronic systems in child protection, drawing on frameworks developed with input from the IEEE Standards Association (IEEE SA), establishing enforceable requirements for digital platforms operating in the country.

On 14 May 2025, the IEEE Standards Association announced the culmination of its collaboration with Indonesian policymakers on the regulation, described by IEEE SA as the first age-appropriate design regulation of its kind in Asia and the Global South. No financial penalties are specified in the source material; the regulation establishes compliance obligations rather than a fining regime.

The regulation requires online service providers to prioritise the best interests of children in platform design and practices, building on the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and its General Comment No. 25. The regulation provides that any digital product or service likely to be accessed by children is required to offer a high level of privacy by default. The regulation further prohibits the profiling and precise geolocation of children, and bans manipulative design practices, including nudging users to weaken privacy settings or collecting more personal data than is necessary for the service.

IEEE SA states that the regulation is closely aligned with the UK Age-Appropriate Design Code and California's equivalent framework. IEEE SA notes that the regulation incorporates provisions drawn from IEEE 2089, the IEEE Standard for Age Appropriate Digital Services Framework, and IEEE 2089.1, the IEEE Standard for Online Age Verification. IEEE SA notes that by embedding provisions from these standards, the regulation requires online services to follow internationally vetted guidelines for protecting children's data and online experiences. IEEE SA also states that it has launched a certification programme to assist industry in conforming to age verification best practices consistent with those standards.

IEEE SA states that children now represent approximately one third of all internet users worldwide, and that the regulatory engagement in Indonesia is intended to serve as a model for collaborative policy development between technical bodies, governments, and civil society. IEEE SA has separately announced the launch of a Technology Policy Collaborative to extend similar advisory engagement to other jurisdictions on digital governance matters.

rows.headline: European Commission Opens Public Consultation on EU Action Plan for Hospital and Healthcare Cybersecurity; Czech NÚKIB Encourages Participation
rows.title: European Commission Opens Public Consultation on EU Action Plan for Hospital and Healthcare Cybersecurity; Czech NÚKIB Encourages Participation
rows.summary:
The European Commission has launched a public consultation on the European Action Plan for the Cybersecurity of Hospitals and Healthcare Providers, published on 15 January 2025, with the Czech National Cyber and Information Security Authority (NÚKIB) drawing domestic attention to both the consultation and a related advisory board recruitment process.

On 14 May 2025, NÚKIB published a notice informing Czech entities that the European Commission had opened the consultation, which remains open until 30 June 2025. NÚKIB notes that it does not administer the consultation process, directing interested parties to the Commission's own digital strategy pages for submission details.

According to NÚKIB, the Action Plan identifies gaps and challenges in the healthcare sector and responds to them through specific initiatives at EU level, alongside recommendations directed at member states for action at national level. NÚKIB states that the plan sets out objectives across four areas: building capacity to prevent cybersecurity incidents; improving information sharing and threat detection capabilities; improving incident response and recovery capabilities; and identifying means of deterring potential threat actors. NÚKIB notes that contributions submitted through the public consultation may influence the final form of recommendations addressed to both EU-level entities and member states, as well as the activities envisaged under the Action Plan.

Separately, NÚKIB states that the European Commission has published a call for applications to a newly established Health Cybersecurity Advisory Board, intended to assist the Commission and the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) in implementing the Action Plan, and to support public-private sector cooperation. NÚKIB states that members of the board will be expected to, among other things, identify and share best practices in cybersecurity and share information with healthcare entities. The deadline for applications to the advisory board was 23 May 2025. NÚKIB states that it welcomes and supports potential interest in membership from Czech entities, noting that participation in the board may contribute to the prioritisation and effective implementation of activities set out in the Action Plan.

rows.headline: Hamburg DPA Issues Guidance on Data Act Obligations and Intersection with GDPR Ahead of September 2025 Application Date
rows.title: Hamburg DPA Issues Guidance on Data Act Obligations and Intersection with GDPR Ahead of September 2025 Application Date
rows.summary:
The Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit (HmbBfDI) has published guidance on the obligations imposed by Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 on harmonised rules on fair access to and use of data (the Data Act) and its interaction with the General Data Protection Regulation, ahead of the Act's application date of September 2025.

On 29 April 2025, the Hamburgische Beauftragte für Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit published an information paper setting out the core requirements of the Data Act and the implications for data protection practice within organisations. No fines are reported in this publication.

The HmbBfDI explains that the Data Act aims to break up existing monopolies over data generated by connected devices, covering the Internet of Things, including connected vehicles, household appliances, televisions, and medical and fitness devices. The authority notes that, under the current landscape, manufacturers routinely receive usage and wear data from such devices, while users and third parties such as independent repair workshops and spare parts producers have typically lacked access to that data. The Data Act, the HmbBfDI explains, is intended to enable both individual consumers and business customers who have purchased internet-connected products to determine for themselves to whom the data generated during use is disclosed, including through licence agreements with third parties.

On the relationship between the Data Act and the GDPR, the HmbBfDI finds that personal data may only be shared with third parties where data protection law does not preclude it (Art. 3(1) Data Act). The authority states that in many cases there will be no legal basis for the disclosure of personal business data, meaning that the core obligations of the Data Act will in practice predominantly concern non-personal data. The HmbBfDI determines that the assessment of whether a personal data element is present, and whether any disclosure would be compatible with the GDPR, can only meaningfully and bindingly be decided by the personnel within an organisation responsible for data protection. The authority further notes that its own role is expanding: where disputes arise between undertakings over access to customer data, the data protection authority may be required to direct that access be granted in accordance with the conditions of the Data Act. Where access is technically feasible and relevant, the HmbBfDI notes it is to be provided directly via an interface or customer portal (Art. 3(1) Data Act), with individual access requests required where that is not the case.
rows.authority: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Hamburgische Beauftragte fur Datenschutz und Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: Nigeria's FCCPC Tribunal Upholds $220 Million Penalty Against Meta and WhatsApp
rows.title: Nigeria's FCCPC Tribunal Upholds $220 Million Penalty Against Meta and WhatsApp
rows.summary: On 25 April 2025, Nigeria's Competition and Consumer Protection Tribunal affirmed the FCCPC's Final Order from 19 July 2024 imposing a $220 million administrative penalty on Meta Platforms and WhatsApp. The order followed a 38-month joint investigation with the NDPC into discriminatory and exploitative consumer practices, privacy practices and consumer data policies. The Tribunal also awarded $35,000 in investigation costs; payment due end of June 2025.
rows.authority: Nigeria Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Commission
rows.body_name: Nigeria Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Commission

rows.headline: Eight US State Privacy Regulators Form Consortium of Privacy Regulators via Memorandum of Understanding
rows.title: Eight US State Privacy Regulators Form Consortium of Privacy Regulators via Memorandum of Understanding
rows.summary:
Eight US state privacy regulators, including the California Privacy Protection Agency and state Attorneys General from seven states, have formed the Consortium of Privacy Regulators through a memorandum of understanding, establishing a bipartisan framework for coordinated privacy law implementation and enforcement across member jurisdictions.

On 16 April 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency announced the establishment of the Consortium of Privacy Regulators, comprising the CPPA and the Attorneys General of California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Indiana, New Jersey, and Oregon. The CPPA notes that no financial penalties are associated with this announcement.

The memorandum of understanding, as described in the CPPA's announcement, outlines the Consortium's goals as facilitating discussions of privacy law developments and shared priorities, with a focus on consumer protection across jurisdictions. The CPPA notes that the participating regulators established the Consortium to share expertise and resources, and to coordinate efforts to investigate potential violations of applicable laws. The CPPA states that the Consortium is to hold regular meetings and coordinate enforcement activity based on members' common interests.

The CPPA notes that although each member state operates under its own privacy law, the laws share certain fundamental features, including consumer rights to access, delete, and stop the sale of personal information, alongside comparable obligations on businesses. The CPPA finds that these commonalities provide the basis for coordinated application of privacy protections across jurisdictions. Michael Macko, the CPPA's head of enforcement, is quoted in the announcement stating that the Consortium reflects a shared commitment to advancing consistent and streamlined enforcement of privacy protections to address real-world privacy harms, citing data misuse affecting health information, location data, children's data, and identity information as areas of concern. The CPPA notes that consumer data is identified by the participating regulators as carrying immense value while also presenting risks of targeting, exploitation, and exposure of highly personal information.

rows.headline: Compensation company fined £90,000 for unlawful marketing calls
rows.title: Compensation company fined £90,000 for unlawful marketing calls
rows.summary:
We have fined AFK Letters Co Ltd (AFK) £90,000 for making more than 95,000 unsolicited marketing calls to people registered with the Telephone Preference Service (TPS), in a clear breach of electronic marketing laws. 

AFK Letters is a company which writes letters seeking compensation and refunds for its customers.

rows.headline: Statement on British Library’s 2023 ransomware attack
rows.title: Statement on British Library’s 2023 ransomware attack
rows.summary:
In October 2023, the British Library reported a ransomware attack to us, which escalated because of the lack of multi-factor authentication on an administrator account.  

Following the incident, the British Library published a cyber incident review in March 2024, which provided an overview of the cyber-attack and key lessons learnt to help other organisations that may experience similar incidents

rows.headline: Transparency must be the default position for water companies
rows.title: Transparency must be the default position for water companies
rows.summary: Public interest in water companies is higher than ever, with firms across the UK facing scrutiny over sewage discharges into lakes, rivers and the sea. Concerns about sewage pollution are mounting, as people have no choice but to rely on these companies.

rows.headline: Statement on police use of facial recognition technology (FRT)
rows.title: Statement on police use of facial recognition technology (FRT)
rows.summary: Facial recognition technology (FRT) can bring clear benefits in helping to prevent and detect crime, but it relies on processing large amounts of sensitive personal data. Therefore, its use must be necessary and proportionate, and its design must meet expectations of fairness and accuracy.

rows.headline: Review into use of children’s data by financial services
rows.title: Review into use of children’s data by financial services
rows.summary: More can be done by the financial services sector when dealing with children’s data.

rows.headline: AEPD Publishes PDPC Anonymisation Tool for Excel-Based Data Processing
rows.title: AEPD Publishes PDPC Anonymisation Tool for Excel-Based Data Processing
rows.summary:
The Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD) has published a downloadable anonymisation tool developed by the Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC), made available via the AEPD website to assist organisations in anonymising personal data in compliance with applicable data protection requirements.

On 9 April 2025, the AEPD published the PDPC Anonymisation Tool on its website, providing a freely downloadable Excel-based utility intended to support data anonymisation processes under the applicable data protection framework, including Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR).

The AEPD states that the tool is distributed as an encrypted archive to prevent antivirus software from quarantining the Excel spreadsheet file. The AEPD states that the archive is extractable using 7-Zip or equivalent software with the password "DIT-AEPD". The AEPD further notes that Microsoft Excel may prevent the execution of macros within the file, and that users are required to unblock the file manually by accessing the file properties and selecting the "unblock" option.

The AEPD states that the anonymisation tool was developed and tested using Excel versions 2010, 2016, and 2019 on the Microsoft Windows operating system, and the authority notes that the tool may not function correctly on other Excel versions within Windows. The AEPD further states that users are required to configure the regional format setting on their device to "Español (España)" via the Windows Region settings in order for the tool to operate as intended. The AEPD provides a SHA-256 hash value of 30ab954cc35f67bc2666a8ac6d94676fceb314e9552863592c0895bccf846695 for users to verify the integrity and authenticity of the downloaded file by running the certutil command via the Windows Command Prompt against the downloaded zip file named "herramienta-anonimizacion-pdpc-es.zip".
rows.authority: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos
rows.body_name: Agencia Espanola de Proteccion de Datos

rows.headline: CJEU Rules GDPR Does Not Permit National Case Law Requiring Prior Consultation of Data Subjects Before Disclosure of Official Documents
rows.title: CJEU Rules GDPR Does Not Permit National Case Law Requiring Prior Consultation of Data Subjects Before Disclosure of Official Documents
rows.summary:
The Court of Justice of the European Union, sitting as the First Chamber, ruled on 3 April 2025 in Case C-710/23 (L.H. v Ministerstvo zdravotnictví) that the GDPR precludes national case law imposing an obligation on public authorities to inform and consult a data subject before disclosing official documents containing that person's personal data, where no such obligation is grounded in national legislative or regulatory provisions.

On 3 April 2025, the Court of Justice delivered its preliminary ruling following a reference from the Nejvyšší správní soud (Supreme Administrative Court, Czech Republic), lodged on 22 November 2023. The underlying dispute concerned a refusal by the Czech Ministry of Health to disclose to L.H. information identifying the natural-person representatives of legal persons named in contracts for the purchase of COVID-19 screening tests and in certificates relating to those tests. No fine was imposed in these proceedings.

The Court finds that the personal data of a natural person acting as the representative of a legal person falls within the material scope of the GDPR (Art. 4(1) GDPR), notwithstanding Recital 14, which states that the Regulation does not cover the processing of personal data concerning legal persons. The Court determines that where such data appears in official documents held by a public authority, its disclosure constitutes processing of personal data subject to the lawfulness requirements of Article 6 GDPR, and may be grounded in a legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) or a task carried out in the public interest (Art. 6(1)(e)).

The Court further finds that Article 86 GDPR permits Member States to reconcile public access to official documents with data protection rights, but that any procedural requirements imposed on controllers in that context, including obligations to notify or consult data subjects prior to disclosure, are required to have a basis in Union or Member State law. The Court determines that a procedural obligation created solely through national case law, without a corresponding legislative or regulatory foundation, is incompatible with the GDPR's requirement that processing be grounded in law. The Court notes that such judge-made obligations cannot substitute for the legislative reconciliation that Article 86 GDPR contemplates between transparency and data protection.

rows.headline: Nigeria's President Tinubu Signs Investments and Securities Act 2025 into Law
rows.title: Nigeria's President Tinubu Signs Investments and Securities Act 2025 into Law
rows.summary: On 29 March 2025, President Bola Ahmed Tinubu signed the Investments and Securities Act 2025, repealing the ISA 2007 and bringing virtual and digital assets within Nigeria's securities framework. The Act recognises digital assets as securities, enables stablecoin licensing under SEC Nigeria, introduces the Accelerated Regulatory Incubation Programme (ARIP) sandbox, and aligns SEC powers with IOSCO's enhanced multilateral standards.

rows.headline: ENISA Publishes Good Practices Report on Cybersecurity for Smart Cars
rows.title: ENISA Publishes Good Practices Report on Cybersecurity for Smart Cars
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) published a good practices report on the security of smart cars, covering connected and semi-autonomous vehicles, with reference to existing standardisation, legislative and policy initiatives across the European Union.

On 25 November 2019, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity released its report titled "Good Practices for Security of Smart Cars", setting out a reference framework intended to promote cybersecurity for connected and automated vehicles across Europe. The report carries no fine or enforcement action, as it is a guidance document published for information purposes only and does not constitute a legal act of ENISA unless adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 2019/881.

ENISA identifies connected and semi-autonomous vehicles as presenting a distinct cybersecurity surface, encompassing in-vehicle systems, infrastructure and backend components. ENISA defines a high-level reference model for smart car architecture and sets out a threats taxonomy covering attack scenarios directed at vehicle networks, software, cloud systems and communications protocols. ENISA finds that threats to smart cars carry implications not only for data security but for physical safety, framing the core concern as "cybersecurity for safety".

ENISA catalogues the good practices across three categories: policies, organisational practices and technical practices. Under policies, ENISA identifies security by design, privacy by design, asset management and risk and threat management as foundational requirements. Organisational practices identified by ENISA include supplier relationship management, staff training and awareness, security management and incident management. Technical practices set out by ENISA span detection capabilities, protection of networks and protocols, software security, cloud security, cryptography, access control, self-protection and cyber resilience measures, and continuity of operations. ENISA notes that semi-autonomous systems require dedicated self-protection and resilience considerations given their operational characteristics.

The report draws on contributions from automotive manufacturers, national cybersecurity authorities including ANSSI and BSI, telecommunications providers, academic institutions and industry bodies, and is addressed to vehicle manufacturers, suppliers, infrastructure operators and policy makers involved in the connected vehicle ecosystem.

rows.headline: ENISA Publishes Good Practices Report on Cybersecurity and Resilience of Smart Cars
rows.title: ENISA Publishes Good Practices Report on Cybersecurity and Resilience of Smart Cars
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Network and Information Security (ENISA) published a report in December 2016, cataloguing good practices and recommendations for securing smart cars against cyber threats, with ENISA identifying sensitive assets, corresponding threats, risks, mitigation factors and proposed security measures across three categories.

On 13 January 2017, the European Union Agency for Network and Information Security published the report titled "Cyber Security and Resilience of Smart Cars: Good Practices and Recommendations", dated December 2016. The report contains no fine or enforcement action and carries no EUR or USD equivalent. It is presented as a guidance document intended for car manufacturers, tier-one and tier-two suppliers, and aftermarket suppliers operating within the EU automotive sector.

ENISA identifies smart cars as presenting a particular cybersecurity challenge because, the agency notes, security measures in connected vehicles must also guarantee physical safety, a dependency that distinguishes this sector from conventional information security contexts. To develop its findings, ENISA engaged experts from industry, academia, law enforcement and national agencies across multiple EU member states, conducting interviews and a validation workshop held in Munich in October 2016. The agency groups its resulting good practices into three categories: policy and standards, organisational measures, and security functions.

The report maps the sensitive assets present within typical smart car architectures, which ENISA identifies as spanning powertrain control, chassis control, body control, infotainment control and communications systems. For each asset category, ENISA sets out the threat landscape and associated risks, and identifies mitigation factors and candidate security measures. ENISA states that the study draws on expertise gathered through its Cars and Roads Security expert group, known as CaRSEC. The agency notes that the publication represents the views and interpretations of its authors and editors and is intended for information purposes only, and that it does not necessarily represent the state of the art at the time of any subsequent reading. ENISA adds that it may update the publication from time to time.

rows.headline: ENISA Hosts 10th eHealth Security Conference in Bucharest on 16 September 2025
rows.title: ENISA Hosts 10th eHealth Security Conference in Bucharest on 16 September 2025
rows.summary:
The EU Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) is convening its 10th eHealth Security Conference in Bucharest on 16 September 2025, focusing on NIS2 Directive implementation, the EU action plan for hospital cybersecurity, and the European Health Data Space (EHDS).

On 26 March 2025, ENISA announced the full-day physical event, organised in collaboration with the Romanian National Cyber Security Directorate (DNSC) and the European Cybersecurity Competence Centre (ECCC), and hosted at the Bucharest University of Economic Studies (ASE Bucharest), Aula Magna. No fines or enforcement determinations are associated with this announcement.

ENISA identifies the healthcare sector as facing persistent cybersecurity challenges, including ransomware, phishing campaigns and threats to medical devices and systems. The conference agenda addresses four thematic sessions: the current policy and legal framework, the evolving threat landscape and regional approaches, the EU action plan for the cybersecurity of hospitals and healthcare providers, and funding opportunities under the Digital Europe Programme. ENISA confirms speakers include a policy officer from the European Commission's DG CNECT covering NIS2 and the EHDS, a representative from the German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) on medical device threats and vulnerabilities, and senior officials from the cybersecurity agencies of Moldova and Ukraine addressing regional approaches.

ENISA notes that Session 3 will examine challenges and opportunities in implementing the EU action plan for hospitals and healthcare providers, with presentations from ENISA's Head of Resilience of Critical Sectors Unit and the Deputy Director of the DNSC. Session 4 will cover ECCC funding calls under the Digital Europe Programme and feature project presentations, including ASCLEPIUS, which ENISA describes as delivering cybersecurity managed services to SMEs in healthcare and the supply chain, and NERO, described as a cybersecurity awareness ecosystem for SMEs focused on patient data security. ENISA identifies the target audience as including healthcare IT professionals, clinical information security professionals, medical device manufacturers, national healthcare authorities, compliance and data privacy professionals, standardisation bodies, and academia.

rows.headline: Nigeria's NDPC Issues NDPA General Application and Implementation Directive 2025
rows.title: Nigeria's NDPC Issues NDPA General Application and Implementation Directive 2025
rows.summary: On 20 March 2025, the Nigeria Data Protection Commission issued the General Application and Implementation Directive (GAID), a binding implementation framework for the Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023. The Directive covers DPIAs, registration of Data Controllers and Data Processors of Major Importance, cross-border transfers, breach notification and data ethics. After a six-month transition, GAID took effect on 19 September 2025, replacing the NDPR 2019.

rows.headline: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Overview of Known Attacks and Impacts on AI Systems
rows.title: Japan AI Safety Institute Publishes Overview of Known Attacks and Impacts on AI Systems
rows.summary:
The Japan AI Safety Institute (AISI Japan) has published a technical document cataloguing known attack types targeting AI systems and their associated impacts, drawing on academic papers and other sources to inform the examination of countermeasures.

On 19 March 2025, AISI Japan released "Known Attacks and Their Impacts on AI Systems (V1)" to provide practitioners and developers with a structured overview of AI-specific security threats. The document does not carry a fine or enforcement determination; it is a technical guidance publication.

AISI Japan identifies eleven categories of impact arising from attacks on AI systems, spanning confidentiality, integrity and availability. Under confidentiality, the Institute identifies model leakage, training data leakage, application information leakage, input information leakage and internal data leakage as potential consequences of attacks. Under integrity, AISI Japan identifies that model malfunction, interpretability malfunction and safeguard bypass represent distinct adverse outcomes. Availability-related impacts identified by the Institute include computational waste, internal data corruption and system compromise, with the document noting that system compromise can itself lead to any category of impact.

The document maps eleven named attack vectors to these impact categories. AISI Japan describes attacks occurring during the training phase, including data poisoning (Attack D), model poisoning (Attack C), adversarial fine-tuning (Attack J) and code injection (Attack I). During inference, the Institute identifies model extraction (Attack A), training data-related information gathering (Attack B), evasion attacks (Attack E), energy-latency attacks (Attack F), prompt stealing (Attack G), prompt injection (Attack H) and Rowhammer attacks (Attack K). The document explicitly limits its scope to attacks that involve intervening in an AI model's training or inference processes, or that exploit and tamper with its inputs and outputs, and excludes conventional cybersecurity attacks such as theft of AI models through unauthorised network access, which AISI Japan notes do not involve interference with training or inference processes.

AISI Japan states that the real-world consequences of these attacks include privacy violations arising from training data leaks in medical AI systems and safety risks from abnormal outputs in automated driving systems, and characterises AI-specific security measures as essential given the increasing frequency with which such attacks are reported as AI systems evolve.

rows.headline: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium reports 2,410 NIS2 registrations and 80% rise in incident reporting since October 2024 transposition
rows.title: Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium reports 2,410 NIS2 registrations and 80% rise in incident reporting since October 2024 transposition
rows.summary:
The Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium (CCB) has reported that 2,410 organisations from critical sectors have registered under Belgium's NIS2 transposition framework, with mandatory registration deadlines and incident reporting obligations under the Network and Information Security Directive (NIS2) producing an 80% increase in reported cyber incidents compared with the preceding period.

On 17 March 2025, the Centre for Cybersecurity Belgium published figures showing that, since Belgium transposed the NIS2 Directive into national law in October 2024, 2,410 organisations from critical sectors including energy, transport, healthcare, digital infrastructure, government and manufacturing have completed mandatory registration at atwork.safeonweb.be. The CCB notes that a broader total of more than 4,500 organisations across all sectors have registered on the platform. Based on estimates derived from figures held by the Federal Public Service Economy, the CCB states that approximately 2,500 organisations fall within the scope of NIS2, indicating that the CCB estimates the vast majority of in-scope entities registered ahead of the deadline.

The CCB finds that incident reporting has increased materially since the NIS2 legislation came into force. The authority states that between August 2023 and September 2024, it received an average of 25 cyber incident reports per month, totalling 556 reports over 18 months. Since the introduction of NIS2 last October, the CCB records 226 incidents, representing an average of 45 reports per month. The CCB attributes this increase to the statutory reporting obligations introduced by NIS2 rather than to any demonstrated rise in the volume of cyber attacks on Belgian organisations, noting that prior to transposition there was underreporting by affected entities.

The CCB states that registered organisations, including those outside the mandatory NIS2 scope, receive access to a range of services including priority vulnerability alerts, cyber threat notifications, quick scan security reports, self-assessment tools and policy templates. The CCB states that NIS2-designated organisations receive priority status in the CCB's alerting processes. The authority's Director General, Miguel De Bruycker, states that organisations are actively working to raise their cybersecurity posture and that registration and incident report volumes are expected to increase further before stabilising.

rows.headline: John Edwards speaks at IAPP's Data Protection Intensive UK 2025
rows.title: John Edwards speaks at IAPP's Data Protection Intensive UK 2025
rows.summary: Hello and thank you for having me at IAPP London. I’ve seen that Sir Chris Bryant is also speaking here today. I was grateful to Chris for speaking at our much smaller event last November – what came through to me there, as it does now, is his understanding of how privacy and data protection affects people. The positives and the harms.
