rows.headline: Belgium publishes list of 26 fundamental-rights authorities under EU AI Act Article 77
rows.title: Belgium publishes list of 26 fundamental-rights authorities under EU AI Act Article 77
rows.summary:
Belgium has published its list of 26 authorities designated under Article 77 of the EU AI Act for fundamental-rights protection. The list, hosted by SPF Economie and last updated 11 March 2025, satisfies the Article 77(2) obligation requiring each Member State to identify and publicly disclose its fundamental-rights authorities by 2 November 2024.

The Article 77 obligation entered into force across the EU on 2 November 2024 under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689. Belgium's federal Ministère de l'Économie (SPF Economie) hosts the official PDF on its Intelligence Artificielle topic page. The PDF was last updated on 11 March 2025.

Confirmed designated bodies include Unia (anti-discrimination, the federal interfederal centre for equal opportunities) and Myria (the Federal Migration Centre). Per Unia's own announcement of 28 March 2025, Belgium has named 26 federal and regional regulators under this designation.

Operational note: Article 77 authorities are not Competent Authorities under Article 70. They do not enforce the AI Act directly. Instead, they receive Article 77 powers — to request and access any AI Act documentation in accessible form, and to request technical testing of high-risk AI systems through the relevant market surveillance authority — to support their existing fundamental-rights mandates.

Source: SPF Economie 'Intelligence artificielle' page (https://economie.fgov.be/fr/themes/line/intelligence-artificielle), confirmed by Unia announcement of 28 March 2025.

rows.headline: ENISA Publishes Governance Framework Outline for National Cybersecurity Strategies Across EU Member States
rows.title: ENISA Publishes Governance Framework Outline for National Cybersecurity Strategies Across EU Member States
rows.summary:
The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) has published a governance framework outline for national cybersecurity strategies (NCSSs), presenting good practices and statistical findings to support EU Member States in structuring and implementing effective cybersecurity governance arrangements under the EU Cybersecurity Act (Regulation (EU) No 2019/881).

On 28 February 2023, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity released the publication titled "A Governance Framework for National Cybersecurity Strategies", authored by ENISA staff alongside consultancy Wavestone, with contributions from national authorities across more than a dozen EU Member States including Austria, Belgium, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, Lithuania, Malta, the Netherlands, Slovakia, Spain, and Sweden. ENISA states that no financial penalties are associated with this publication.

ENISA finds that effective governance frameworks for NCSSs require attention to several key elements, including clear institutional responsibilities, coordination mechanisms between relevant national bodies, and structured processes for the deployment and review of cybersecurity strategies. The publication identifies a collection of good practices that European countries have put in place to ensure these elements function effectively across current and future NCSSs. ENISA notes that the statistical outline is intended to link key findings to the proposed framework components and to illustrate how Member States have approached governance in practice.

The agency states that the publication is a lightweight companion to a fuller detailed study, "Building Effective Governance Frameworks for the Implementation of National Cybersecurity Strategies", to which readers are directed for comprehensive analysis. ENISA states that the publication represents the views and interpretations of the agency and is not to be construed as a legally binding act unless adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 2019/881. ENISA states that the document is made available free of charge for information purposes and reproduction is authorised provided the source is acknowledged.

rows.headline: CPPA Issues Stipulated Final Order Against Background Alert, Inc. for Failure to Register as Data Broker Under California Delete Act
rows.title: CPPA Issues Stipulated Final Order Against Background Alert, Inc. for Failure to Register as Data Broker Under California Delete Act
rows.summary:
The California Privacy Protection Agency has issued a Stipulated Final Order against Background Alert, Inc., a people-search data broker operating backgroundalert.com, for failing to register with the Agency as required under California's Delete Act (Civ. Code § 1798.99.82(c)).

On 26 February 2025, the Board of the California Privacy Protection Agency adopted the Stipulated Final Order in Case No. ENF24-D-BA-23 against Background Alert, Inc., a corporation based in Huntington Beach, California. The source document does not specify a monetary penalty figure; no fine amount is therefore reported here.

The CPPA finds that Background Alert operates an online people-search website allowing users to search for individuals by first name, last name, and state, and that the company compiles reports drawn from public records including birth records, death records, arrest records, marriage and divorce records, professional licence records, and registered sex offender databases. The CPPA finds that Background Alert charges customers a monetary fee to access full compiled reports and markets its service as searching "literally billions" of records.

The CPPA determines that Background Alert's business model extends beyond the provision of raw public records. The CPPA finds that inferences constitute a material component of the company's operations, specifically that Background Alert identifies patterns within public records to draw conclusions about individuals who "may somehow be associated with" a searched-for person, including potential family members, roommates, business associates, and romantic partners. The CPPA finds that such inferences constitute Personal Information within the meaning of the CCPA and the Delete Act (Civ. Code §§ 1798.140(v)(1), 1798.99.80(a)), and that combining seemingly innocuous data points in this manner presents particular risks to consumer privacy. The relevant period for the Agency's factual findings is stated as 1 January 2023 through 11 February 2025.

rows.headline: CPPA Settles with Data Broker Background Alert Over Delete Act Registration Failure, Requiring Three-Year Operational Shutdown
rows.title: CPPA Settles with Data Broker Background Alert Over Delete Act Registration Failure, Requiring Three-Year Operational Shutdown
rows.summary:
The California Privacy Protection Agency has reached a settlement with Background Alert, Inc., a California-based data broker, for failing to register and pay an annual fee as required by the Delete Act (Civ. Code § 1798.99.82(c)), with the Stipulated Final Order requiring the company to cease operations through 2028 or face a USD 50,000 fine.

On 26 February 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency's Board approved the Stipulated Final Order in Case No. ENF24-D-BA-23, resolving the Enforcement Division's allegations that Background Alert, Inc., operating via backgroundalert.com and headquartered in Huntington Beach, California, failed to register as a data broker between 1 February and 8 October 2024. The Delete Act required businesses operating as data brokers in 2024 to register with the CPPA by 31 January 2025 or face fines of USD 200 per day.

The CPPA's Enforcement Division alleged that Background Alert amassed billions of public records, drew inferences from those records to identify individuals who, in the company's own framing, "may somehow be associated with" a searched-for person, and generated consumer profiles from identified patterns. The CPPA finds that Background Alert promoted this service with the statement, "It's scary how much information you can dig up on someone." The CPPA determines that this activity constituted operation as a data broker subject to registration obligations under the Delete Act.

Under the terms of the settlement, Background Alert is required to cease its data broker operations within 15 days of the order and to maintain that cessation through 2028. The CPPA finds that if Background Alert violates any term of the agreement, including the operational shutdown requirement, a USD 50,000 fine becomes payable. The settlement forms part of a broader investigative sweep of data broker registration compliance announced by the CPPA on 30 October 2024. The CPPA notes that the Delete Act's registration fees also fund development of the Delete Request and Opt-Out Platform, a centralised deletion mechanism scheduled to be available to consumers in 2026.

rows.headline: Norwegian Digitalisation Agency Issues Requirement for Cross-Border Digital Service Interoperability Within the EEA
rows.title: Norwegian Digitalisation Agency Issues Requirement for Cross-Border Digital Service Interoperability Within the EEA
rows.summary:
The Norwegian Digitalisation Agency (Digdir) has issued a requirement, applicable to state bodies and a recommendation for municipalities, that digital services be adapted for cross-border information exchange within the European Economic Area, drawing on the Digitalisation Circular and the Digital Europe Programme.

On 18 February 2025, Digdir updated its formal guidance stipulating that digital services are required, where appropriate, to be adapted to enable information exchange across national borders within the EEA. Digdir states the requirement is sourced in the Digitalisation Circular and is directed at enabling public authorities, businesses and residents to carry out tasks digitally across EEA member states.

Digdir states that the obligation applies as a binding requirement for state-level entities, while for municipalities it is classified as a recommendation. The agency notes that the underlying rationale is to facilitate seamless digital interaction between public bodies, commercial operators and individuals operating within the EEA area. Digdir further references the Digital Europe Programme as a relevant framework supporting cross-border interoperability, indicating that compliance is expected to align with that programme's technical and governance standards.

Digdir identifies cross-border services as a distinct category within its broader data sharing and use architecture, placing this requirement under the heading of transboundary services. The agency's national architecture team is identified as the responsible contact point for implementation guidance, and Digdir notes that the requirement was originally created on 17 December 2021, with the current update reflecting the February 2025 revision. Digdir notes that no specific penalty provisions or enforcement mechanisms are cited in the published guidance, and no articles from a single legislative instrument are referenced inline, as the source instrument is the domestic Digitalisation Circular rather than a numbered EEA regulation.

rows.headline: Protecting children’s data in a digital world
rows.title: Protecting children’s data in a digital world
rows.summary: Part of parenting is educating and preparing your child for daily life, from showing them how to cross the road safely to teaching them stranger danger. When that daily life also involves social media and video sharing platforms – with children spending up to eight hours a day on these channels – safety is no less significant. But it feels more complicated.

rows.headline: EDPB Publishes Expert Paper on AI Applications and Data Protection Requirements Following BfDI Initiative
rows.title: EDPB Publishes Expert Paper on AI Applications and Data Protection Requirements Following BfDI Initiative
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has published an expert paper on artificial intelligence applications and associated data protection obligations, produced through its Support Pool of Experts programme at the initiative of the Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI), the German Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information.

On 6 February 2025, the BfDI announced that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) had commissioned and published the expert paper under its Support Pool of Experts programme. The paper was authored by Dr Kris Shrishak and is freely accessible on the EDPB's website. No financial penalties are associated with this publication.

The BfDI notes that where personal data are processed using artificial intelligence methods, data protection law requirements are required to be observed alongside any applicable AI-specific obligations. The expert paper, produced at the BfDI's initiative, examines challenges arising in AI applications at various stages of the AI lifecycle, with particular focus on the detection and removal of discriminatory bias and discrimination risks, as well as the effective implementation of data subject rights (Art. 12–22 GDPR) across those lifecycle phases.

The EDPB's Support Pool of Experts programme draws on specialised technical and legal expertise to assist supervisory authorities and inform broader regulatory understanding. The BfDI confirms that the findings of the paper are now available and that the results address complex algorithmic systems where compliance with GDPR obligations, including requirements relating to transparency, fairness, and accountability (Art. 5 GDPR), presents particular technical difficulty. The BfDI describes the paper as addressing practical challenges in ensuring that data protection principles are upheld in AI development and deployment contexts across the full application lifecycle.
rows.authority: Bundesbeauftragte fur den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit
rows.body_name: Bundesbeauftragte fur den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit

rows.headline: OECD AI Observatory Publishes Common Reporting Framework for AI Incidents
rows.title: OECD AI Observatory Publishes Common Reporting Framework for AI Incidents
rows.summary:
The OECD AI Observatory has published a standardised framework for reporting AI incidents internationally, setting out a common structure for documenting AI-related harms across industries and jurisdictions, drawing on the Observatory's definition of AI incidents adopted in May 2024.

On 31 January 2025, the OECD AI Observatory released "Towards a Common Reporting Framework for AI Incidents", developed with guidance from the OECD Expert Group on AI Incidents, co-chaired by Marko Grobelnik, Mark Latonero, Irina Orssich, and Elham Tabassi. The publication does not impose fines or binding obligations and carries no financial penalty.

The Observatory defines an AI incident, as formalised in May 2024, as any event, circumstance, or series of events in which an AI system's development, use, or malfunction causes harm, either directly or indirectly. The framework identifies a range of incident types, including deepfake fraud, non-consensual AI-generated imagery, disinformation, biased algorithmic decision-making, and failures in automated systems. The Observatory notes that in 2023, France alone recorded over 130,000 online scams, representing an 8% increase from the prior year, and attributes part of this growth to AI-generated content and deepfake technology. Among the cases catalogued, the Observatory references a fraud incident in which a victim transferred 830,000 euros to perpetrators using deepfake videos and AI-generated images impersonating a well-known public figure.

The Observatory finds that AI-related harms do not arise solely from malicious actors. The publication identifies design flaws as a separate category of risk, citing a recruitment algorithm discontinued after it was found to discriminate against women, and a separate AI hiring system that resulted in a six-figure settlement following findings that it excluded women over 55 and men over 60. The Observatory also references a healthcare algorithm that the publication states unintentionally prioritised White patients over Black patients by linking care allocation to cost rather than medical urgency, noting that subsequent intervention reduced the measured bias by over 80%.

The Observatory states that the common framework is intended to be consistent yet flexible, enabling policymakers to align responses across diverse legal and regulatory environments. The Observatory's AI Incidents Monitor, described as a media-based tracking tool, is presented as a companion resource for analysing emerging incident patterns. The Observatory states that the framework identifies three intended outcomes for adopting the common structure: improved risk assessment through comparative incident data, faster identification of high-risk AI systems, and enhanced cross-border knowledge-sharing among regulators and industry participants.

rows.headline: Our strategy for levelling the playing field for online tracking in 2025
rows.title: Our strategy for levelling the playing field for online tracking in 2025
rows.summary: Online tracking is a part of everyday digital life. It enables personalised advertising, funds many free services and shapes our online experiences.

rows.headline: Company fined £200,000 for instigating unlawful loan promotion nuisance texts
rows.title: Company fined £200,000 for instigating unlawful loan promotion nuisance texts
rows.summary: We have fined West Sussex-based company ESL Consultancy Services Ltd (ESL) £200,000 for knowingly instigating unlawful loan promotion nuisance text messages being sent to people who had not consented to receive them.

rows.headline: ICO response to government on economic growth
rows.title: ICO response to government on economic growth
rows.summary: The Prime Minister, the Chancellor, and the Business Secretary wrote to the Information Commissioner (along with 16 other regulators) at the end of December asking for proposals to boost business confidence, improve the investment climate, and foster sustainable economic growth.

rows.headline: Debunking data protection myths about AI
rows.title: Debunking data protection myths about AI
rows.summary: From unlocking medical breakthroughs to revolutionising public services in the UK, AI has huge potential to transform our lives for the better. Across the economy, businesses seem excited about investing in AI to drive growth and innovation, while the tech is inspiring vibrant discussions amongst policymakers and the public alike.

rows.headline: ICO takes action to tackle cookie compliance across the UK’s top 1,000 websites
rows.title: ICO takes action to tackle cookie compliance across the UK’s top 1,000 websites
rows.summary: We have today announced plans to bring the UK’s top 1,000 websites into compliance with data protection law.

rows.headline: CPPA and South Korea's PIPC Sign Declaration of Cooperation on Privacy and Data Protection
rows.title: CPPA and South Korea's PIPC Sign Declaration of Cooperation on Privacy and Data Protection
rows.summary:
The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and the Personal Information Protection Commission of the Republic of Korea (PIPC) signed a declaration of cooperation on 10 January 2025, establishing a framework for bilateral collaboration on privacy enforcement, best-practice sharing, and cross-border data protection matters under their respective domestic regimes, including the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA).

On 10 January 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency and the Personal Information Protection Commission formalised the arrangement through signatures by CPPA Executive Director Ashkan Soltani and PIPC Chairperson Dr Haksoo Ko. The CPPA states that no financial penalties or enforcement outcomes are associated with this declaration.

The declaration, as described in the CPPA announcement, sets out four areas of cooperation between the two authorities: sharing best practices, knowledge, and information on procedures and methods, including in the course of investigations; sharing annual enforcement plans and annual activity reports; facilitating meetings between staff members; and developing mechanisms for mutual collaboration. The CPPA states that international cooperation is considered essential in addressing privacy challenges arising from cross-border data flows in an interconnected digital environment.

The PIPC notes in its statement accompanying the declaration that the arrangement is consistent with its ongoing efforts to diversify and expand global cooperation, and that the agreement is intended to enhance benefits for citizens in both jurisdictions. The CPPA states that its remit centres on promoting consumer privacy rights and business responsibilities under the CCPA, while the PIPC exercises authority over personal information protection under South Korean law. The CPPA states that the declaration does not establish binding legal obligations between the two authorities but provides a cooperative structure through which each may draw on the other's regulatory experience and enforcement insight.

rows.headline: ECOWAS Court Orders Nigeria to Amend Cybercrimes Act § 24 in SERAP Case
rows.title: ECOWAS Court Orders Nigeria to Amend Cybercrimes Act § 24 in SERAP Case
rows.summary: In March 2024, the ECOWAS Court of Justice ruled in favour of the Socio-Economic Rights and Accountability Project (SERAP), finding that section 24 of Nigeria's Cybercrimes Act 2015 contravened Article 9 of the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights and the ICCPR. The Court ordered Nigeria to amend the provision. The ruling drove the Cybercrimes Amendment Act signed on 28 February 2024.

rows.headline: Nigeria Signs Cybercrimes Amendment Act 2024 Following ECOWAS Court Ruling
rows.title: Nigeria Signs Cybercrimes Amendment Act 2024 Following ECOWAS Court Ruling
rows.summary: On 28 February 2024, Nigeria signed the Cybercrimes (Prohibition, Prevention, etc.) (Amendment) Act 2024, amending twelve sections of the 2015 Act. The Amendment broadens the scope of offences and enhances penalties for identity theft, cyberstalking and data breaches. It responded to a March 2024 ECOWAS Court ruling that section 24 of the original Act contravened the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights and the ICCPR.
rows.authority: Nigeria Office of the National Security Adviser
rows.body_name: Nigeria Office of the National Security Adviser

rows.headline: Nigeria's President Tinubu Signs Data Protection Act 2023 into Law
rows.title: Nigeria's President Tinubu Signs Data Protection Act 2023 into Law
rows.summary: On 12 June 2023, President Bola Ahmed Tinubu signed the Nigeria Data Protection Bill 2023 into law as the Nigeria Data Protection Act, establishing the country's first federal data-protection statute. The NDPA creates the Nigeria Data Protection Commission as independent regulator, codifies data subject rights, and supersedes the NITDA-issued Nigeria Data Protection Regulation 2019 as Nigeria's privacy framework.

rows.headline: Turkey's KVKK Issues Agentic AI Data Protection Guidance
rows.title: Turkey's KVKK Issues Agentic AI Data Protection Guidance
rows.summary:
Turkey's Personal Data Protection Authority (KVKK) published a formal guidance document on agentic artificial intelligence on 15 April 2026, setting out how existing data protection obligations under Law No. 6698 apply to agentic AI systems operating in Turkey.

According to Prokopiev Law Group's analysis of the guidance, the document is not a binding regulation but constitutes soft-law guidance currently in force, with no prior consultation period and no compliance deadline or transition period attached.

The guidance identifies four primary compliance areas for organisations deploying agentic AI. First, it classifies derived and inferred data, including outputs generated by correlating multiple datasets, as personal data subject to full KVKK compliance. Second, it requires that accountability among AI developers, providers, and deploying organisations be clearly allocated and documented across multi-agent architectures. Third, it mandates a risk-based governance approach incorporating privacy by design and privacy by default principles. Fourth, it treats data accuracy as a direct legal requirement under Article 4(1)(d) of the KVKK Law, meaning erroneous AI-generated outputs used in individual assessments constitute unlawful processing.

Prokopiev Law Group notes that organisations in financial services, insurance, and human resources face particular exposure, given their reliance on individual assessments that may be informed by agentic AI tools.

The KVKK signals it will assess agentic AI systems across their full data lifecycle, including model outputs and downstream decisions, not solely the handling of input data. The guidance follows separate KVKK announcements in February 2026 concerning Grok and Google Assistant, indicating sustained supervisory attention to AI-related data processing across multiple product categories.

rows.headline: China TC260-003 Generative AI Security Guidelines
rows.title: China TC260-003 Generative AI Security Guidelines
rows.summary: A source document linked to China's TC260-003 Guidelines on Generative AI Security has been identified, referencing a translation hosted by Georgetown University's Centre for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET). The document, titled 'Generative AI Safety' in its English-language version, appears to form part of China's national standardisation framework for artificial intelligence governance. TC260 refers to the National Information Security Standardisation Technical Committee, which operates under China's national standards body and plays a central role in developing cybersecurity and data security norms. However, the extracted content from this source consists entirely of binary-encoded PDF data and does not yield readable text from which specific provisions, requirements, or findings can be accurately reported. As a result, no factual claims about the substance of the guidelines can be responsibly attributed to this document at this time. Readers seeking detailed analysis of the TC260-003 framework are directed to the original English-language translation available via the CSET repository at Georgetown University.

rows.headline: Morocco Introduces 'Digital X.0' Framework Law for AI Governance
rows.title: Morocco Introduces 'Digital X.0' Framework Law for AI Governance
rows.summary:
Morocco has unveiled a landmark framework law titled 'Digital X.0', designed to embed artificial intelligence, data governance, and digital identity management into the country's national modernisation agenda. The draft legislation, currently under review by the General Secretariat of the Government, was presented by Amal El Fallah Seghrouchni, Minister Delegate for Digital Transition and Administrative Reform.

The law serves as the legal foundation for Morocco's Maroc Digital 2030 strategy, which positions AI as a central pillar of economic competitiveness and public sector reform. According to the draft legislation, Digital X.0 is structured around three core priorities: data governance, digital identity, and interoperability.

On data governance, the framework aligns with existing provisions under Law 09-08 on personal data protection, ensuring secure and transparent data use across public and private sectors. The digital identity component introduces a sector-specific system granting citizens direct control over their personal data, limiting institutional access to information strictly necessary for each service. The interoperability pillar establishes a traceable consent framework requiring explicit user authorisation for all exchanges of information between government agencies and private actors.

The bill also represents Morocco's first formal attempt to integrate AI into administrative and economic governance, introducing rules covering transparency, accountability, and the ethical use of algorithms. Cybersecurity provisions are included to build digital trust without placing excessive compliance burdens on innovation.

'This framework is about balancing innovation with trust,' said El Fallah Seghrouchni. 'We want to ensure that AI and digital technologies are harnessed responsibly to serve citizens, not control them.'

rows.headline: South Africa Publishes National AI Policy Framework for Public Comment
rows.title: South Africa Publishes National AI Policy Framework for Public Comment
rows.summary:
South Africa's Department of Communications and Digital Technologies (DCDT) has published the SA National AI Policy Framework, opening the document for public consultation. The framework, released on 25 October 2024, invites stakeholders to submit comments and inputs to the department by 17h00 on Friday, 29 November 2024.

The DCDT has made the 3.16 MB document available through its official website, encouraging respondents to direct written submissions to designated departmental contacts. The department has advised that inputs should be brief and, where possible, sequenced in line with the framework document and its sub-topics to assist with analysis. No prescribed format has been specified for submissions.

The publication of the framework represents a formal step by the South African government to establish national policy positions on artificial intelligence. The DCDT oversees a broad portfolio of digital and communications programmes, including digital infrastructure, digital society and economy, and partnerships and stakeholder management, all of which intersect with the governance of emerging technologies such as AI.

South Africa joins a growing number of nations that have moved to formalise AI governance structures at the national level. The public consultation process provides civil society organisations, industry bodies, academic institutions, and individual citizens with an opportunity to contribute to the shaping of the country's AI policy direction. The DCDT has not outlined a timeline for finalising the framework following the close of the consultation period.

rows.headline: Hong Kong Ethical AI Framework Version 2.0 Published
rows.title: Hong Kong Ethical AI Framework Version 2.0 Published
rows.summary:
Hong Kong's Office of the Government Chief Information Officer has published Version 2.0 of its Ethical AI Framework, dated December 2025, according to the document hosted on the Digital Policy Office's official website.

The framework, available via the Digital Policy Office portal under its data governance policies and standards section, represents an updated iteration of Hong Kong's formal approach to ethical artificial intelligence governance. The document is listed under the government's data governance work, signalling its role as an official policy instrument for the region's public sector AI practices.

However, the substantive content of the framework, including its principles, guidelines, and specific governance requirements, cannot be verified from the available extracted text, which returned only encoded binary data rather than readable document content. As a result, the specific provisions, scope, and obligations set out in Version 2.0 cannot be reported with accuracy at this time.

The Digital Policy Office's publication of an updated framework suggests Hong Kong continues to develop its formal AI governance infrastructure, building on earlier iterations of its ethical AI guidance for government bodies and potentially the broader technology sector. Further details of the framework's contents are available directly via the Digital Policy Office website.

rows.headline: Source Document Unreadable: No Story Generated
rows.title: Source Document Unreadable: No Story Generated
rows.summary: No news story could be produced from this source. The PDF document titled 'Report on errors in Federal Civil Service Bill, 2080', hosted on the Nepal Parliament HR portal, could not be processed as its extracted text consists entirely of corrupted binary data with no legible content. PAI's editorial standards require that all published news stories be based on verifiable, readable source material. As the document's text is not recoverable from the provided extraction, no factual claims about the Federal Civil Service Bill, 2080 or any reported errors therein can be accurately reported. Readers seeking information on this topic are advised to access the original document directly via the Nepal Parliament website or contact the relevant parliamentary committee for a readable version of the report.

rows.headline: Israel Issues Draft AI Privacy Guidelines Ahead of Law Amendment
rows.title: Israel Issues Draft AI Privacy Guidelines Ahead of Law Amendment
rows.summary:
The Israeli Privacy Protection Authority has published draft guidelines outlining how the country's Privacy Protection Law applies to artificial intelligence systems, with the rules set to gain additional force following the entry into force of Amendment 13 to the Law in August 2025.

According to an analysis by Israeli law firm Gornitzky GNY, the guidelines cover several key areas affecting companies that develop or deploy AI systems. On consent, the Authority states that using personal data in AI systems requires informed consent from data subjects, including disclosure of the purposes of data collection, the recipients of the data, associated risks, and whether an automated system is involved in the collection process.

On accountability, the Authority states that organisations training AI models are 'very likely' required to appoint a Data Protection Officer under the amended Law. The guidelines also recommend conducting a Data Protection Impact Assessment before deploying AI systems to process personal information, describing this as the best method for verifying and demonstrating compliance with privacy requirements.

The guidelines further recommend that organisations adopt internal policies governing the use of generative AI tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and CoPilot. Such policies should specify which employees are authorised to use these tools, which tools are permitted, what types of data can be uploaded, and how prompts are retained.

On web scraping, the Authority's position is that scraping personal data from the internet to train AI models without informed consent constitutes unlawful privacy infringement. The Authority also states that operators of online platforms must take active steps to prevent data scraping from their services.

rows.headline: China TC260-003 Generative AI Security Guidelines
rows.title: China TC260-003 Generative AI Security Guidelines
rows.summary: A news story cannot be accurately produced from this source. The provided document text consists entirely of binary-encoded PDF data rather than readable content. No specific facts, provisions, dates, or regulatory details from China's TC260-003 Generative AI Security Guidelines can be reliably extracted from the supplied material. Publishing a story based on fabricated or assumed details would not meet journalistic accuracy standards. To produce a compliant news item, a properly extracted plain-text version of the TC260-003 guidelines document would be required.

rows.headline: NIST Expands AI Risk Framework with Critical Infrastructure Profile
rows.title: NIST Expands AI Risk Framework with Critical Infrastructure Profile
rows.summary:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) has released a concept note for an AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) Profile focused on Trustworthy AI in Critical Infrastructure, published on 7 April 2026. The profile is designed to guide critical infrastructure operators towards specific risk management practices when engaging AI-enabled capabilities.

The development builds on NIST's original AI RMF 1.0, which was released on 26 January 2023 following a consensus-driven, open, and transparent process that included public comment periods, multiple workshops, and a Request for Information. The framework, led by the Information Technology Laboratory's AI Programme in collaboration with private and public sector partners, is intended for voluntary use and aims to improve the incorporation of trustworthiness considerations into the design, development, use, and evaluation of AI products, services, and systems.

NIST has also previously released several companion resources, including the AI RMF Playbook, an AI RMF Roadmap, and an AI RMF Crosswalk. On 30 March 2023, the agency launched the Trustworthy and Responsible AI Resource Centre to facilitate implementation of and international alignment with the framework.

Most recently, on 26 July 2024, NIST published NIST-AI-600-1, the Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative Artificial Intelligence Profile, which assists organisations in identifying risks unique to generative AI systems. Use cases demonstrating how organisations are applying the AI RMF are available through the AI Resource Centre.

rows.headline: Israel Publishes Draft AI Privacy Guidelines Ahead of Law Amendment
rows.title: Israel Publishes Draft AI Privacy Guidelines Ahead of Law Amendment
rows.summary:
The Israeli Privacy Protection Authority has published draft guidelines on the applicability of the Privacy Protection Law to artificial intelligence systems, according to an analysis by law firm Gornitzky GNY. The guidelines outline the Authority's interpretation of existing privacy law in the context of developing and using AI systems, with particular relevance to Amendment 13 to the Privacy Protection Law, which enters into force in August 2025.

According to Gornitzky GNY's review, the draft guidelines set out that the use of personal data in AI systems requires informed consent from data subjects. Valid consent must include disclosure of the purposes for data collection, the recipients of the data, whether provision of data is legally required, the types and sources of data, associated processing risks, and whether data is being collected via an automated AI-based system.

On accountability, the Authority states that organisations training AI models are described as 'very likely' to be required to appoint a Data Protection Officer under the amended law. The guidelines also recommend conducting a Data Protection Impact Assessment prior to deploying AI systems for personal data processing.

The guidelines further address the use of generative AI tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and CoPilot, recommending organisations adopt internal policies governing which staff use such tools, what data is uploaded, and how retention of prompts is limited.

On web scraping, the Authority states that collecting personal data from the internet to train AI models without informed consent constitutes unlawful privacy infringement. Publication of personal data on social media does not, in the Authority's view, amount to informed consent for AI training purposes. Platform operators are directed to take steps to prevent scraping from their services.

rows.headline: Mongolia's Central Bank Publishes Legal Framework Overview
rows.title: Mongolia's Central Bank Publishes Legal Framework Overview
rows.summary:
The Central Bank of Mongolia, known as Mongolbank, has published an overview of the laws and legal frameworks governing its operations, available on the institution's official website.

The documentation covers a broad range of regulatory areas central to Mongolia's financial system. These include laws related to monetary policy, foreign exchange regulation, payment systems, banking supervision, and anti-terrorism financing measures. The framework also addresses consumer credit debt-to-income ratios and the legal basis for Mongolbank's contractual arrangements with the Government of Mongolia.

According to Mongolbank's published materials, the Central Bank Law currently in force was adopted in 1996 and remains the primary legislative instrument governing the institution's mandate and operations. The bank operates a Monetary Policy Committee, maintains foreign currency reserves, and conducts open market operations as part of its core functions.

The legal documentation further outlines Mongolbank's supervisory responsibilities over the banking sector, including licensing of banking activities, prudential requirements, and procedures for compulsory bank liquidation. A dedicated Financial Stability Council is also referenced within the framework, with its own operational rules and membership structure.

The payment system section of the framework details the National Payment System, its policy guidelines, participant classifications, and merchant category codes. Mongolbank's role in gold and precious metals procurement is likewise covered under the published legal structure.

The materials are accessible in Mongolian through the Mongolbank official web portal and serve as the authoritative reference for all entities operating within Mongolia's regulated financial sector.

rows.headline: Philippines Updates National AI Strategy with DTI Roadmap 2.0
rows.title: Philippines Updates National AI Strategy with DTI Roadmap 2.0
rows.summary:
The Philippine Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) launched the National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 (NAISR 2.0) in July 2024, presenting a comprehensive blueprint for an AI-driven economy built through multi-year, multi-stakeholder consultations.

According to the NAISR 2.0 framework, the strategy's origins date to late 2019, when the DTI formally initiated efforts to position the Philippines as an AI leader in Southeast Asia. At that time, the country ranked third in the region for AI readiness according to Oxford Insights. The process involved structured consultations with government agencies, academic institutions, industry leaders, and civil society groups.

The roadmap evolved through three distinct phases. The first version, launched in May 2021, established foundational pillars covering talent development, AI policy, and mission-driven research across agriculture, manufacturing, and services. The 2.0 update responded to the global rise of generative AI and growing emphasis on ethical AI governance.

The July 2024 launch drew support from multiple Philippine government bodies, including NEDA, DICT, Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, the Philippine Space Agency, the Department of Budget and Management, and the Senate of the Philippines. The Asian Development Bank served as co-supporter, with endorsements from Google, AWS, Microsoft, IBM, Siemens, and Bosch Philippines.

A key initiative established under the roadmap is the Centre for AI Research (CAIR). The NAISR 2.0 framework has since been superseded following the Philippines' adoption of a new national AI strategy under the Department of Science and Technology (DOST). The DTI roadmap is now preserved as an archived reference documenting the country's foundational AI governance work.

rows.headline: Da Afghanistan Bank Publishes Islamic Banking Regulatory Framework
rows.title: Da Afghanistan Bank Publishes Islamic Banking Regulatory Framework
rows.summary:
Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB) has published a comprehensive set of Islamic Banking Regulations and Guidelines on its official website, establishing a structured regulatory framework for Islamic financial institutions operating in Afghanistan.

The framework covers a wide range of regulatory areas, including asset classification and provisioning, capital adequacy, liquidity risk management, profit distribution, and enforcement actions. Separate versions of the National Shariah Governance Framework have been issued for full-fledged Islamic banks and for institutions operating Islamic windows or units.

According to DAB's published materials, the regulations also address the conversion of conventional banks into Islamic banks, with dedicated guidelines outlining the procedural and compliance requirements for such transitions. Licensing regulations for Islamic banks and windows are included within the same regulatory package.

The central bank has additionally released product outlines and Shariah parameters covering key Islamic finance instruments, including Murabaha, Ijarah, Musharakah, Mudarabah, Salam, and Istisna'. Supporting guidelines address areas such as late payment charges, profit sharing investment accounts, product transparency and disclosure, and the outsourcing of Islamic banking operations.

A suite of examination manuals accompanies the regulatory documents, covering on-site and off-site examination procedures, Shariah compliance, Shariah board member assessments, and product verification processes. Terms of reference for Shariah Supervisory Boards and procedures for Islamic banking operations are also included.

All documents are available for download through DAB's official website, providing financial institutions with direct access to the full regulatory and compliance framework governing Islamic banking in Afghanistan.

rows.headline: Hong Kong Publishes Ethical AI Framework Version 2.0
rows.title: Hong Kong Publishes Ethical AI Framework Version 2.0
rows.summary: Hong Kong's Office of the Government Chief Information Officer has published Version 2.0 of its Ethical AI Framework, dated December 2025, signalling an update to the city's governance approach for artificial intelligence. The framework, made available through the Digital Policy Office, forms part of Hong Kong's broader data governance policies and standards programme. The document represents the second iteration of the territory's ethical AI guidance, building on earlier work to establish principles and standards for the responsible use of AI systems in Hong Kong. The framework is publicly accessible via the Digital Policy Office website under its data governance section. No further substantive details from the document's content were available for review at the time of publication, as the source file was not rendered in a readable format. PAI will provide further coverage as additional details from the framework become accessible.

rows.headline: RBI Publishes Domestic Regulatory Measures for Banking Sector
rows.title: RBI Publishes Domestic Regulatory Measures for Banking Sector
rows.summary:
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has published an annex detailing important domestic regulatory measures introduced between December 2024 and early 2025, covering a range of banking and financial sector governance areas.

According to the RBI's report, guidelines on Government Debt Relief Schemes were issued on 31 December 2024, establishing prudential treatment for exposures under State Government debt waiver programmes. The guidelines aim to maintain credit discipline and mitigate moral hazard by providing a model operating procedure for designing relief measures.

In February 2025, the RBI introduced a 'stock broker connect' facility on the NDS-OM platform, permitting SEBI-registered stock brokers to directly access the electronic trading platform for secondary market government securities transactions on behalf of individual clients. The measure is designed to facilitate retail participation in government securities.

Additional February 2025 measures permitted matching and guaranteed settlement of trades between Primary Members and Gilt Account Holders on NDS-OM, with an option to settle such trades through CCIL, bringing uniformity to trading and settlement norms across government securities transactions.

The RBI also reviewed prudential norms for Urban Co-operative Banks (UCBs) in February 2025, revising credit concentration risk parameters, housing loan exposure limits, and extending a glide-path for asset valuation adjustments by two additional years.

On 24 March 2025, the RBI issued revised Master Directions on Priority Sector Lending, following a comprehensive stakeholder review. Key changes include enhanced loan limits for housing, broadened eligibility under renewable energy, and a revision of overall PSL targets for UCBs to 60 per cent of Adjusted Net Bank Credit.

rows.headline: Lebanon's TRA Publishes Legal Framework for Telecoms Regulation
rows.title: Lebanon's TRA Publishes Legal Framework for Telecoms Regulation
rows.summary:
Lebanon's Telecommunications Regulatory Authority (TRA) has published an overview of the legal framework governing its mandate and activities, outlining the key laws and decrees under which it operates.

According to the TRA's official website, the authority's operations are grounded primarily in Telecom Law 431 of 2002, which was issued to provide the framework for organising the telecommunications services sector and to set the rules for its transfer to the private sector.

The TRA has also highlighted Decree No. 1328, issued in September 2025, which appointed the current TRA Board and formally announced its members, their commencement dates, and the length of their terms of office. This follows the earlier Decree No. 1, issued in February 2007, which had appointed the former TRA Board under similar terms.

Administrative and financial governance of the authority is underpinned by Decree 14264, issued in 2005, which specifies the administrative and financial regulations according to which the TRA is managed. Separate financial procedures documents are also made publicly available through the authority's website.

The TRA's published framework covers a broad range of regulatory areas, including service provider licensing, spectrum management, interconnection, quality of service, type approval regulation, numbering, and dispute resolution. The authority also addresses consumer protection matters, including children's protection and ICT road safety guidance.

The publication of this legal framework provides operators, investors, and consumers in Lebanon with a consolidated reference point for understanding the regulatory environment governing the country's telecommunications sector.

rows.headline: Andorra Reviews Urban Planning Law in Closed Committee Session
rows.title: Andorra Reviews Urban Planning Law in Closed Committee Session
rows.summary:
Andorra's Consell General has convened a legislative committee session to advance modifications to the General Law of Territorial Planning and Urbanism, known by its Catalan acronym LGOTU. The closed-door session, held on 8 April 2026 at the Sala de Comissions in the parliamentary building, brought together nine committee members to work on adapting the law's articles to conclusions drawn by a dedicated Study Commission.

The Study Commission was previously established to analyse technical actions and measures in territorial planning and urbanism, with the aim of ensuring sustainable urban growth across the Principality. The legislative committee is now tasked with translating those conclusions into concrete amendments to the existing legal framework.

The session's agenda included approval of a prior meeting's minutes, substantive review of the legal articles to be amended, and consideration of other matters of interest to the committee. Among the members present were Jordi Casadevall Touseil, Gemma Riba Casal, Pol Bartolomé Areny, Bernadeta Coma González, Meritxell López Guitart, Marc Magallon Font, Laia Moliné Cintas, Carine Montaner Raynaud, and Victor Pintos Morell.

The session was designated as non-public, meaning no external observers were permitted to attend. The LGOTU serves as the principal legislative instrument governing land use and urban development in Andorra, and its modification reflects the government's broader commitment to regulated and sustainable territorial growth.

rows.headline: RBI Publishes Key Domestic Regulatory Measures for 2024-25
rows.title: RBI Publishes Key Domestic Regulatory Measures for 2024-25
rows.summary:
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has published an annex detailing important domestic regulatory measures introduced between December 2024 and early 2025, covering debt relief, government securities trading, and lending norms.

According to the RBI's report, guidelines on Government Debt Relief Schemes were issued on 31 December 2024 to maintain credit discipline and mitigate moral hazard. The guidelines lay down prudential treatment for exposures arising from State Government debt waiver schemes and provide a model operating procedure to align stakeholder expectations.

In February 2025, the RBI introduced a 'stock broker connect' facility on the NDS-OM platform, permitting SEBI-registered stock brokers to directly access the electronic trading system for government securities on behalf of individual clients. The measure is intended to facilitate retail participation in government securities markets.

The RBI also permitted matching and guaranteed settlement of trades between Primary Members and their Gilt Account Holders on NDS-OM, with an option to settle such trades through CCIL, bringing uniformity to trading and settlement norms across government securities transactions.

On 24 February 2025, the RBI reviewed prudential norms for Urban Co-operative Banks (UCBs), revising credit concentration limits, housing loan ceilings for Tier-3 and Tier-4 UCBs, and extending the glide-path for Security Receipts valuation by two additional years.

On 24 March 2025, the RBI issued revised Priority Sector Lending directions, enhancing several loan limits, broadening renewable energy loan classifications, and revising overall PSL targets for UCBs to 60 per cent of Adjusted Net Bank Credit or Credit Equivalent of Off-Balance Sheet Exposures, whichever is higher.

rows.headline: Delaware General Assembly Publishes Legislative Agenda Reports
rows.title: Delaware General Assembly Publishes Legislative Agenda Reports
rows.summary:
The Delaware General Assembly has made its agenda and activity reports publicly available through the official legislative website, providing transparency into the legislative process for residents and stakeholders across the state.

The reports, accessible via the General Assembly's online portal, cover a range of legislative activity including bill tracking, committee meetings, and daily activity summaries. The platform allows users to access recent legislation, budget and appropriations bills, and general notices, alongside the full Delaware Code and administrative regulations.

The General Assembly's website also hosts resources detailing how a bill becomes law, the rules governing both the Senate and the House, and schedules for committee hearings. Members of the public can review voting records, meeting minutes, and committee agendas through the portal.

Legislative oversight functions are supported by several bodies listed on the site, including the Finance Committee, the Capital Improvement Committee, and the Legislative Oversight and Sunset Committee. Task forces and commissions operating under the General Assembly are also documented, with their meeting schedules publicly listed.

The Division of Legislative Services and the Office of the Controller General are among the administrative offices referenced, with the latter responsible for budget analysis and grant-in-aid processes. The site further outlines public participation opportunities in the legislative process.

Legislative Hall, located at 411 Legislative Avenue in Dover, serves as the seat of Delaware's legislature. The General Assembly can be contacted directly by telephone on (302) 744-4114. All meeting recordings for the Senate Chamber, House Chamber, and Joint Finance Hearing Room are archived and available via YouTube.

rows.headline: Turkey's KVKK Issues Guidance on Agentic AI Data Protection
rows.title: Turkey's KVKK Issues Guidance on Agentic AI Data Protection
rows.summary:
Turkey's Personal Data Protection Authority (KVKK) published a guidance document on agentic artificial intelligence on 15 April 2026, setting out how existing data protection obligations apply to AI systems capable of autonomous action.

According to an analysis by Prokopiev Law Group, the guidance is not a binding regulation under Law No. 6698 on the Protection of Personal Data, but represents the DPA's formal position and constitutes soft-law guidance currently in force. No prior public consultation preceded its publication.

The guidance identifies four primary compliance areas for organisations deploying agentic AI in Turkey. First, it classifies derived and inferred data, including outputs generated by correlating multiple datasets, as personal data subject to full KVKK compliance. Second, it requires that accountability among AI developers, providers, and deploying organisations be clearly allocated and documented, given that responsibility fragments across multi-agent architectures. Third, it mandates a risk-based governance approach incorporating privacy by design and privacy by default principles. Fourth, it treats data accuracy as a direct legal requirement under Article 4(1)(d) of the KVKK Law, meaning erroneous AI-generated outputs used in individual assessments constitute unlawful processing.

The KVKK signals it will assess agentic AI systems across their full data lifecycle, including model outputs and downstream decisions, not solely the handling of input data. No compliance deadline or transition period is imposed.

Sectors identified as facing direct exposure include financial services, insurance, and human resources, where staff deploy agentic AI tools in individual assessments. The guidance follows parallel KVKK announcements in February 2026 concerning Grok and Google Assistant, indicating sustained supervisory attention to AI-related data processing.

rows.headline: Lebanon's TRA Operates Under 2002 Telecom Law Framework
rows.title: Lebanon's TRA Operates Under 2002 Telecom Law Framework
rows.summary:
Lebanon's Telecommunications Regulatory Authority (TRA) has outlined the legal framework governing its mandate and regulatory activities, centred on Telecom Law 431 of 2002. According to the TRA's official website, Law 431 was issued to provide the framework for organising the telecommunications services sector and to set the rules for its transfer to the private sector.

The authority's operations are carried out in accordance with a series of official laws and decrees. Decree 14264, issued in 2005, specifies the administrative and financial regulations under which the TRA is managed. A further decree, Decree No. 1328, appointed the current TRA Board in September 2025, formally announcing board members, the commencement date of their work, and the length of their term of office.

The TRA's regulatory responsibilities span a broad range of areas including service provider licensing, spectrum management, interconnection, quality of service, and dispute resolution. The authority also oversees type approval regulation, numbering plans, and consumer protection measures, including children's protection and ICT road safety guidelines.

The TRA additionally conducts public consultations, publishes market reports and consumer reports, and maintains a quality of service measurement framework with key performance indicators and network outage reporting. The authority has also published guidance addressing Starlink satellite internet services in Lebanon.

The legal framework page serves as the TRA's central reference point for the legislative basis underpinning telecommunications governance in Lebanon, with full texts of the relevant laws and decrees available on the authority's website.

rows.headline: NIST Releases Critical Infrastructure Profile for AI Risk Framework
rows.title: NIST Releases Critical Infrastructure Profile for AI Risk Framework
rows.summary:
The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) has released a concept note for an AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) Profile focused on Trustworthy AI in Critical Infrastructure, published on 7 April 2026. The profile is designed to guide critical infrastructure operators towards specific risk management practices when engaging AI-enabled capabilities.

The development builds on the original NIST AI RMF 1.0, which was released on 26 January 2023 following a consensus-driven, open, and transparent process. That process included a Request for Information, multiple public comment drafts, workshops, and other public input opportunities. The framework is intended for voluntary use and aims to improve the incorporation of trustworthiness considerations into the design, development, use, and evaluation of AI products, services, and systems.

Led by the Information Technology Laboratory (ITL) AI Programme, the framework was developed in collaboration with both the private and public sectors. NIST also launched the Trustworthy and Responsible AI Resource Centre on 30 March 2023 to facilitate implementation and international alignment with the AI RMF.

In a further development, NIST released NIST-AI-600-1, the Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative Artificial Intelligence Profile, on 26 July 2024, to help organisations identify risks unique to generative AI systems. Companion resources including the AI RMF Playbook, AI RMF Roadmap, and AI RMF Crosswalk have also been published to support organisations in applying the framework across a range of contexts.

rows.headline: Central Bank of Mongolia Publishes Legal Framework Overview
rows.title: Central Bank of Mongolia Publishes Legal Framework Overview
rows.summary:
The Central Bank of Mongolia, known as Mongolbank, has published a comprehensive overview of the laws and legal frameworks governing its operations, available on its official website. The documentation covers a broad range of regulatory areas central to Mongolia's financial system.

According to Mongolbank's published materials, the legal framework encompasses legislation related to the central bank itself, banking supervision, monetary policy, currency regulation, and payment systems. The Central Bank (Mongolbank) Law, which currently governs the institution, was adopted in 1996 and remains in force.

The published framework also addresses anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing activities within the banking sector, alongside regulations governing bank licensing, structural reform, and prudential requirements. Mongolbank's documentation further outlines legal provisions relating to credit information, consumer debt-to-income ratios, and agreements concluded between Mongolbank and the Government of Mongolia.

On the monetary policy front, the framework details the structure of the Monetary Policy Committee, its operating procedures, and the instruments employed, including required reserves, open market operations, and central bank financing facilities. The National Payment System is also covered, with dedicated policies and a development strategy outlined separately.

The materials are organised into distinct legal categories, providing regulated entities and the public with structured access to all relevant legislation governing Mongolia's central banking and financial regulatory environment.

rows.headline: Morocco Introduces Digital X.0 Framework Law for AI Governance
rows.title: Morocco Introduces Digital X.0 Framework Law for AI Governance
rows.summary:
Morocco has unveiled a landmark framework law titled 'Digital X.0', designed to embed artificial intelligence, data governance, and digital identity into the country's national modernisation agenda. The draft legislation, currently under review by the General Secretariat of the Government, was presented by Amal El Fallah Seghrouchni, Minister Delegate for Digital Transition and Administrative Reform.

The law serves as the legal backbone of Morocco's Maroc Digital 2030 strategy, which positions AI as a central pillar of national modernisation and economic competitiveness. According to the framework, Digital X.0 is built around three core priorities: data governance, digital identity, and interoperability.

On data governance, the law aims to ensure secure, ethical, and transparent use of data in alignment with Law 09-08 on personal data protection. The digital identity component introduces a sector-specific identity system granting citizens control over their personal data, limiting institutional access to only what is necessary for each service. The interoperability pillar establishes a traceable consent framework requiring explicit user authorisation for all information exchanges between government agencies and private actors.

The bill also represents Morocco's first formal attempt to integrate AI into administrative and economic governance, establishing rules for transparency, accountability, and the ethical use of algorithms. The framework places significant emphasis on cybersecurity, seeking to build digital trust without imposing restrictive compliance burdens on innovation.

'This framework is about balancing innovation with trust,' said El Fallah Seghrouchni. 'We want to ensure that AI and digital technologies are harnessed responsibly to serve citizens, not control them.'

rows.headline: Philippines DTI Publishes National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0
rows.title: Philippines DTI Publishes National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0
rows.summary:
The Philippine Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) has published the National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 (NAISR 2.0), a comprehensive framework designed to position the Philippines as a competitive AI-driven economy in Southeast Asia.

According to the roadmap's official documentation, the strategy was officially launched in July 2024 and represents the culmination of a multi-year development process dating back to 2019. The framework evolved through three distinct phases: an initial scoping phase in 2019, the launch of the first National AI Strategy Roadmap (Version 1.0) in May 2021, and the updated 2.0 release in July 2024.

The 2.0 update responds directly to the global rise of Generative AI and growing international attention to ethical AI governance. The roadmap was developed through structured consultations involving government agencies, academic institutions, industry leaders, and civil society groups, reflecting what the DTI describes as a whole-of-nation approach.

The July 2024 launch received formal support from multiple Philippine government bodies, including the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA), the Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT), Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, and the Senate of the Philippines. The Asian Development Bank served as co-supporter, while technology firms including Google, AWS, Microsoft, IBM, Siemens, and Bosch Philippines provided endorsements.

A key initiative established under the roadmap is the Centre for AI Research (CAIR), which continues to serve as a reference point for the country's approach to balancing technological ambition with ethical responsibility and workforce protection.

The NAISR 2.0 has since been superseded by a new national AI strategy adopted under the Department of Science and Technology (DOST).

rows.headline: Da Afghanistan Bank Publishes Islamic Banking Regulatory Framework
rows.title: Da Afghanistan Bank Publishes Islamic Banking Regulatory Framework
rows.summary:
Da Afghanistan Bank (DAB) has published a comprehensive set of Islamic Banking Regulations and Guidelines on its official website, establishing a formal regulatory structure for Islamic financial institutions operating in Afghanistan.

The framework covers a broad range of regulatory areas, including asset classification and provisioning, capital adequacy, liquidity risk management, profit distribution, and enforcement action. Separate regulations govern the licensing of full-fledged Islamic banks as well as Islamic windows and units operating within conventional banking structures.

A National Shariah Governance Framework has been issued in two versions, one for full-fledged Islamic banks and another for Islamic window or unit operations, reflecting the different governance requirements applicable to each institutional type.

DAB has also released detailed product outlines covering key Islamic financing instruments, including Murabaha, Ijarah, Musharakah Mutanaqisah, Mudarabah, Salam, and Istisna. Shariah parameters for each product are published separately to guide institutions on compliance requirements.

Supporting guidelines address specific operational matters such as the conversion of conventional banks to Islamic banks, late payment charges for Islamic financing products, outsourcing of Islamic banking operations, and the appointment of external Shariah auditors.

The regulatory package is accompanied by a suite of examination manuals, including on-site and off-site examination manuals, Shariah review and compliance manuals, and a Shariah Board Members Assessment Manual, providing supervisory tools for DAB's oversight function.

All documents are available for download through the Islamic Banking Regulations section of the Da Afghanistan Bank website.

rows.headline: South Africa Publishes National AI Policy Framework for Public Comment
rows.title: South Africa Publishes National AI Policy Framework for Public Comment
rows.summary:
South Africa's Department of Communications and Digital Technologies (DCDT) has published the SA National AI Policy Framework, opening a public consultation period for stakeholders to submit comments and inputs on the document.

The framework, published on 25 October 2024, was made available via the DCDT's official website and invites written submissions from interested parties. The department set a deadline of 17h00 on Friday, 29 November 2024 for comments to be sent to designated departmental officials.

The DCDT advised that submissions need not follow a prescribed format, though contributors were encouraged to keep their inputs brief. The department also recommended that comments be sequenced in line with the framework document and its sub-topics where possible, to facilitate ease of analysis.

The publication of the framework represents a formal step by the South African government to establish a structured policy approach to artificial intelligence governance at the national level. The DCDT oversees a broad portfolio of digital and communications policy in South Africa, encompassing digital infrastructure, digital society and economy, and media and content programmes, among other areas.

The framework document, which is 3.16 MB in size, falls under the department's broader mandate to guide South Africa's digital transformation. The DCDT operates under the leadership of the Minister of Communications and Digital Technologies and oversees several portfolio organisations, including the South African Broadcasting Corporation, SENTECH, and the State Information Technology Agency.

rows.title: Source Document Unreadable: No Story Generated

PAI's editorial standards require news stories to be based on verifiable facts drawn directly from readable source material. The PDF titled 'Report on errors in Federal Civil Service Bill, 2080', hosted on the Nepal Parliament HR portal, could not be parsed into readable content. To produce an accurate news item about this report, a readable version of the document, such as a properly extracted text file or an accessible web page, would be required.

rows.headline: Andorra Reviews Urban Planning Law in Closed Legislative Session
rows.title: Andorra Reviews Urban Planning Law in Closed Legislative Session
rows.summary:
Andorra's Consell General has convened a legislative committee session to advance modifications to the General Law of Territorial Planning and Urbanism, known by its Catalan acronym LGOTU. The session, held on 8 April 2026 in the Sala de Comissions at the parliament building in Andorra la Vella, was closed to the public.

The committee, comprising nine members including Jordi Casadevall Touseil, Gemma Riba Casal, Pol Bartolomé Areny, Bernadeta Coma González, Meritxell López Guitart, Marc Magallon Font, Laia Moliné Cintas, Carine Montaner Raynaud, and Victor Pintos Morell, met to review and adapt the LGOTU's articles in line with conclusions reached by a dedicated Study Commission.

That Study Commission was established to analyse technical actions and measures in territorial planning and urbanism, with the aim of ensuring sustainable urban growth across the Principality. The legislative committee's agenda included approval of the minutes from session 01/26, followed by discussion of how the law's existing articles should be updated to reflect the Study Commission's findings.

The modification process forms part of Andorra's broader effort to bring its legal framework for land use and urban development into alignment with sustainability objectives identified through parliamentary study. The committee also addressed other matters of interest during the closed session.

Further parliamentary activity is scheduled in the coming weeks, with a plenary session of the Consell General set for 30 April 2026, followed by a meeting of the Legislative Commission for Education, Research, Culture, Youth and Sport on 5 May 2026.

rows.headline: Delaware General Assembly Publishes Legislative Agenda Reports Online
rows.title: Delaware General Assembly Publishes Legislative Agenda Reports Online
rows.summary:
The Delaware General Assembly has made its agenda and activity reports publicly available through its official legislative website, providing citizens and stakeholders with direct access to the state's legislative proceedings and scheduling information.

The online portal, hosted at legis.delaware.gov, offers a centralised resource covering a broad range of legislative materials. These include bills and resolutions, budget and appropriations documents, committee meeting schedules, and daily activity reports from both the Senate and House chambers.

The resource covers the full structure of Delaware's legislature, listing Senate and House committees alongside joint committees, task forces, and commissions. Members of the public can access voting records, meeting minutes, committee agendas, and archived recordings from the Senate Chamber, House Chamber, and Joint Finance Hearing Room via linked YouTube channels.

Additional resources listed on the portal include the Delaware Code, the Delaware Constitution, the Administrative Code, and the Landlord Tenant Code. The Division of Legislative Services and the Office of the Controller General are also represented, with the latter responsible for budget oversight, appropriations, and grant-in-aid processes.

The General Assembly's physical home, Legislative Hall, is located at 411 Legislative Avenue in Dover, Delaware. The portal notes accessibility provisions and provides contact information for the public, including details for Freedom of Information Act requests and media credentialling.

The publication of agenda reports and activity logs reflects the legislature's stated commitment to public participation in the legislative process, with resources available to assist citizens in navigating bill progression and committee activity.
